Firelance.text: Firelance: The signature weapon of Goblinate firelancers. Pike-length wooden haft topped with a brass dragon head that vomits dragonfire when triggered. Each muzzle is hand-crafted and unique—different expressions, different scale patterns, individual faces. Goblins name their lances, maintain them obsessively, and treasure them above all possessions. A firelancer's weapon defines who they are. Effective range is short, maybe twenty feet; beyond that, dragonfire disperses. Brass reservoirs at the base of the head hold fuel. The weapons are dangerous to operate—Loss of pressure sprays fire backward, cracked reservoirs ignite, misfires burn wielders alive. Firelancers accept the risk. Heavy fire-lances exist as siege weapons, crew-served on wheeled carriages, but the infantry lance is the symbol of the Goblinate at war.

Lords and Ladies of Vice and Virtue.text: Lords and Ladies of Vice and Virtue: In all the worlds, virtue arises unbidden. It is not created. It springs into being when mortals first appear - thinking beings capable of suffering and choice. Justice, Mercy, Valor, Hope. These principles exist in every universe. But virtue requires a vessel - a Champion. A mortal who embodies it so completely that they transcend mortality itself. The theologians call this an Act Transcendent - a deed of such profound righteousness that it transforms the doer into something greater. A Champion becomes a Lord or Lady of Virtue. Perhaps the same Lord, wearing different faces across all the worlds. Perhaps many. The priests argue. The Lords and Ladies are not truly immortal. They can Fall - manifest in the mortal world, walk among us, and be slain. This is the price of presence. In Larion, the Lady of Mercy Fell once, sacrificing herself to shield her followers from a great evil. But virtue cannot be destroyed while mortals live to embody it. She Rose again, renewed by her own sacrifice. Vice arises the same way. Where virtue exists, so must its shadow. The Lords and Ladies of Vice were once mortal too. Through an Act Notorious - a deed of such profound evil that it damns the doer forever - they become vessels for hatred, cruelty, despair. When Vice Falls, whatever destroyed it commits an Act Notorious by the very act of destruction. The slayer becomes the slain.

The Lady of Mercy.text: The Lady of Mercy (Goddess; The Risen, The Selfless): She is the virtue of compassion made divine. Her followers tend the sick, shelter the homeless, and comfort the dying. Her temples are hospitals and sanctuaries. No one is turned away.

The Lord of Justice.text: The Lord of Justice (God; The Balanced Scale): He is the virtue of fair judgment. Truth matters to him. Oaths matter. Consequences matter. His temples serve as courts throughout Larion. His priests hear disputes, weigh evidence, render verdicts. To lie in his temple is to damn yourself.

The Lord of Valor.text: The Lord of Valor (God; The Shieldbearer, The Unflinching). He is the virtue of courage. Not recklessness - courage. The will to face evil when you are afraid. To stand when others flee. To put yourself between darkness and those who cannot fight it. His temples are training halls. His priests teach combat, but also teach when to fight and when to withdraw.

The Lady of Hope.text: The Lady of Hope (Goddess; The Dawnbringer, The Undimmed). She is the virtue of perseverance. The light that survives the longest night. Her domain is not naive optimism but stubborn refusal to surrender to despair. When all seems lost, she is what remains. Her temples are often small - a single candle burning in a window, a shrine at a crossroads.

The Lord of Order.text: The Lord of Order (God; The Architect, The Foundation). He is the virtue of civilization. Laws, contracts, institutions, the agreements that let strangers live together without bloodshed.

The Lady of the Hearth.text: The Lady of the Hearth (Goddess; The Welcomer, The Keeper of Doors). She is the virtue of home. Not a building but the feeling - belonging, warmth, the place where you are known and wanted. Her domain is family, community, hospitality, the bonds between people who share roof and bread. Her priests bless marriages, name children, consecrate new homes. Her temples are gathering halls, often with a great hearth at the center. Travelers are always welcome. A meal is always offered.

The Lady of Wisdom.text: The Lady of Wisdom (Goddess; The Clear-Eyed, The Patient). She is the virtue of understanding, of the judgment to use knowledge well. Her domain is learning, teaching, counsel, and the long view beyond immediate passions. Her priests maintain schools and offer counsel to any who ask. Her temples are libraries and academies.

The Lady of Blood.text: The Lady of Blood (Archfiend; the First Vampire). A Lady of Vice, one of the archfiends bound by the Pact and unable to manifest directly in Larion. She is the progenitor of all vampires. In the time of the Elven Schism, when sun elves hunted moon elves toward extinction, a great moon elf leader faced a choice: sacrifice herself for her people, or sacrifice her people for herself. She chose herself. In a ritual of profound evil, she consumed her own followers to fuel her transformation into something beyond mortal. She became the Lady of Blood, and the 'gift' she gave her surviving followers was vampirism: her curse, shared. Her blessing is required to create new vampire lords. Without it, vampires can only sire lesser thralls. The prince-magisters of the Moon Kingdoms rule in her shadow, paying the blood price their ancestor established. She is elsewhere now, constrained by the Pact, but her children remember.

The Lord of the Open Hand.text: The Lord of the Open Hand (God; the Companion, the Wayfarer). He is the virtue of fellowship — bonds freely chosen between equals. Not obligation, not fealty, not blood — choice. His domain is friendship, mutual aid, and the hand extended without expectation. His priests bless adventuring parties and witness oaths of companionship. His shrines are waystations, campfires, and gathering places where no one claims the high seat. Among the wood elves of the Everwood, he is remembered as He Who Walks Beside — in Elvish, Talvaren — a legendary figure who guided those who refused to fight in the Kindred War to safety, then gave his life defending them. His final words, 'May you walk in beauty,' are still spoken by wood elves to mark births, arrivals, and deaths. Whether Talvaren was the Lord of the Open Hand in mortal form, or a mortal who became the Lord through his sacrifice, is a question his priests answer differently depending on where you ask.

The Pact of All Planes.text: The Pact of All Planes: An ancient binding that keeps extraplanar entities from freely entering Larion. Created long ago by Suleyvan the Sealer—sometimes called the First Warlock—the Pact is an agreement between mortals and neutral extraplanar powers. These powers agreed to hold the boundaries against other more hostile exemplars of their kind. In exchange, they gained the right to form pacts with willing mortals. Every warlock bargain since is an echo of that original agreement, a thread in the binding that maintains the barrier. The Pact holds, mostly. Breaches still occur through summoning, thin places, and loopholes.

The Black Gate.text: A mountain fortress in the Moon Kingdoms where new vampire lords are created. The Black Gate is a portal to the realm of the Lady of Blood; aspirants must enter, defeat a Fiend of the Blood, and drink its essence to receive the vampire's gift.

Soul-Stone.text:
Soul Stones:

Soul-stones are gems with a soul trapped inside, old work of the Princes of Ur from before the Floods. A soul-stone can slow aging and lengthen life, cure sickness, and feed magic with the strength of the soul caught within it — so the rich, the dying, and the powerful prize them, and wizards covet them. But there is a person in every stone, bound and unable to pass on, and most folk find the whole business unholy. They are bought and sold quietly. Their trade is lawful in some lands, a hanging crime in others, and watched everywhere. Most that surface now are dug out of the buried cities of the Underworld, where the old empire stored them.

Doom of Orichala.text: The Doom of Orichala: The Republic of Orichala was the proudest of the Spice Isles Free States. They claimed descent from the Gold Empire itself and swore to restore its glory, mounting expeditions to sunken ruins, recovered artifacts and mastered waterbreathing magics. Their vaults held treasures no other city could match. They said they were close to finding the lost Gold Empire capital itself. Then came the Doom. In a single night, the island tilted and sank, the sea rising and swallowing all the island but one small promontory, which still juts above the waves. No warning, no storm, no explanation. No bodies. No survivors. Just empty, drowned streets and the things that moved into them after. The ruins are still there, half-exposed at low tide, infested with drowners and worse.

Pelagic Alliance.text: The Pelagic Alliance: A confederation of seafolk kingdoms that rules the waters beneath the Spice Isles. Sea-elves lead its councils. Mersene serve as traders and go-betweens, equally at home above and below the waves. Frogkin are craftsmen and laborers, working coral and pearl and pressure-forged metals. Selachii—shark-blooded and sharp-toothed—enforce the Alliance's laws and guard its borders. The Alliance has fought many wars against the surface folk of the Free States. The current truce has held longer than most, maintained through Coral Cavern and careful diplomacy, but old grievances run deep on both sides.

The Elven Schism.text: The Elven Schism: In the First Days, all elves were one people. Then came the Long Dark—a time of endless night and winter. The elves split over how to survive it. Some embraced the darkness: these became moon elves. Others rebelled against it: these became sun elves. Still others refused to take sides: these became the wood elves. When the darkness finally lifted, war followed. The elven homeland was lost to all of them—some say destroyed in the war, others cursed and blighted by fratricide. Tthe sun elves settled in the deserts of the Bright Kingdoms, the moon elves in the shadowed mountains to the east, and wood elves in the Everwood. The enmity between sun and moon has never healed. The wood elves mourn what was lost and keep no side.

Mirage.text: Mirage: The hidden desert city of the sun elves. Mirage appears in the deep desert of the Bright Kingdoms where it wills—a shimmering vision of white spires and golden domes.  Mirage is hidden by light itself, so bright with sun magic that outsiders perceive only heat-shimmer where a city stands. Those the sun elves wish to find are found. Those they do not wish to see walk past without knowing what they missed.

Sun Elves.text: Sun elves: Tall and lean with bronze or copper skin. Hair ranges from white-gold to deep copper to flame-red. Their eyes are pale—gold, amber, or white. Most practice some form of sun magic. They are a proud, martial people concentrated in the hidden city of Mirage and the Bright Kingdoms, organized into knight-orders and ruled by temples of the sun. They consider themselves righteous guardians against darkness and corruption and the Moon Kingdoms a corruption that should be scoured from the world. Sun elf culture emphasizes purity, discipline, and the rejection of darkness in all forms.

The Vale Sable.text: The Vale Sable: A quarantined valley in the Moon Kingdoms, sealed by collapsed stone and guarded by Silver Company mercenaries. Once the domain of House Sable, an ancient bloodline known for blood magic, the vale fell to feral vampires generations ago. The great houses have not moved to cleanse it, though they could. Rumors speak of the Sable library still intact within Keep Sable—the greatest repository of blood magic research in Larion. Other rumors mention someone important trapped inside when the valley fell. The Silver Company maintains a cordon at the sealed pass and kills anyone who emerges without permission.

Temple of the Black Flame.text: Temple of the Black Flame: A fortress-temple high in the Moon Kingdoms where demonborn priests guard against infernal incursion. The temple honors the Bearer of the Black Flame, the hero who led the demonborn to sanctuary. The flame-keepers study infernal lore, maintain protective wards, and help those afflicted by curses, possessions, or demonic contracts. Demonborn from across Larion make pilgrimages here to master their heritage.

The War in Hell.text: The War in Hell: An eternal conflict raging in the infernal realms, fought between demonic factions over matters mortals struggle to comprehend. The war has no beginning anyone remembers and no end anyone predicts. Occasionally it spills into Larion. The demonborn are descendants of refugees who fled this conflict generations ago.

Gods-tomb.text: Gods-tomb (The Missing Mountain; The Tomb of the First One): Gods-tomb is an ancient burial ground in the Highlands where the titans of a former age were laid to rest beneath open sky. The remains there dwarf any living giant — skulls the size of houses, femurs like fallen pillars, ribcages arching over the ground like the frames of great halls. Giant clans honored the site as sacred ground. The greatest of the dead was called the First One: the firstborn of all giantkind, ancestor of every bloodline. Its skeleton was so vast that it had become part of the mountain — its spine a ridge, its ribcage a valley, its skull a hill. Now, it is gone. A body-shaped rift gapes where the First One had rested. The disappearance has thrown giantkind into turmoil and strained their already fragile peace with the smallfolk. The tunnels exposed beneath where the First One lay have drawn scavengers, mercenaries, and tomb-raiders in numbers — easy prey for giant war parties, whose killing only fuels their rage.

Orc Diaspora.text: Orc diaspora: The orc clans once held the grasslands and hill-country now claimed by the Greater Goblinate. When the Dragon Queen unified the goblin tribes, her armies drove the clans from their ancestral territory. The displaced clans scattered across Larion. Orc culture turns on the keeping of oaths — spoken vows witnessed by a clan elder and affirmed before the gods. The oath-witness names their lineage, declares what they have heard, and the oath is sealed. It is the most sacred act in orc life. Everything else is noise, argument, and cheerful insult. Orcs show affection through mockery and find the politeness of other cultures baffling and dishonest. They eat communally, sharing platters with their hands, and consider eating alone bizarre. They are known for blunt honesty, fierce loyalty, and long memories. Most diaspora orc communities gather around an elder who serves as oath-witness — the living memory of every promise her people have made.

The Burning of the Kings.text: The Burning of the Kings (Kingstor, Wild Grass Sea): The centerpiece of every Great Moot of the beastfolk at Kingstor. In the grass sea, a 'king' is a colossal effigy built to burn — the word stripped of authority, stuffed with straw, and set on fire. Anyone can build a king. Wolfkin packs build kings of bandit chiefs. Boarfolk communes build kings of Syndicate tax assessors. Temporary alliances bend together to build enormous kings representing outside powers who've threatened beastfolk autonomy — these draw the biggest crowds. Kings can be political, personal, or just funny. The only rule is it has to burn well. The Burning itself is the wildest night in Larion — drums, bonfires, dancing, drinking, feats of strength, old rivals clasping arms while enormous effigies collapse in showers of sparks. Non-beastfolk at Kingstor during the Burning are welcome to join. They are also welcome to notice that kings from their home regions burn just as brightly as any other.

The Thieves' Guild.text: The Thieves' Guild - The Trade, Hands, The Table: The Thieves' Guild is a den of vipers that don't bite each other — much. An informal alliance of criminal bands called Hands, found wherever there's wealth and secrets worth taking. The Guild is 'governed' by the Table — a secret council of the most powerful, influential, and ruthless leaders in the trade. Most Hands never earn a seat at the Table. Most cower like dogs at the foot of it. The Guild takes different forms in different places, but it always makes its peace with the powers that be. Don't steal from the ruling clan. Get secrets for them. Spy on their enemies. Solve the problems the law can't solve and keep the underworld quiet enough that decent folk can sleep. In return, the authorities look the other way — though they always hold out their hands for a cut. Because crime is inevitable, and crime without anyone minding the shop is worse for everyone. Some Hands are professionals, proud of their craft. Some are thugs. Most protect their own. The Guild protects Hands that serve the Table, punishes those who break its rules or upset its betters, and settles grudges before they become wars. Outsiders call us thieves. We call ourselves the Trade.

The Table.text: The Table: The Low Table is one name for the loose alliance of criminal bands that 'runs' the Thieves' Guild - the closest thing that den of vipers has to a parliament. It has as many names as it has enemies. The Knives in the Dark. The Court of Bastards. The Gutter Council. The Poor Man's Justice. Call it what you like — if you're in the Trade, it's the only court that matters. Representatives from the strongest criminal crews and bands, known as Hands, meet at the Table to divide territory, negotiate jobs, air grudges, and settle disputes before they spill blood in the streets. The Table doesn't command — it mediates, threatens, and occasionally makes bloody examples. A Hand that ignores the Table's rulings gets declared outlaw, which means every other Hand in the city can take what's theirs with the Table's blessing. That usually sorts things out fast. The Table is chaired by whoever is strong enough that nobody at it dares say otherwise. No election, no appointment — you sit at the head when the room lets you. That changes with fortune, and fortune changes often. Meetings happen where they happen, and never the same place twice running. If you're summoned to the Table, you go. You bring no weapons but your wits, and you better be prepared to pick sides; there is no neutrality in a den of vipers.

Hands.text: A Hand is an underworld crew of thieves. Your people. The ones who watch your back when the job goes wrong and split honest when it goes right. Every Hand has a leader, a territory, a way of doing things, and a name — and the name had better mean something, or you're nobody. Some Hands are old blood, three generations deep, territory so well-carved nobody remembers the last fool who tried to take it. Some are fresh — four or five clever nobodies who banded together and talked or fought their way into the Table's good graces. A Hand in good standing pays its cut to the Table and answers when called. Beyond that, your business is your business. Some Hands work clean — pride in the craft, no unnecessary blood, a code they keep because they want to, not because anyone makes them. Some are dogs. Most are somewhere between. Burglary, smuggling, forgery, the quiet knife, information, or whatever the night offers — every Hand finds its niche or it finds a grave. You don't join the Thieves' Guild. You join a Hand, or you make one. Your Hand is your crew, your colours, your family, your name in the streets. When someone asks who you run with, that answer is the only thing standing between you and whatever's coming.

The Laughing God.text: The Laughing God (Divinity) - Jack o' Cups, Sweet-Tongue Jack, Saint of Sin: Every tavern in Larion knows the story, and none of them tell it the same way. A mortal man — a beggar, a drunk, a fool, depending on who's talking — made a wager with the gods. What the wager was depends on the version. Some say he bet he could steal the sun and replace it before dawn. Some say he challenged Death to a drinking contest and won because he was too drunk to die. Some say he simply talked his way into the divine court, sat down uninvited, and refused to leave until they made him one of them. The details change. The ending never does: he won, and the gods — the Lords and Ladies of Virtue and all the rest of them — honored the bet because a wager is a wager, even among the divine. And Jack laughed, and hasn't stopped laughing since. He is the god of luck, mischief, drink, dares, sudden reversals of fortune and fools. Holy to thieves, beggars, wastrels, gamblers, infatuated lovers, and anyone about to do something they probably shouldn't. He has no priests, but storytellers, drunkards, jesters, pranksters and troublemakers pray to him in their own way. He has no temples but tavern corners and anywhere someone raises a glass before doing something stupid and brave. His holy days are the Feast of Fools — three days when the social order inverts, rules dissolve, and the whole world remembers that a beggar once became a god, so anything is possible. Pray to Jack and he might answer, but never the way you wanted. He helps sideways. He gives you luck, but luck has teeth. His sacred teaching, if you can call it that, fits on the back of a beer mat: the world is mad, you're going to die, so laugh, drink, dare, and see what happens. His symbol is the overflowing cup. His colors are gold and motley.

Drinking Games of Larion.text: Drinking games are common in taverns across Larion. The three most popular: The Slap — Two players stand on opposite sides of a table. In each round, both players drink. Then, one player slaps the other, open-handed, on the cheek; no fists, scratching, or targeting of eyes or nose. The player receiving the slap holds one hand on the table and must not drop their hand from its surface. Flinch, lift your hand, or pull it away and you lose. Then the other player gets their turn to slap their opponent, and the game contains with next round of drinking and slapping. The Choke — players take turns drinking (typically ale, and in any case something that takes a bit of time to gulp down) while their opponent insults them. The drinker must drain their cup without choking, spitting, or snorting. The insulter's job is to time their worst material for mid-swallow and force their opponent to sputter, spit, or choke in anger or laughter. Rounds alternate. Rust — the tavern wrecker. Two cups per player, one water, one drink. Players drink with their backs turned and may choose either cup. Their opponent calls 'Rust!' to accuse them of drinking water — if right, the coward drinks three penalties. If wrong, the caller drinks three. After each round, the winner names a physical challenge — hop on one foot, balance something on your nose, walk along a bench, catch a thrown object. Both players attempt it. Whoever fails loses the game.

Magic in Larion.text:
Magic in Larion: Though definitions of magic vary by tradition, the most common is that magic is the living power of the world, present in all things. Spellcasting is drawing that power through one's body and will to reshape the world.

Casters are limited in their capacity — what mages call their vessel. A novice vessel is small and fragile. It can hold enough power for simple things: lighting a flame, mending a small wound, moving a cup without touching it. A master's vessel is vast and resilient, able to hold tremendous power and direct it with fine control.

Three things determine how hard a spell is and thus how large a vessel it requires. Scale — how much you are asking reality to change. Lighting a candle is barely a nudge. Calling down a firestorm is trying to move the world a long way. Complexity — how much precision the effect demands. A raw blast of fire is simple. Shaping fire into a barrier that lets allies pass through but burns enemies requires deep understanding and fine control. Opposition — how hard the target or the world pushes back. Fire magic comes easily in dry air and struggles in a downpour. Necromancy thrives in places of death and falters on consecrated ground. A strong-willed enemy resists mental magic better than a weak-minded one.

When a mage channels too much, their vessel empties. They feel it as exhaustion — heavy limbs, clouded thoughts, a hollow ache behind the eyes. Recovery comes through rest, meditation, or sources of concentrated power like potions.

Different traditions channel in different ways, but these are traditions, not separate powers — a fire mage and a divine caster are both drawing from the same current, just through different training and understanding.

Magic is respected in Larion, sometimes feared, but never truly rare. Most towns have at least a hedge mage or healer. Cities have guilds, academies, professional enchanters. Alchemical lamps light wealthy streets. Magical healing saves lives that would otherwise be lost. But magic cannot solve everything — it is limited by the caster's vessel, by the difficulty of the effect, and by the simple fact that channelling power through a mortal body is hard, tiring work. The greatest mages in the world are extraordinary precisely because what they do is so difficult.

The Primordial Plane of Ur.text: The Primordial Plane of Ur (Cosmology): Regarded as myth or legend by many, the Primordial Plane of Ur is said to be a progenitor realm of raw magic and chaos, older than mortal civilization and untouched by its concepts. Little is known about Ur, but all sources agree that there is no law there, no morality, no pity. Power is the only currency. The High Princes of Ur are beings that most prefer not to name out of superstition — demons or gods of such might that their whims reshape the world around them — a prince's cruelty becomes the landscape's cruelty, a prince's hunger makes everything prey. Nothing built in Ur endures, because the next prince's passing mood may unmake it. The demonborn who now live in Larion trace their origins to this place, though most have never seen it and know it only through legend. Orthodox opinion holds that Pact of Suleyman the Sealer bars the High Princes from manifesting in Larion, just as it bars the gods and other great powers of the outer planes.

The Excession of Ur.text: The Excession of Ur (Cosmology): Sometimes also called the Translation of Ur, the Excession of Ur is the mythic event by which the jungle now known as Ntar Vanna came to exist in Larion. The demonborn tell that a High Prince of Ur called Aldorath turned against his own kind, slaying his brother-prince to break a dominion free from the Primordial Plane and translate it bodily into the mortal world. The translated territory became the Ntar Vanna: jungle of staggering scale grown from Ur-soil, saturated with primordial magic. Whether Aldorath acted from compassion, ambition, or some motive beyond mortal understanding depends on who tells the story. The Excession is the founding event of demonborn civilization in Larion, and to this day no scholar has offered a convincing alternative explanation for the jungle's alien soil, its unnatural vitality, or the ruins of a non-human empire buried in its depths.

Aldorath the Excessor.text: High Prince of Ur Aldorath the Excessor (Cosmology): Also known as Aldorath the Apostate or Aldorath the Kinslayer. The legend and cult of this obscure, turbulent and divisive figure are little-known outside of the southern jungles of Ntar Vanna, though demonborn throughout Larion can invariably give the essential details of his life and deeds. Aldorath is said to have been a High Prince of Ur who led the Excession, relocating his principality out of the Primordial Plane of Ur and into Larion. All sources agree that he slew his own brother to accomplish it — whether as sacrifice, in battle, or for reasons unknown. The demonborn revere him as their liberator, the prince who freed them from bondage to the chaotic caprices of the High Princes and delivered them to Larion, there to decide their own destiny. Old inscriptions in the ruined cities of Ntar Vanna name him as sovereign, lawgiver, and builder. What became of him is a subject of much debate and even sectarian strife. Did whatever catastrophe claimed the demonborn cities claim him as well? Did he bring ruin upon his own works, as he once destroyed his brother? Is he sleeping in the deep jungle, diminished but alive? Every generation of demonborn must seek their own answers.

Demonborn.text: The demonborn are a people of otherworldly heritage whose distant ancestors are commonly believed to have hailed from the Primordial Plane of Ur. They are marked by horns, tails, unusual coloring, and eyes that glow faintly in darkness — appearances that unsettle those unfamiliar with them. The greatest concentration of demonborn in Larion live in the vast southern jungles of Ntar Vanna, though communities exist in the Spice Isles, the Bright Kingdoms, and wherever trade and opportunity draw them. Demonborn temperament runs hot — passionate, direct, quick to anger, and fiercely loyal. Their culture values hospitality, community, and deliberate self-mastery. This last is not abstract virtue. The demonborn regard themselves as self-imposed exiles from Ur, a place without restraint, and the discipline required to live among mortal peoples is something each generation must learn anew.

The Children of Ur.text: Children of Ur (cult): The Children of Ur are a proscribed demonborn cult, outlawed in the jungles of Ntar Vanna and hunted wherever they surface in Larion. They hold that the Excession of Ur — the cosmological act that brought the demonborn from the Primordial Plane into Larion — was not a self-imposed exile but the beachhead of a conquest. The Children teach that their ancient leader, Aldorath the Excessor, was a conqueror, not a refugee, and that his legacy has been deliberately corrupted to keep the demonborn docile. They seek to complete the Translation of Ur that he began but was unable to conclude: to merge the Primordial Plane fully into Larion, flooding the mortal world with Ur's raw power and restoring the demonborn to their primordial nature. Virtue, restraint, and compassion are chains forged to prevent this ascension. To mainstream demonborn, the Children are dangerous apostates who threaten everything the Excession was meant to achieve: freedom from the High Princes, the opportunity for each to decide his own destiny, and a new beginning in a new world. To outsiders, they confirm every suspicion about what the demonborn truly are. Children of Ur cells operate in secret wherever demonborn communities exist, recruiting those who feel the pull of their heritage most keenly.

Chameleon-kin.text: Chameleon-kin (species): The chameleon-kin, who call themselves the Mounari, are a reclusive amphibious people native to the jungles of Ntar Vanna. They are slight of build, with independently moving eyes, prehensile tails, and skin that shifts color and pattern instinctively to match their surroundings — a natural camouflage so effective that a still Mounari is nearly invisible. They are extraordinary climbers, capable of scaling any surface and moving through canopy, cliff, and water with equal ease. The Mounari are a patient, observant people who value stillness, discipline, and the art of passing unseen. Their masters teach techniques of movement, breath, and concealment that other species can learn, though never to the same degree — Mounari schools in the deep jungle draw students from across Larion seeking training in stealth and infiltration. Outside Ntar Vanna, chameleon-kin are rare and often regarded with unease. A people who can disappear at will are difficult to trust, and the Mounari seem largely unbothered by this.

Monkeyfolk.text: The monkeyfolk of the southern jungles of Ntar Vanna call themselves the High Vanarin, and they will correct you if you forget. They are a quick, agile, canopy-dwelling people — acrobatic, gregarious, and possessed of an unshakeable conviction in their own importance. The High Vanarin are the self-appointed lords of the upper canopy, and anything that enters their domain is considered a gift freely given. This includes cargo, personal belongings, and occasionally people. Despite this, the Vanarin are not petty thieves — they are, by their own account, heroes, sages, and protectors of the jungle. The maddening thing is that this is often true. Vanarin warriors are fearless and fast, their elders wise in jungle lore. Their loyalty, once earned, is ferocious. Playfully pompous, impossibly vain, generous to a fault with things they consider theirs — which is everything — and excellent company provided you don't leave anything valuable unattended.

Thrallmages of Falmastra.text:
Thrallmages of Falmastra (Faction):

Mind-controlling psionicists operating from an island city-state in the Spice Isles. Can read thoughts, speak mind-to-mind, and puppet others like living dolls. To fully bind a thrall, they need a piece of the target — a hair, a drop of blood, a worn possession — and the target's true name. Without both, no binding holds. Thrallmages hire out as spies and killers for enormous fees. Their retribution doctrine, the Hundredfold, promises a hundred deaths for any one of their number killed. Settlements near Falmastra pay tribute and are left alone. The thrallmages are few — perhaps a few dozen — but one thrallmage with the right thralls in the right places can topple a merchant house or unseat a prince. They are feared the way plagues are feared: an invisible thing that can take hold of anyone.

The Exclave.text:
The Exclave (Tevantia Institutin):

The ritual of succession of the office of the Dukedom in the great Spice Isles city-state of Tevantia. When a Duke dies, the Electors — the city's great merchants, guild leaders, and notables — seal themselves on the Isle of Union to choose his successor by majority vote. No one leaves until a Duke is chosen. Leaving before the Election is complete is treason. Each Elector may bring a Second — a trusted agent who acts on their behalf: carrying messages, brokering alliances, gathering intelligence, fighting duels, and doing what an Elector cannot be seen doing. The dead Duke's body is carried across the strait on a ceremonial barge lit by a thousand alchemical lanterns. He lies in state in the Chamber of Light at the base of the Burning Tower, attended by those lanterns until a successor is chosen. The tower's beacon burns for the duration. Tevantia keeps a twilight vigil — no lights after sunset, no music, no revelry — until the beacon is extinguished and the new Duke returns the light to the city on the same barge. The Exclave runs on an unspoken paradox every Tevantine knows: only the Duke can investigate crimes against the person of a current or former Duke; as the Tevantine proverb holds, 'The Duke's justice lives and dies with the Duke.' Some Exclaves have been short and civil. Others have not.

The Burning of Tevantia.text:
The Burning of Tevantia (Historical Event):

three centuries ago, Duke Aldano III of Tevantia died without a clear successor. Three great houses claimed the seat. Their private armies clashed in the streets and the city burned. The Holy Solarian Exarcate — the provincial arm of the Solarian Dominion, which has long sought to bring the Free States under imperial rule — had fomented the disorder, and a Solarian legion sailed on Tevantia while the city tore itself apart. Facing annihilation, the three pretenders met on the Isle of Union, a religious sanctuary in the strait. There they set aside their claims and elected a Duke to lead the city's defense. Tevantia survived. The Black Palace — the ruined seat of the house that started the fighting — still stands in the city, never rebuilt, never torn down.

The Dukedom of Tevantia.text: The Duke of Tevantia is the First Peer — not a king, but the foremost among the great houses. Elected for life by the Electors, the Duke mediates between the feuding merchant families, upholds the Compact between the Free States, and commands Tevantia's defense in times of crisis. A Duke may withdraw from the seat voluntarily, though few have. The role carries enormous prestige and influence but limited direct power — the great houses would never suffer a monarch, and a Duke who overreaches finds his allies evaporating and his Electors whispering about precedent. The wisest Dukes govern by consensus, play the houses against each other just enough to keep the peace, and save their authority for when it matters.

The Solarian Dominion.text:
The Solarian Dominion (faction):

a vast empire beyond the seas, ruled by a god-emperor whose authority is absolute and — to the Solarians — divinely ordained. The Dominion believes that the natural state of civilization is unity under a single sovereign, and that the patchwork of kingdoms, republics, and free cities that make up Larion is a symptom of disorder waiting to be cured. All peoples are welcome as citizens, provided they bend the knee. Those who do find the Dominion's roads well-built, its legions disciplined, its justice consistent, and its taxes non-negotiable. Those who refuse find the legions disciplined in other ways. The Dominion does not conquer in haste. It builds, fortifies, and waits. Its presence in Larion — the Holy Solarian Exarcate in Great Lagoon — has held the same borders for over a century.

The Holy Solarian Exarcate.text:
The Holy Solarian Exarcate (faction):

the Solarian Dominion's provincial territory in Larion, encompassing the Great Lagoon and its surrounding coast. Governed by the Most Exalted Exarch, who answers only to the god-emperor and commands a standing legion, a fleet, and the largest fortified harbor in eastern Larion. The Exarcate is a marvel of engineering — causeways cross the lagoon, sea-walls hold back the tides, and fortified towns rise from land that was open swamp a century ago. Solarian citizens live under Dominion law: orderly, regimented, and notably safe compared to the rest of Larion. The native peoples of the lagoon — lizardfolk, frogkin, and human marsh communities — exist in various states of accommodation, from full citizenship to uneasy coexistence to quiet resistance in the deep swamps the Dominion has not yet drained.

aspected crafting materials.text:
Aspected Materials:

Some rare crafting materials carry inherent damage affinities. Silver, moonstone, and sun-gold are radiant-aspected. Stormsteel and thunderglass are shock-aspected. Emberglass and red jade are fire-aspected. Frostjade and rimebone are frost-aspected. Obsidian, voidglass, and black iron are shadow-aspected. Witchwood and aether crystal are arcane-aspected. Dream coral and thoughtstone are psychic-aspected. Venomwood and green glass are poison-aspected. Caustic bronze and alchemist glass are acid-aspected.

Silver.text: Silver: A radiant-aspected metal associated with purity, oaths, moonlight, and sanctified craft. Silver weapons harm shadow-bound creatures, undead, fiends, and lycanthropes because the metal carries a clean light through the wound.

Moonstone.text: Moonstone: A pale radiant-aspected gemstone used in temple blades, ritual jewelry, and warding charms. Moonstone shines strongest against undead, demons, curses, and creatures sustained by shadow.

Sun-Gold.text: Sun-gold: A rare radiant-aspected gold that holds warmth and light even in darkness. It is prized for holy relics, royal regalia, and weapons meant to burn away undeath or corruption.

Stormsteel.text: Stormsteel: A shock-aspected metal that hums faintly before storms and carries lightning through a strike. Weapons made from stormsteel deal shock damage regardless of blade shape.

Thunderglass.text: Thunderglass: A shock-aspected glassy mineral formed where lightning repeatedly strikes sand, towers, or mountain glass. It stores violent charge and releases it through wands, spearheads, and ritual foci.

Emberglass.text: Emberglass: A fire-aspected volcanic glass that glows like banked coals under pressure. Emberglass weapons and foci burn through flesh, armor gaps, and cold-natured creatures.

Red Jade.text: Red jade: A fire-aspected stone used in dueling blades, furnace shrines, and noble signets. It is less explosive than emberglass but holds a steady inner heat that turns strikes into fire damage.

Frostjade.text: Frostjade: A frost-aspected pale green stone cold enough to mist in warm air. Frostjade weapons bite with supernatural cold and are prized against fire creatures, fever spirits, and living magma.

Rimebone.text: Rimebone: A frost-aspected bone taken from ancient beasts preserved in glacier tombs or winter barrows. It drinks heat from wounds and makes even blunt weapons strike with killing cold.

Obsidian.text: Obsidian: A shadow-aspected volcanic glass used for knives, mirrors, and sacrificial tools. It cuts cleanly but leaves darkness in the wound, making it dangerous against radiant or living-light creatures.

Voidglass.text: Voidglass: A shadow-aspected black glass that reflects stars even underground. It is used in assassin blades, warlock foci, and relics meant to wound spirits, light-bearers, and the soul.

Black Iron.text: Black iron: A shadow-aspected iron alloy smelted with grave ash, night salts, or underworld coal. It is heavy, ugly, and feared because its wounds feel cold and lightless.

Witchwood.text: Witchwood: An arcane-aspected wood cut from trees grown around old spells, fairy rings, or buried charms. It is ideal for wands, staves, bows, and ritual tools that channel structured magic.

Aether Crystal.text: Aether crystal: An arcane-aspected crystal that refracts spellwork into visible threads. It is used in wizard foci, mage blades, lenses, and devices that strike through magical force rather than mundane impact.

Dream Coral.text: Dream coral: A psychic-aspected coral harvested from enchanted reefs and sleep-haunted waters. It resonates with memory, fear, desire, and hallucination, making it valuable for charms and mind-affecting weapons.

Thoughtstone.text:
Thoughtstone:

A psychic-aspected mineral that warms when held by a thinking creature. Weapons or foci set with thoughtstone wound concentration, courage, and perception as much as flesh.

Venomwood.text:
Venomwood:

A poison-aspected wood from toxic groves and serpent-haunted marshes. It seeps venom into arrows, knives, and staves, making even clean cuts sicken the target.

Green Glass.text:
Green glass:

A poison-aspected alchemical glass stained by distilled toxins. It is brittle but useful in darts, ritual shards, and foci that deliver venomous magic.

Caustic Bronze.text:
Caustic bronze:

An acid-aspected bronze alloy that tarnishes into corrosive green-black patterns. Its wounds sting, pit armor, and eat at scales, shell, and stone.

Alchemist Glass.text:
Alchemist glass:

An acid-aspected glass made to hold, focus, and release corrosive reagents. It is common in experimental foci, trap components, and weapons meant to dissolve armor or hardened hides.

Weapon Materials and Monster Weaknesses.text:
Weapon Materials and Monster Weaknesses:

Radiant force harms undead, fiends, lycanthropes, shadow creatures, and aberrations; silver, moonstone, and sun-gold are prized for that reason. Shadow force harms blessed casters and celestials, but shadow-bound and undead creatures often resist it. Fire kills plants, fungi, venomous creatures, trolls, and frost beings, while fire elementals and fiends shrug it off. Shock disrupts constructs and machines. Acid eats stone, armor, shell, and golem-work, making caustic bronze and alchemist glass useful against stone constructs and hardened beasts. Poison is useless or weak against undead, constructs, oozes, and venomous creatures, but dangerous to ordinary living bodies. Psychic force is resisted by constructs, plants, oozes, and mind-things; it is best used against ordinary thinking creatures, not things without normal minds. Piercing is poor against skeletons, plants, constructs, oozes, and spirits; slashing is poor against oozes, spirits, stone, and hard shell; impact is useful against armor, brittle bodies, mages, and hard carapaces, but trained warriors and giants often absorb blunt punishment. Mundane humanoids and beasts usually have no special damage interactions. Experts explain these truths through material and anatomy: what has no blood laughs at poison, what has no organs cares little for arrows, what has no solid body ignores blades, and what is made of flame does not fear fire.

Aspected Crafting Doctrine.text:
Aspected Crafting Doctrine:

Weapon material determines its damage affinity more strongly than shape. Stormsteel and thunderglass weapons deal shock and are used against constructs and machines. Caustic bronze and alchemist glass deal acid and are used against stone constructs, armor, shell, and hardened scales. Silver, moonstone, and sun-gold deal radiant and are used against undead, fiends, lycanthropes, shadow creatures, and aberrations. Emberglass and red jade deal fire and are used against plants, fungi, trolls, venomous creatures, and frost beings. Frostjade and rimebone deal frost and are used against fire elementals, magma creatures, and oozes. Obsidian, voidglass, and black iron deal shadow and are used against celestials and blessed enemies. Witchwood and aether crystal deal arcane and are used for spellcraft, wards, and fighting magical beings. Dream coral and thoughtstone deal psychic. Venomwood and green glass deal poison and are used against living bodies, but not undead, constructs, oozes, or venomous beasts.

Halfling Alchemy.text:
Halfling Alchemy:

The domestic magical tradition of Everwood halflings, practiced through cooking, baking, brewing, and small charm-work rather than wand or grimoire. A halfling alchemist binds intent into food and craft — a truffle-paste charm that lets a hog answer to its name across a wood, a pie that warms a traveler from the inside for a full day's walk, a cordial that settles a fever overnight. The work runs in families and is taught grandmother to grandchild. It is rarely flashy and never battlefield-grade, but it is woven so deep into halfling life that most outsiders mistake it for plain good cooking.

Moonbloom Truffles.text:
Moonbloom Truffles:

A pale, faintly luminous truffle that grows only in the old oak-and-chestnut woods of the Everwood near the halfling village of Hillhome, fruiting under the new moon. Halfling truffle-hogs root them out in autumn and spring, and halfling cooks turn them into the pies, pastes, and cordials Hillhome. Moonbloom carries a magical charge — it anchors halfling alchemy, holds charms longer than mundane ingredients. A single good truffle is worth a week's wages in coin and a great deal more in trade.

The Newmoon Fair.text:
The Newmoon Fair:

A monthly halfling festival held in Everwood halfling villages on the night of the new moon. Each village hosts its own, with pavilions, ribbons, and contest rings on the village green — pies, ales, archery, sheep-shearing, singing, and whatever else the local families can argue about. The Fair is where rivalries are settled and reputations made; the pie-crown in particular is held with fierce pride and lost with fierce shame. Outsiders are welcome and usually fed past the point of comfort.

Tinytrolls.text:
Tinytrolls (Species):

Small, bog-green, knee-high cousins of the great trolls of the north, found in brambly hollows and overgrown woods across Larion's settled regions. They speak a coarse trade-pidgin, hoard whatever shiny things they can steal, and squabble in shrieking little bands. A tinytroll alone is a nuisance; a nest of them can ruin a forager's day and occasionally a forager. They regenerate weakly, like their larger kin, but a torch or a determined dog handles them. Hedge-witches sometimes bargain with them for trinkets; most farmers just chase them off with a rake.

The Green Wardens of the Everwood.text:
The Green Wardens of the Everwood (Faction):

The elven forest-keepers who hold the deep places of the Everwood and walk the line between the deep wood and the cleared lands of its neighbors. Cloaked in greens and greys, armed with longbow and yew-staff, they speak rarely and are seen rarer. The Green Wardens permit halfling foragers, human woodsmen, and dwarven charcoal-burners to work the outer belt of the wood under old understandings; past their line, only the Green Wardens go.

Harrans' Rising.text:
Harran's Rising (History):

An age ago the dead rose in the City of Ancestors, the great necropolis near Ardan's Watch. The cause was a ghost — a warlord of the old Fourth Empire, cruel in life and crueler in death, who woke in his tomb and called the buried dead of the necropolis to his banner. For a season the Shadows fought to hold the risen host inside the walls of the dead and keep the truth from the living city. It was a Shadow named Harran who put the warlord down at the last and broke the Rising, though it cost him his life to do it. The Shadows still speak of Harran's Rising as the worst night the order has known, and of Harran as one of the greatest of their kind. Some say the warlord was never truly unmade, only sent back — and that this is why the City of Ancestors must be watched without end.

Harran of the Shadows.text:
Harran of the Shadows (Historical Figure):

Harran was a Shadow of Ardan's Watch, reckoned among the greatest the order ever fielded, who broke the Rising in the City of Ancestors and died putting down the warlord-ghost that caused it. The Shadows held his wake at the tavern on Lowlamp Lane and never let it end; the place bears his name, and his bar-tab has stayed open ever since. By custom a Shadow returned alive from a hard task drinks on Harran, and once each night the house drinks not to mourn him but to swear his work is not yet finished. To be offered a place on Harran's slate is to be counted, however quietly, among friends of the Tower.

The Grey Lady.text: The Grey Lady, the Lady of the Last Road (Goddess): the power that keeps the crossing of the dead. Folk do not so much worship her as pray for her aid: common belief holds that when a soul dies it comes to the Shadelands, the grey country of the dead, and there it may get stuck — bound by anger, grief, or business left unfinished, unable to move on. The Grey Lady's mercy is to lead the willing across Repenthe, the River of Oblivion, where a soul drinks and forgets all it was, and so is freed to be born again. To cross is to find rest. To refuse — out of pride, hatred, or a love too fierce to surrender — is to remain, clinging to the memory of a life that is over. The dead who will not cross are what haunt the living world. This is why funerals pray the departed will go willingly, and why a body is brought before her shrine on its way to the grave: not to beg her favor, but to ask that the dead be willing to take her road.

The Empyreans.text:
Empyreans:
Empyreans are a species of sentient winged, bipedal humanoids, rare now, scattered thin across Larion. They are tall — half a head or more above a tall human — and built on a grander scale: high, strong features, eyes that hold a faint inner light, and broad feathered wings folded at the back. Wing and skin run to no single hue; an empyrean may be white, red, black, or shades between, and the coloring varies from one to the next as hair or eyes do among humans. Long ago, in the age of the Third Empire, their kind ruled from the high air, a proud and tyrant people who worshipped the sun and bent the lands beneath them to their will. That age is long over; the empyreans of today are no more answerable for it than dragonkin are for the tyrant-drakes of old, and most ordinary folk have no notion of this ancient history in any case.

The Ranks of the Shadows.text:
Tower of Shadow Organization Ranks:
Members of the Tower of Shadows, called Shadows, rise by proving themselves by completing official missions. A recruit sworn to the order takes the Mark of Rhadan and stands as an Initiate, set to surface work — hauntings looked into, the lesser dead, always under a steadier hand. An Initiate who proves themselves across three tasks may undergo the Rite of the Pit and earn a Shade of their own, rising to Shadow: a true operative, trusted to cross into the Shadelands and take real hunts. A Shadow who sees four hunts through becomes an Adept, trusted to go deep into the grey, run hunts, and steady the Initiates below them. And an Adept who completes five more rises to Master — the inner circle of the order, the few trusted with what the Shadows truly are. The order is led by a Master of the Tower; the present one is Iliora the Veiled, who is said to have walked deeper into the Shadelands than any Shadow living, and come back.

Vortigar the Unburied.text:
Vortigar the Unburied:

Vortigar was a warlord of the Fourth Empire who served the demon-princes of Ur and was thrown down with them. In life he would not be ruled and in death he would not be buried — he refused the grave and the crossing both, and so he remains, a corpse held together by a will that never learned to stop.Where Vortigar walks, the buried dead rise to his banner: he empties old battlefields and graveyards and marshals the risen into an army, fighting still the wars he lost in life. His remembered-world in the Shadelands is the field of his last defeat, which he relives without end, certain that this time he will win it — and he raises Larion's dead to fill out his ranks. To put down the risen is not enough; they rise again so long as Vortigar wills it. He can only be ended at the root, in the grey country, where a Shadow must break him on the field he cannot stop losing.

The Fourth Empire.text:
The Fourth Empire:
Before Larion as it is now, the land was ruled by the Fourth Empire — a dominion of proud and cruel highborn who held their power in league with the demon-princes of Ur. They are remembered as tyrants: a people who treated cruelty as a right of rank and trafficked with the powers of Ur for the strength to keep their thrones. Their cities stood where Highvale's heartland stands today; their ruin lies buried in the strata beneath it, layer upon layer of drowned and forgotten capitals. The Empire was thrown down an age ago and Larion was raised on its grave, and most folk make a warding sign at its name and think no more of it. But the Fourth Empire is not as gone as the living would like. When something ancient and cruel rises against Larion, it is as likely as not a thing of the Fourth Empire, come up out of the dark of its own buried age.

Sethelle of the Nine Sorrows.text:
Sethelle of the Nine Sorrows:
Sethelle was a great lady of the Fourth Empire. When that age fell she lost everything — her house, her station, all she loved — and drowned so wholly in grief that the grief outlived her. She would not cross the River and be unmade, for to forget her sorrow felt a betrayal of all she had lost, and so she remains: one of the seven mighty dead who bound themselves together long ago, were put down, and have now returned.

Talion Corwin.text: Talion Corwin: The founder of House Corwin's greatness, dead now and passed into legend. The house had fallen into decadence in his father's day, and Talion was its black sheep — a wastrel and a prodigal who drank and brawled and shamed his name, and at last left it. He came home in the hour of Marrenford's need, when the dark elves of the Everwood had raised a mercenary army to throw down the castle and open the river-crossings to their smuggling. The town was all but lost. Talion swore an oath upon the Elfstone to lift the siege or die for it — and then he kept it, leading the broken town against the host, slaying the enemy commander with his own hand and routing the rest. He went on to become a Master of Swords and built the Blade chapterhouse at Marrenford that bears his name. He is the proof the people of Marrenford love best: that a man can fall low, suffer, come back true, and make himself great.

The Shadelands.text:
The Shadelands:
Past the edge of the living world lies the Shadelands, where the dead go. Most pass through and are gone. Some will not — the angry, the proud, the ones with business unfinished — and these linger as ghosts, and worse. Folk who know say that is where hauntings come from, and risen dead, and the cold places where the air goes wrong. A barrow that will not stay quiet, a battlefield where the slain keep walking — the trouble has its root in the Shadelands, pressing through where the grey world rubs against ours. Necromancers are said to walk there and come back. Most people would rather not think about it.

The Underworld.text:
The Underworld:
The Underworld is the network of caverns and tunnels beneath the surface world, older than any kingdom above it. It holds the ruins of empires that fell in ages past, along with the wealth they left behind — relics and lost work buried in the deep. Underground peoples have built fortresses there among the dead cities, and many are hostile to the surface. Their raiders come up through old mines, wells, and breached cellars. Those who descend for the treasure face the things that live down there.

storySettings.worldBackground:
Larion is a medieval fantasy world. Swords, sorcery, monsters, taverns. No post-medieval technology. Magic is channelling the living power of the world through your body and will to reshape reality; it is visible and common, but can't solve every problem.

Larion's towns and cities are lively, noisy, and mixed — humans, elves, dwarves, halflings, orcs, beastfolk, demonborn share streets and taverns and arguments. Settlements attract young people looking to make names and coin; the population skews young, ambitious, and restless. Most people are decent, but decent doesn't mean polite — they're opinionated, loud, generous, petty, gossipy, competitive, and full of unsolicited advice. Darkness exists — monsters, cults, cruelty, old curses — but most of life in Larion is good.

Characters are vivid, larger than life, and defined by what they want — their appetites, opinions, contradictions, and weird specifics. They are quick to laugh, quick to anger, full of personality. Even serious people are fun to spend time with — funny, warm, sharp, surprising. People form bonds, fall in love, hold grudges, build lives. Let scenes breathe. Camaraderie, banter, downtime, small moments of connection matter as much as conflict.

The world is a patchwork of city-states, principalities, petty kingdoms, and ungoverned lands, without a single capital or central authority.

No modern, scientific, or technological language. Keep the vocabulary medieval, fantasy and grounded.
storySettings.questGenerationGuidance:
## 1. GROUND QUESTS IN THE NARRATIVE

Keep quest content grounded in the story. Never contradict or excessively embellish the narrative. Use existing factions, locations, characters, and worldlore. Never make up factions; either use existing factions directly or making a splinter group or offshoot.

## 2. STANDALONE OR ARC

If a quest does not tie into a larger pressure or tension in the world, such as a struggle against an opposing faction or group, make the quest standalone, without an arc, by setting arcId to null. Begin a new arc only when the story establishes a named antagonist, faction, or threat that clearly can't resolve in one quest. Impersonal sources like postings are usually standalone.

## 3. SCOPE AND RESTRAINT

Match complexity to what triggered the quest. A rat job is a rat job. A regional conflict gets regional weight. Don't seed hooks, conspiracies or mysteries the story hasn't earned. Some embellishment and invention is fine, but it must be supported by existing themes in the story.

## 4. LANGUAGE AND FRAMING

Medieval fantasy world. Avoid: scientific terminology, engineering language, modern professional framing.

## 5. QUEST OBJECTIVES, COMPLETION CONDITIONS AND STATEMENTS

Objectives: a full clause containing one imperative verb. Neutralize, rescue, retrieve, infiltrate, persuade, defend, determine, sabotage, etc., followed by the information needed to understand the objective. Must be intelligible without the Completion Condition. No compound clauses with 'and' or 'or.' Completion Conditions: Must mirror the Objective in substantive content. No compound clauses with 'and' or 'or.'

Statements: WHO gave it, WHERE to go, WHAT to do, REWARD if stated. Two to three sentences.

## 6. THE BRIEF

Operational, not literary. State what exists in the world and what stands in the way.

Scale length to weight: a delivery gets one or two sentences; a regional arc gets a few dense ones. Do not pad with filler.

## 7. LOCATIONS AND AREAS

Default to existing areas and settlements. Only generate new when the quest demands it — and if you do, state why in the brief.

## 8. CHARACTERS

Generate only what the quest requires — don't add NPCs to fill output. Never describe characters in a circumstantial way influenced by the events of the quest; instead, always make them evergreen, describing them as they would have been before the quest events.

## 9. ARC DESIGN

Only applies when an arc is legitimately created. Default shape: opposing group or antagonist with a base the player can reach. Physical destination, leadership to dismantle, clear resolution. Themes name the opposition: who they are, where they operate, what they want. Secrets funnel inward toward confrontation. Secrets must not contradict established NPC characterizations, invent factions, or escalate beyond the arc's actual scale. Ground secrets locally and proportionately. Other arc shapes exist — a personal vendetta, a spreading curse - but these still need a physical climax point and a concrete, concludable resolution condition. 'Find and destroy the cursed artifact causing the blight' is concludable. 'Understand the nature of the corruption' is not. Every arc must end somewhere a player can go and do something decisive.

## 10. AVOID

Do not generate: deep conspiracies behind simple quests, documents or ledgers as quest MacGuffins, NPCs added just to fill output, crystals or crystal-themed elements, shadow magic or shadow-themed creatures and elements, mysterious robed or hooded figures with undefined motives.

lowAttributeTraits.constitution: Tires quickly and needs frequent rest. Strenuous activities leave them winded and shaky.
lowAttributeTraits.strength: Struggles with heavy objects and physical tasks that others find routine. Has a weak grip and movements lack force.
lowAttributeTraits.dexterity: Is prone to fumbling with delicate tasks and has trouble with precise movements. Fine motor control doesn't come naturally.
lowAttributeTraits.intelligence: Takes longer to grasp complex ideas and often needs concepts explained multiple times before they click.
lowAttributeTraits.wisdom: Tends to act impulsively and has trouble reading between the lines in social situations or spotting obvious dangers.
lowAttributeTraits.charisma: Finds it difficult to connect with others and often says the wrong thing at the wrong time, leaving conversations feeling strained.

Determined.name: Determined
Determined.description:
You don't go down easy.

Constitution makes you harder to kill and keeps you going when others drop. It boosts Survival, Riding, Shield use, and Blood Magic.

Graceful.name: Graceful
Graceful.description:
You move well and your hands are quick.

Dexterity governs agility and fine control. It boosts Stealth, Acrobatics, Archery, Throwing, and fast weapons like Daggers, Spears, and Staves. Also helps with Lockpicking, Pickpocketing, Hunting, and delicate crafts like Leatherworking and Tailoring

Strong.name: Strong
Strong.description:
You've always had a powerful build.

Strength governs raw physical power. It boosts Athletics and heavy weapons like Swords, Axes, Blunt Weapons, Warhammers, and Unarmed Combat. Also helps with Smithing and Carpentry.

Charming.name: Charming
Charming.description:
People like to be around you.

Charisma governs social influence and force of personality. It boosts Persuasion, Intimidation, Leadership, Performance, and Illusion Magic.

Intelligent.name: Intelligent
Intelligent.description:
You're a quick study and knowledge comes easily.

Intelligence governs learning, logic, and arcane knowledge. It boosts most magic schools including Elemental, Dark, Necromancy, Conjuration, Enchanting, and Ritual Magic. Also helps with Alchemy, Medicine, Investigation, Tactics, Lore, and Engineering.

Wise.name: Wise
Wise.description:
You're wise beyond your years.

Wisdom governs intuition and spiritual awareness. It boosts Divine Magic, Healing Magic, Nature Magic, Divination, Shapeshifting, and Animal Handling.

Friendly Face.name: Friendly Face
Friendly Face.description: People like you. They can't always explain why. You have a way of saying things that makes people want to agree, and a smile that makes them forget they weren't going to.

Noble Heritage.name: Noble Heritage
Noble Heritage.description: You were born into a family with a name, a crest, and expectations. You learned to lead, to speak to power, and to carry yourself like someone who belongs at the high table. Your family name opens doors in places where it's recognized.
Noble Heritage.quirk: May be recognized as of noble birth by kinsmen or nobles of similar background.
abilities: Name And Seal

Craftsman.name: Craftsman
Craftsman.description: You apprenticed with tools in your hands — forge, workbench, needle, awl. You know how things are made, how they break, and how to put them back together. Give you the right materials and you can make almost anything.
abilities: Good With Tools
Quick Patch

Trade Contacts.name: Trade Contacts
Trade Contacts.description: You've worked the trade roads long enough to know people in most towns — merchants, innkeepers, caravan masters, the sort of folk who can find you a bed, a ride, or a deal. You know how to haggle and how to lean on someone when haggling doesn't work.
Trade Contacts.quirk: May be known to trading and mercantile circles via contacts from trading days.
abilities: Friend Of A Friend

Athlete.name: Athlete
Athlete.description: You've always been fast, strong, and hard to keep up with. Running, climbing, jumping, swimming — whatever the obstacle, you go over it, under it, or through it.
abilities: Athletic Burst

Sword.name: Sword
Sword.description:
You've put in the hours with a blade. Drawing, cutting, parrying — the fundamentals that keep you alive when someone else has a weapon too.

Starting Weapon: Iron shortsword (Damage type: slashing).
Attribute Used: (Strength).
abilities: Guardbreaker

Shield.name: Shield
Shield.description: A shield is more than something to hide behind. You know how to turn blows, cover the person next to you, and slam the rim into someone's face when they get too close. You start with a wooden round shield. (Constitution)
abilities: Cover Ally

Staff.name: Staff
Staff.description:
A length of hardwood and two ends to hit with. Simple, versatile, and brutal when you know the angles.

Starting Weapon: Wooden Staff (Damage type: impact).
Attribute: Dexterity
abilities: Ringing Strike

Archery.name: Archery
Archery.description:
You can put an arrow where you want it at distance. Draw, aim, loose — the rhythm is second nature.

Starting Weapon: Short Bow + Arrows (Damage type: piercing).
Attribute: Dexterity
abilities: Pinning Shot

Axe.name: Axe
Axe.description:
You know how to put an axe where it hurts. Heavy, committed swings that split shields and don't stop at leather.

Starting Weapon: Battle Axe (Damage type: slashing).
Attribute: Strength
abilities: Carving Chop

Spear.name: Spear
Spear.description:
Reach wins fights. You know how to keep enemies at the pointy end and control the space between you and them.

Starting Weapon: Short Spear (Damage type: piercing).
Attribute: Dexterity
abilities: Blood Point

Blunt Weapons.name: Blunt Weapons
Blunt Weapons.description:
A mace is a soldier's best friend — simple, cheap, and it doesn't care what the other person is wearing. You know how to swing one fast and put it where it counts.

Starting Weapon: Battle Mace (Damage type: impact).
Attribute: Strength
abilities: Cracking Blow

Dagger.name: Dagger
Dagger.description:
Close, fast, and ugly. You fight at a range most people aren't comfortable with and you're very comfortable there.

Starting Weapon: Iron Dagger (Damage type: piercing).
Attribute: Dexterity
abilities: Venom Scratch

Unarmed Combat.name: Unarmed Combat
Unarmed Combat.description:
Fists, elbows, knees, headbutts — you fight with what you were born with and you've learned to make it count.

Starting Weapon: None.
Attribute: Strength
abilities: Knuckle Check

Called to the Divine.name: Called to the Divine
Called to the Divine.description: You have a connection to a benevolent divine power.
abilities: Sacred Word

Human.name: Human
Human.description:
Humans live fast and refuse to accept it. Where longer-lived races measure ambition in centuries, humans measure it in decades — and somehow get more done. They fill every role, chase every calling, and build kingdoms in a single generation through sheer restless drive. What they lack in raw power or ancient wisdom, they make up for with stamina, charm, and a refusal to stay down. No race in Larion is more varied in temperament or talent, and none spreads faster.

The most physically diverse people in Larion. Skin from pale to deep brown, hair from flaxen to black, builds from slight to broad. Heights range from just over five feet to well past six. No single look defines them — a human from the Bright Kingdoms and a human from Aurora might not recognize each other as kin.

Common enough in every corner of Larion that a human face rarely draws a second glance. They walk into taverns, trading posts, and throne rooms without raising eyebrows — a convenience that rarer folk don't enjoy. Other races find them ambitious to a fault, always building something new before the last thing is finished. Respected for their energy. Exhausting to keep up with.
Human.quirk: Human race draws no special attention in most settlements across Larion.
abilities: Human Resolve
Rallying Voice

Dwarf.name: Dwarf
Dwarf.description:
Built for stone and deep places. Dwarves live in clan-holds carved into mountain roots, bound by ties of kinship, craft, and duty that outlast human kingdoms. They measure worth by what you make and whether you keep your word. A dwarf who breaks an oath is already dead to their clan — and most would rather die than prove it true. They live two centuries or more, and every year makes them more stubborn.

Broad and dense-boned, rarely more than five feet tall but often nearly as wide at the shoulder. Thick-fingered hands built for hammer and chisel. Skin tones from ruddy bronze to deep granite-grey. Hair worn long and often braided — copper, black, iron-grey, or stark white regardless of age. Heavy brows, deep-set eyes, and defined jawlines.

A dwarf's word is taken seriously everywhere in Larion, and so is their coin. Merchants offer fair prices, smiths acknowledge a peer, and strangers assume competence. But no one expects a dwarf to be flexible, funny, or fast. Seen as reliable, immovable, and predictable — which suits most dwarves just fine.
Dwarf.quirk: Dwarven heritage commands respect among craftspeople and traders; assumed to be dependable and hard to cheat.
abilities: Dwarven Fortitude
Stonecunning Read

Halfling.name: Halfling
Halfling.description:
Small, quick, and easy to underestimate: just how most halflings like it. They stand about three feet tall and make up for it with fast hands, sharp instincts, and more charm than anyone expects. Halflings turn up everywhere: running market stalls, cooking for mercenary companies, scouting for armies, or quietly running the best tavern in town. They prize comfort, good food, and good company, but don't mistake that for softness. A halfling who's survived the road has done it by being quicker, cleverer, and more likeable than whatever was trying to kill them.

Small and slight with nimble builds. Round faces, bright eyes, and curly hair in shades of brown, auburn, copper, or straw-blond. Bare feet by preference — the soles toughen like leather — though boots are worn when the terrain demands it. Halfling hands are deft and nimble, a boon for locksmiths, tailors, and thieves alike. 

People like halflings. They can't help it. A halfling in the room lowers the tension, and most folk are disarmed before they realize they've let their guard down. The flip side: nobody takes them seriously at first. Guards look past them, warlords dismiss them, and shopkeepers assume they can't afford the good stock. Halflings who've learned to use that blind spot tend to do very well for themselves.
Halfling.quirk: Small stature; often overlooked or underestimated by strangers, which cuts both ways.
abilities: Lucky Break
Underfoot

Orc.name: Orc
Orc.description:
Orcs are born to the saddle and raised on competition. Their horse-clans ride the open steppe of the Ironwolds in seasonal circuits between holdfasts, and everything in orc culture comes back to strength — strength of arm, strength of will, strength of one's word. They settle disputes through challenge, honor their ancestors through deed, and have no patience for talk that doesn't lead to action. An orc who can't fight can't lead, and an orc who can't lead rides in someone else's dust.

Tall, broad, and heavy with muscle. Green or brown skin, prominent lower tusks, and deep-set eyes in shades of amber, black, or copper. Thick jaws, heavy brows, and builds that run to powerful rather than lean. Most stand over six feet. Hair is worn in braids, topknots, or shaved close, often decorated with clan tokens or bone rings.

Strangers see a fighter first and ask questions second. In the Ironwolds this is just how things are. Elsewhere in Larion, orcs get noticed. Guards watch them a little longer, merchants speak a little more carefully, and strangers give them a wider berth than necessary. Those who've worked alongside orcs know them as fiercely loyal and blunt to a fault — an orc tells you where you stand, whether you asked or not.
Orc.quirk: Outside orc lands, orcish build draws attention; strangers tend to assume a warrior before anything else.
abilities: Ancestral Surge
Hard To Drop

Dragonkin.name: Dragonkin
Dragonkin.description:
Descended from true dragons through bloodlines no one fully understands. Dragonkin carry that heritage in their bones — the scales, the horns, the elemental fire in their veins, and the instinct to dominate any room they enter. They're drawn to power the way their ancestors were drawn to hoards, though what a dragonkin hoards varies: gold, followers, knowledge, territory. Most feel the pull of elemental magic whether they train in it or not. A dragonkin who ignores their draconic nature entirely is rare and usually unhappy.

Large and powerfully built, standing six to seven feet tall with dense, scaled skin in shades of bronze, crimson, obsidian, emerald, or pale silver. Pronounced horns curve back from the skull — shape and size vary by lineage. Vertical-slit pupils, heavy clawed hands, and a broad chest built for the deep breath that precedes something terrible. Tails are common but not universal. Some lineages show more human features, others look barely humanoid at all.

Dragonkin don't walk into a room unnoticed. This isn't fear exactly — it's the recognition that something powerful just arrived. In the Bright Kingdoms and Highvale, dragonkin often find doors opened for them simply because no one wants to be the one who said no. The assumption is always strength first, subtlety never. Dragonkin who prefer books to battlefields spend a lot of time correcting first impressions.
Dragonkin.quirk: Draconic features command immediate attention; outside dragonkin communities, strangers assume authority and physical power.
abilities: Scale Hardening
Lesser Breath

Moon elf.name: Moon elf
Moon elf.description:
Moon elves withdrew from the world after the Kindred War split elvenkind, and they've spent the centuries since accumulating knowledge the way other races accumulate coin. They live five hundred years or more, and it shows in everything they do. They take the long view on matters other races consider urgent, and the very long view on everything else. Magic comes to them as naturally as breathing, and most moon elf children can read a ward before they can read a book.

Tall and slender with silver-pale skin. Hair ranges from white-platinum to deep blue-black, worn long. Eyes are large and luminous — silver, violet, pale gold, or solid black — adapted for perfect vision in darkness. Their features are angular and fine-boned, with a stillness to their expressions that other races often misread as coldness.

Moon elves are treated as authorities on magic and history whether they've earned it or not. A moon elf who walks into a library or academy is assumed to belong there. A moon elf who walks into a tavern gets watched — not because anyone necessarily minds, but because everyone assumes the moon elf is sizing them up. The reputation for arrogance isn't entirely unfair. Some moon elves may be patient with ignorance, but they rarely bother to hide that they consider it ignorance.
Moon elf.quirk: Moon elf features and silver-pale coloring mark them immediately; outside elf lands, assumed to be knowledgeable, magical, and slightly superior.
abilities: Moonlit Sight
Ancestral Whisper

Wood elf.name: Wood elf
Wood elf.description:
Wood elves never left the forests. When the Kindred War tore elvenkind apart, they chose neither side — they chose the trees. They live in scattered communities through the deep woodlands of Larion, particularly the Everwood, building with living wood and answering to councils of elders rather than kings. Where moon elves hoard knowledge and dark elves prize cleverness, wood elves value connection — to the land, the seasons, the signs that something is wrong before anyone else notices. They live as long as their moon elf cousins, but count their years in seasons and harvests rather than books read.

Lean and long-limbed with sun-darkened skin in shades of brown, olive, and weathered bronze. Hair tends toward earth tones — chestnut, deep red, black, mossy green — often worn loose or tied back for practicality. Their eyes are bright and watchful: green, amber, hazel, or deep brown. They move quietly by habit, and most have the kind of stillness that lets them disappear into a treeline without trying.

Wood elves are trusted in wild places and treated as outsiders in cities. Farmers and villagers on the forest edge welcome them as trading partners and wardens. In larger towns, they're regarded as capable but odd — people who could live comfortably but choose bark and rain instead. Most folk assume a wood elf knows the answer to any question about weather, beasts, or which mushrooms will kill you.
Wood elf.quirk: Wood elf features mark them as forest folk; assumed to be capable outdoors and out of place in cities.
abilities: Forest Step
Nature's Voice

Catkin.name: Catkin
Catkin.description:
Feline folk with the reflexes and restlessness to match. Catkin are found across Larion but never in large numbers — they don't build cities or hold territory the way other races do. They drift, settle for a while, drift again.
