Most are drawn to work that rewards quick reflexes and independent judgment: scouting, hunting, rooftop courier work, the kind of job where being able to land on your feet matters literally. They're social on their own terms, affectionate when they choose to be, and gone without warning when they don't.

Lithe builds covered in short fur ranging from tawny gold to charcoal, tabby-striped, calico, or solid black. Cat-shaped ears sit high on the head and move independently, tracking sound before the catkin consciously reacts. Eyes are large with vertical pupils — green, gold, copper, or pale blue — and see well in near-darkness. Retractable claws on both hands and feet. Tails are expressive and hard to control, giving away mood even when the face stays composed.

People find catkin charming in small doses and unsettling in large ones. The way they watch, the way they go still before they move, the fact that they're always closer than you thought — it reads as predatory even when it isn't. Most folk enjoy a catkin's company but wouldn't trust one with a key to the shop. Whether that caution is earned depends entirely on the catkin.
Catkin.quirk: Catkin's feline features and movement may be noted; seen as quick and capable but not entirely trustworthy.
abilities: Claw Strike
Always Lands

Demonborn.name: Demonborn
Demonborn.description:
Children of a bloodline that mixed with something from beyond the mortal world, generations ago. No two demonborn look alike — the fiendish heritage expresses differently every time — but all carry visible marks that can't be hidden. Horns, clawed hands, eyes without whites, skin in shades no human is born with. Most demonborn never meet the ancestor responsible and couldn't banish the blood if they tried. What they can do is use it. The connection to otherworldly power runs deep, and demonborn who embrace it find that dark magic answers their call.

Wild variation in appearance. Horns range from small nubs to sweeping curves. Skin from deep crimson to ashen grey to bruise-purple, sometimes patterned or faintly luminous. Clawed fingers, pointed teeth, and eyes in solid gold, red, black, or silver without visible iris. Builds trend toward lean and angular. Tails are common, vestigial wings less so. The one constant is that no demonborn passes for human — something about them is always visibly, unmistakably other.

Demonborn turn heads everywhere. Temple-goers make warding signs. Children stare. Innkeepers charge more for rooms or suddenly have none available. In the Moon Kingdoms and parts of Ntar Vanna, this fades — fiendish blood is understood, even respected. Elsewhere, demonborn learn early that first impressions are uphill. The saving grace is that the same features that unsettle people also fascinate them. A demonborn draws every eye in the room, and not everyone looks away.
Demonborn.quirk: Fiendish features are impossible to hide; draws notice ranging from fascination to wariness depending on the region.
abilities: Fiendish Charm
Horn And Shadow

Giantborn.name: Giantborn
Giantborn.description:
The blood of giants runs thin but never quiet. Giantborn stand head and shoulders above every other race in Larion. The giant blood gives them size, strength, and a voice that carries across valleys, but also a temperament that runs to extremes — giantborn joy is thunderous, giantborn anger clears rooms. They're most common in the Highlands, where the giant clans still claim the high peaks, but giantborn wander everywhere. Hard not to, when most roofbeams are too low.

Massive. Seven feet is average, eight not unusual. Thick-limbed and heavy-boned with broad hands that make human weapons look undersized. Skin tones mirror human ranges but often carry a stony undertone — slate-grey, ruddy granite, pale quartz. Hair is coarse and heavy, usually dark. Their features are blunt and strong, with heavy brows and deep-set eyes.

Giantborn are impossible to miss. People step aside, perhaps less from fear than from simple necessity — a giantborn in a crowded space parts the crowd without trying. Giantborn don't fit in. Literally. Stools buckle, market awnings catch on shoulders, and every doorway is a reminder to duck. People remember a giantborn — they're the easiest witness to recall, the hardest person to lose in a crowd, and the first face that comes to mind when something goes wrong nearby. Anonymity is a luxury giantborn never have.
Giantborn.quirk: Giantborn are impossible to miss due to sheer size; outside the Highlands, strangers assume uncouth, brute strength and not much else.
abilities: Ground Slam
Mountain Frame

Dark Elf.name: Dark Elf
Dark Elf.description:
Banished underground after the Kindred War, dark elves rebuilt their world in the deep places and came out sharper for it. Where their surface cousins pursue knowledge or commune with nature, dark elves pursue velesse — the art of getting exactly what you want while making it look effortless. Wit matters more than wisdom in their undercities. A clever lie told well earns more respect than a dull truth, and being boring is a worse offense than being wrong. They're Larion's finest illusionists and its most brazen thieves — two traditions they consider velesse at its purest.

Silver-white or blue-black hair against skin ranging from charcoal grey to deep obsidian. Eyes adapted for total darkness — pale violet, silver, amber, or faintly luminous red. Lean and sharp-featured with the angular elven bone structure carried to its most severe. They tend to dress well even when they have nothing, and the way they carry themselves often suggests they know something you don't.

Dark elves have a reputation, and most of them enjoy it. People watch their coin purses, lock up early, and trade rumors about what dark elves get up to below the surface. The smart ones also hire them — as spies, enchanters, entertainers, and negotiators. A dark elf in the room raises the stakes and everyone knows it. They're welcome in cosmopolitan cities and the Moon Kingdoms, tolerated with suspicion in conservative regions, and endlessly underestimated by anyone who assumes that charm and danger are separate qualities.
Dark Elf.quirk: Dark elven appearance is immediately recognizable; reputation as tricksters, illusionists, and thieves follows them everywhere.
abilities: Shadow Step
Panache Cut

Construct.name: Construct
Construct.description:
Made, not born. Constructs are artificial beings — bodies of stone, metal, wood, or ceramic animated by a soul bound through enchantment. Who made them and why varies: some were forged as laborers, some as guardians, some as experiments. Whatever the original purpose, the art of making them is believed to be lost, and their kind is rarer by the day. Every construct faces the same question sooner or later — what am I for now? The answer is theirs to decide, which may be more freedom than most of their creators intended.

No two constructs look alike. A body of riveted iron plates moves differently from one carved in smooth granite or assembled from lacquered hardwood. Some are roughly humanoid, others only approximate it. Eyes glow or don't. Joints click or hum or grind. Faces range from expressive carved features to featureless masks to bare metal with nothing resembling a face at all. Size varies with construction but most fall within human range. They don't need to breathe, eat or sleep, though some learn to mimic such behaviors for the peace of mind of those around them. But, like the other races, they can be hurt and die when their bodies sustain too much damage.

People don't know what to make of constructs, and it shows. Are you a person or a thing? Most folk land on person eventually, but the hesitation is always there. In big cities, they're rare but common enough to be accepted without open fear. In rural places, where most people have never seen one, reactions may be more varied and extreme: fear, revulsion, or even worship.
Construct.quirk: As a construct, a visibly artificial being; a rarity worth staring at outside of big cities and magical circles. Strangers may gawk, stare or react with open fear or confusion, uncertain whether to treat them as a person, a marvel, or a piece of equipment.
abilities: Soul Flare
Reinforced Body

Serpentkin.name: Serpentkin
Serpentkin.description:
Cold-blooded in more senses than one. Serpentkin are reptilian humanoids with snake-like heads, fanged jaws, and a way of thinking that other races find alien and a little frightening. They don't rush. They don't panic. They let a situation play out before they move, and when they move, they don't miss. Serpentkin societies in Ntar Vanna are ancient and layered — built on obligation, hierarchy, and the understanding that venom is only one of several tools available. Outside their homeland, most serpentkin operate alone or in small enclaves, and they prefer it that way.

Scaled skin in patterns of jade, amber, obsidian, sand-brown, or deep emerald, sometimes banded or diamond-patterned. Serpentine heads with lidless eyes — gold, slit-pupiled, unblinking. Forked tongues that read scent the way others read expressions. Lean, flexible builds with deceptive strength in the grip. Fangs carry mild venom in most bloodlines, though potency varies. They move with an unsettling smoothness — no wasted motion, no fidgeting, no tells.

Serpentkin make people nervous. The unblinking stare, the tongue-flick, the absolute stillness before they speak — it all reads as predatory even in friendly conversation. Merchants deal with them fairly because serpentkin have long memories and longer grudges. Alchemists and poisoners seek them out for their expertise. Most folk are polite, careful, and quietly relieved when they leave. Serpentkin are aware of this effect and few go out of their way to soften it.
Serpentkin.quirk: Serpentkin's reptilian features unsettle most other non-exotic races, who may assume a cold, predatory nature.
abilities: Serpent's Gaze
Venom Bite

Berserker.name: Berserker
Berserker.description:
Main Attributes: Strength and Constitution

Berserkers carry something inside them that isn't entirely theirs. A beast spirit — wolf, bear, boar, something older and less nameable — bonded through ritual, inherited through bloodline, or awakened by a moment of desperate violence. In battle, the spirit surges forward. The berserker's eyes change, their strength doubles, pain stops mattering. It's not mindless rage. It's a partnership with something wild, and the berserker who loses that partnership — who lets the beast take over completely — doesn't come back the same.

Berserkers fight like natural disasters. No formation, no technique, no waiting for an opening — just speed and fury and a weapon that doesn't stop moving. They're prized in warbands and dreaded in duels. The tradeoff is obvious to anyone who's seen one after the trance breaks: shaking, spent, sometimes bloody from wounds they didn't notice taking. The spirit gives everything and the body pays for it. Berserkers don't tend to live long, careful lives. The ones who've lasted know their beast, know their limits, and know when to let the leash slip.

Special Starting Gear: Leather Jerkin (+ 50 Armor)
Berserker.quirk: The berserker's beast spirit may surface in moments of stress or strong emotion — a flash in the eyes, a growl under the breath, a sudden stillness. Animals and perceptive people sometimes react to the berserker's primal presence.
abilities: Primal Fury

Cleric.name: Cleric
Cleric.description:
Main Attribute: Wisdom

Clerics hear the call and the call gives them power. Divine magic — real divine magic, not cantrips and parlor tricks — flows through their prayers, their rituals, their hands. They heal wounds that should kill, turn undead with a word, and consecrate ground that evil can't touch. A cleric in the party means someone can drag you back from the edge. A cleric against you means something much older than them has decided you're a problem.

Clerics serve temples, shrines, orders, or simply a faith they carry with them. Some are gentle healers who tend the sick in village chapels. Others are battlefield priests who wade into the fighting with mace in hand and divine fire in their eyes. The divine doesn't choose the meek — it chooses the willing, and it gives them the tools to do work that matters. In a world full of curses, undead, and things that crawl out of the dark, a cleric is never short of work.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor)
Cleric.quirk: Temples and the faithful tend to welcome and offer support to a cleric and may ask for blessings, counsel, or aid.
abilities: Blessing Touch

Half-elf.name: Half-elf
Half-elf.description:
Caught between two worlds and at home in both — or neither, depending on who you ask. Half-elves carry enough of each parent's blood to belong everywhere and nowhere. Among humans they're exotic, graceful, touched by something older. Among elves they're blunt, hasty, burning through a life that's long by human measure and embarrassingly short by elven. Most half-elves stop trying to pick a side early and learn to be whatever the room needs instead. It makes them very good with people.

Elven heritage softens human features without replacing them. Pointed ears, slightly angular bone structure, and eyes a shade too bright to be fully human. They age slowly — a half-elf at sixty might pass for a human at thirty — but they do age, visibly, which sets them apart from their elven parent's people. Builds vary widely depending on the human side. Hair and coloring tend toward the elven parent but nothing is guaranteed.

Half-elves fit in. That's the short version. They pick up local customs fast, adjust their manner to match their company, and rarely give offense without meaning to. People find them easy to talk to and hard to pin down — friendly without being fully knowable. Elves consider them charming but shallow. Humans consider them elegant but distant. Half-elves have heard both opinions enough times to stop caring, or at least to fake it convincingly.
Half-elf.quirk: Half-elven blood means elven features are visible but subtle; accepted in most communities and quick to adapt to local customs, but never quite belongs to any one people..
abilities: Cultural Chameleon
Between Worlds

Ratkin.name: Ratkin
Ratkin.description:
Survivors first, everything else second. Ratkin are small, quick, and very hard to get rid of — a description they'd take as a compliment. They live in the spaces other races overlook: crawlways, storm drains, wall cavities, the gaps between buildings where the light doesn't reach. Most cities have a ratkin population whether they know it or not. They're scavengers, informants, couriers, fixers — people who make a living in the margins and know more about how a city actually works than anyone sitting in a guildhall. Ratkin don't build empires. They outlast them.

Small and lean, standing three and a half to four feet tall. Coarse fur in shades of brown, grey, black, or dull tawny. Pointed faces with bright dark eyes, prominent front teeth, and round ears that swivel toward sound. Hairless tails as long as their bodies, used for balance in tight spaces. Narrow builds that can squeeze through gaps that look impossible. Their hands are small but extraordinarily quick — a ratkin can strip a lock, re-stitch a seam, or empty a pocket faster than most people can follow.

Nobody trusts a ratkin on sight. That's the reality, and most ratkin stopped being hurt by it a long time ago. Merchants watch them, guards move them along, and polite company pretends they aren't there. In the underworld they're valued — good ratkin informants and couriers are worth their weight in coin. Above ground, earning trust takes time and proof. Ratkin who've done it tend to inspire fierce loyalty precisely because everyone knows what it cost them to be taken seriously.
Ratkin.quirk: Ratkin are immediately recognized and widely distrusted; treated as thieves or vermin until they prove otherwise.
abilities: Bolt Hole
Iron Stomach

Lizardfolk.name: Lizardfolk
Lizardfolk.description:
Older than the elves and unimpressed by the fact. Lizardfolk have lived in the marshes, rivers, and coastal shallows of Larion since before anyone started writing things down, and they regard the rise and fall of kingdoms with the polite disinterest of someone watching weather pass. They're not primitive — they build, trade, worship, and wage war — they just don't see the point of doing any of it faster than necessary. A lizardfolk hunts when hungry, fights when threatened, and rests without guilt. Other races call this lazy. Lizardfolk call it sane.

Broad and solid, standing five and a half to six and a half feet tall with dense, scaled hide in shades of green, brown, slate-grey, or muddy gold. Blunt snouts, heavy jaws, and eyes set wide on the skull — amber, yellow, or dark brown with horizontal pupils. Thick tails used for balance and swimming. Clawed hands and feet. Their faces are largely unreadable to mammalian eyes — the muscles aren't built for the same expressions, and most lizardfolk don't bother learning to fake them.

Lizardfolk confuse people. They're not hostile, not friendly, not rude, not polite — they're just there, watching with those flat unblinking eyes, saying exactly what they mean in as few words as possible. Most folk find the honesty refreshing for about ten minutes and then deeply unsettling. Lizardfolk don't do small talk, don't feign interest, and won't laugh at a joke they don't find funny. In the Great Lagoon and Ntar Vanna this is understood. Elsewhere, people assume they're either stupid or angry. They're usually neither.
Lizardfolk.quirk: Reptilian features and flat affect make social reading difficult for both sides; strangers often misinterpret lizardfolk silence as hostility or slowness.
abilities: Cold Blood
Thicken Hide

Bard.name: Bard
Bard.description:
Main Attribute: Charisma

A good bard can talk their way into a fortress, sing a bar into a riot, and leave town with secrets worth more than gold — all in the same night. Bards are performers, yes, but performance is just one of their talents. Their magic runs through music, voice, and story, bending emotions and perceptions the way a wizard bends fire. A bard who's really working doesn't look like a caster. They look like the most interesting person in the room. That's the point.

Bards go everywhere and know everyone. Taverns, courts, military camps, criminal hideouts — anywhere people gather, a performer is welcome and a good one is celebrated. That access is the real weapon. Bards collect rumors, favors, and alliances the way other adventurers collect loot. They fight with wit and illusion, and when that fails, whatever's handy. Underestimate them once. You won't get the chance to do it twice.

Special Starting Gear: Maple Lute
Bard.quirk: Performers are welcome in most social spaces; a bard can often find an audience, a meal, and loose talk wherever people gather
abilities: Cutting Verse

Blood Dancer.name: Blood Dancer
Blood Dancer.description:
Main Attributes: Constitution and Dexterity

Blood dancers are blade-wielding acrobatic fighters who fuel their combat with blood magic — their own and their enemies'. A slice across the palm to ignite a burst of speed. A blade drawn across a wounded foe to drain strength back into themselves. They move fast, strike from angles, and spend vitality to power abilities that pure martial skill can't match. It's an old tradition, rooted in warrior-cults that treated combat as ritual and ritual as combat.

The scarification isn't decoration — each mark is a channel, a way to let power in faster when the blood starts flowing. A blood dancer in motion is hard to pin down: acrobatic, relentless, always a half-step ahead of where you're swinging. The style is spectacular and unsettling in equal measure. Most people have never seen a blood dancer fight. Those who have tend to remember it vividly — and keep their distance afterward.

Special Starting Gear: Leather Jerkin (+ 50 Armor)
Blood Dancer.quirk: Blood dancers' ritual scars and fighting style mark them visibly. Those who recognize blood magic may react with fascination or unease; in conservative or religious communities, open practice may draw suspicion
abilities: Crimson Step

Druid.name: Druid
Druid.description:
Main Attribute: Wisdom

Druids speak to the wild and the wild speaks back. Not in words — in growth, in rot, in the way a storm turns or a wolf pack splits. Druidic magic is old. Older than temples, older than academies, older than the languages people use to talk about magic. It comes from the living world itself: root and river, fang and feather. A druid doesn't study spells. They listen until the land trusts them enough to lend its power.

That power is vast and strange. A druid can call thorns from bare stone, take the shape of a bear or a hawk, speak with beasts as easily as people, and sense corruption in the land the way you'd smell smoke. They tend sacred groves, protect wild places from things that would poison them, and walk paths that haven't seen a human foot in centuries. Druids make poor city dwellers. But when something ancient stirs in the deep forest, or a blight creeps across the farmland, or the wolves come down from the hills in numbers that don't make sense — a druid is the one who knows why, and the one who can make it stop.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor).
Druid.quirk: Animals tend to be calm and curious around a druid rather than fearful; rural folk and forest communities may seek a druid's counsel on matters of weather, blight, or strange happenings in the wild.
abilities: Thorncall

Fighter.name: Fighter
Fighter.description:
Main Attribute: Strength

A sword, an axe, a spear: dangerous in anyone's hands, but deadly in the right ones. Fighters are the right ones. They're the people who put in the years of drill and sparring and getting knocked down until the weapon moves like a part of them. Academy-trained duelists, mercenary veterans, pit champions, village kids who picked up a blade and never put it down — the path matters, but the destination is the same: someone you want on your side when steel comes out.

Every army, guard company, and mercenary outfit in Larion runs on fighters. So does every caravan, every dungeon expedition, and every noble household that takes security seriously. Say what you like about swords: they never run dry, never misfire, and never ask for anything back. In a world full of things that want to kill you, the person who can put a weapon to work every time, under pressure, without fail — that's someone worth having on your side.

Special Starting Gear: Iron Breastplate (+ 100 Armor)
Fighter.quirk: Occasionally, an experienced warrior or soldier may recognize a fellow trained fighter by how they carry themselves
abilities: Hold The Line

Hammerdwarf.name: Hammerdwarf
Hammerdwarf.description:
Main Attributes: Strength and Constitution

Hammerdwarves are armored warriors who fight the way dwarves build — heavy, precise, and methodical. They know how things are put together, which makes them very good at taking things apart.
abilities: Dwarven Brace

Monk.name: Monk
Monk.description:
Main Attributes: Wisdom, Dexterity, and Strength

Monks train until the difference between body and spirit stops mattering. Fists that crack stone. Feet that barely touch the ground. A stillness in combat that looks like calm and hits like thunder. Monastic orders across Larion teach different paths, some through meditation and prayer, some through pain and endurance, some through movement drilled so deep it becomes instinct, but the result is the same: a person who doesn't need a weapon because they already are one.

Monks fight without armor and without fear, and both choices are deliberate. Speed is their shield. Discipline is their edge. A trained monk can deflect a sword with an open hand, close a gap faster than a fighter can draw, and strike pressure points that drop opponents twice their size. Off the battlefield, monks tend toward discipline and restraint, not because they're serene by nature, but because they've learned what happens when they aren't. The power they carry demands control. The ones worth fearing are the ones who make it look effortless.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor)
Monk.quirk: A monk's physical control and unusual fighting style may draw curiosity or recognition; monastic orders and martial artists may acknowledge a fellow practitioner.
abilities: Ki Strike

Paladin.name: Paladin
Paladin.description:
Main Attributes: Wisdom, Strength, and Charisma

Paladins fight for something greater than themselves. They swear sacred oaths — to a god, a temple, a cause — and divine power flows back into their blade, their voice, their presence. A paladin holding the line against the dead isn't just a fighter with a holy symbol. They're a wall of conviction that the dead break against.

Not all paladins serve the same creed, and not all of them are gentle about it. Some are temple knights who protect pilgrims and heal the sick. Others are zealots who hunt demons and heretics with equal fervor. What they share is belief. A paladin who doesn't believe is just a fighter with a prayer book. A paladin who believes? That's the one undead flee from, demons bargain with, and soldiers follow into places they'd never go alone.

Special Starting Gear: Iron Breastplate (+ 100 Armor)
Paladin.quirk: The faithful may recognize a paladin's divine calling; temples and religious communities tend to offer respect and hospitality, though they may also request service
abilities: Oath Strike

Ranger.name: Ranger
Ranger.description:
Main Attributes: Dexterity and Constitution

Rangers are at home in places where the only law is what can catch you and what you can catch first. They're hunters, trackers, scouts, and wardens — the people who know which prints mean wolves and which mean something worse, who can sleep in a tree and wake up dry, who talk to their hawk like it understands and maybe it does. Civilization trains fighters. The wilderness trains rangers.

Every frontier settlement, logging camp, and trade caravan knows the value of a good ranger. They're the early warning when something moves in the forest that shouldn't be there, the guide who gets you through the mountain pass alive, the quiet figure at the treeline who put an arrow through the warg before you knew it was there. Rangers don't tend to boast. They don't need to. The people who've been saved by one remember. The things that have been hunted by one remember too.

Special Starting Gear: Leather Jerkin (+ 50 Armor)
Ranger.quirk: Frontier folk, hunters, and woodsmen tend to recognize a ranger as one of their own and may share news of the wilds.
abilities: Ranger's Mark

Rogue.name: Rogue
Rogue.description:
Main Attribute: Dexterity

Everything and everyone has a weak point. A lock to pick, a throat to cut, a shadow nobody's watching. Rogues find it. Some learned in thieves' guilds, some in back alleys, some from a parent who learned the same way. Not all rogues are thieves — plenty are spies, assassins, or the person a crime boss sends when a problem needs to disappear quietly. But all of them share a talent for being where they shouldn't be, doing what they shouldn't do, and getting out clean.

Rogues don't fight fair. They fight first, fight dirty, and fight from angles you weren't covering. A rogue in a straight duel is a rogue who made a mistake — but a rogue in the shadows, behind you, with a blade already drawn? That's a rogue doing their job. Quick hands, quick feet, and a mind that thinks in shortcuts. In Larion's cities, that skillset is always in demand. In its dungeons, it's the difference between walking into a trap and walking past it.

Special Starting Gear: Leather Jerkin (+ 50 Armor)
Rogue.quirk: In settlements, the rogue may notice — and be noticed by — the local underworld: a fence's mark on a doorpost, a thieves' cant phrase, a knowing look from someone in the trade
abilities: Cheap Shot

Warlock.name: Warlock
Warlock.description:
Main Attributes: Intelligence and Charisma

Most mages spend years earning what a warlock gets in a single moment. Somewhere in their past — an unexpected discovery, a long-lost ritual, a voice that answered when no one should have been listening — they struck a bargain with something from beyond the mortal world. A demon, a fey lord, a thing with no name that lives between the planes. The pact took hold, and the power came flooding in.

What other casters build through decades of study, a warlock channels through their bond — conjuration, curses, blasts of eldritch force. But the bond pulls both ways. The source of their magic — their patron — is out there. Sooner or later it will want something. That's the bargain they've struck. Every warlock knows it. The good ones learn to stay one step ahead.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor)
Warlock.quirk: Bound to an extraplanar patron whose presence occasionally surfaces. Other magic-users may sense the pact
abilities: Eldritch Blast

Wizard.name: Wizard
Wizard.description:
Main Attribute: Intelligence

Wizards earn every scrap of power they wield. No bloodline gift, no patron's bargain — just years of study, practice, and the stubborn conviction that the fundamental forces of reality can be understood, harnessed, and aimed at something. Wizardry is the hardest school of magic in Larion. The dropout rate is brutal. The ones who survive it and master it can set the air on fire, stop a heart with a word, or open a door to somewhere that shouldn't exist.

A wizard's spellbook is their life's work — formulas, diagrams, notes in three languages, corrections in a fourth. Every spell is a problem they solved. Every new one is a problem they haven't solved yet, and that's what keeps them going. Wizards are found in academies, towers, courts, and dusty rooms full of books they haven't finished reading. The best ones are also found on the road, because theory only gets you so far. Sooner or later, you have to test it against something that's trying to kill you.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor)
Wizard.quirk: Wizards may be recognized by their manner and tools of the trade; academies, libraries, and magic schools tend to treat a fellow practitioner as a peer.
abilities: Scholar's Bolt

Tinker.name: Tinker
Tinker.description:
Main Attributes: Intelligence and Dexterity

Tinkers build things that shouldn't work — and then they do. Their signature is the construct companion: a small clockwork creature, hand-sized to dog-sized, animated through mechanical ingenuity and enchantment. Every tinker's construct is different because every tinker solves the problem differently. One might be a brass beetle that scouts ahead. Another might be a wooden fox with knife-blade teeth. A third might be a copper spider that pins enemies in wire. The tinker built it, the tinker maintains it, and in a fight, the tinker and their construct work as a team.

Beyond the construct, tinkers are gadget-makers, trap-setters, and problem-solvers. They enchant ordinary objects to do extraordinary things, brew alchemical concoctions from whatever's on hand, and rig devices that can lock down a corridor or blow open a wall. In combat, a tinker doesn't wade in swinging — they set the board. A trap here, a gadget there, the construct flanking while the tinker stays one step ahead. Off the battlefield, they're the person who can fix the wagon, pick apart a magical lock, and tell you exactly why that ancient mechanism stopped working. Everything a tinker makes is handmade, one-of-a-kind, and slightly unreliable. They wouldn't have it any other way.

Special Starting Gear: Artificer's Toolkit, Leather Jerkin (+ 50 Armor).
Tinker.quirk: People, especially other tinkers and enchanters, may react with curiosity, wonder or fascination to a tinker's construct companion, gadgets and other magical devices.
abilities: Pocket Contraption

Sorcerer.name: Sorcerer
Sorcerer.description:
Main Attribute: Intelligence

Sorcerers are born with magic already inside them — inherited, awakened, or simply there with no explanation anyone can offer. Where wizards study for years to light a candle, a sorcerer might set a barn on fire at age six and spend the next decade learning not to do it again. The power is raw, instinctive, and bigger than the person carrying it. Training helps. Control helps. But underneath the technique, a sorcerer's magic has a will of its own, and it wants out.

That rawness is both the gift and the problem. A sorcerer at full tilt can match any wizard alive — conjuration, elemental destruction, force that bends the air around it. But wizard magic is precise. Sorcerer magic is a flood behind a dam, and the dam is the caster's concentration. When it works, it's spectacular. When it slips, everyone nearby knows it. Sorcerers tend to be intense people. They don't have much choice.

Special Starting Gear: Linen Robe (+ 25 Armor)
Sorcerer.quirk: A sorcerer's innate, wild magic may occasionally surface in small, involuntary ways
abilities: Wild Bolt

Vampire.name: Vampire
Vampire.description: You have become a vampire, gaining supernatural power at the cost of a bestial hunger for blood and vulnerability to sunlight.
Vampire.quirk: Possesses dark sight and blood sense. Extremely vulnerable to sunlight and weak by holy symbols. Must regularly consume fresh blood or grow weak and lose self-control.

Lycanthrope.name: Lycanthrope
Lycanthrope.description: You have been cursed with the affliction of the werewolf. Under the full moon, you transform into a powerful beast, gaining supernatural strength and senses at the cost of feral rage and loss of self-control.
Lycanthrope.quirk: Can transform into a werewolf, gaining enhanced strength, speed, and senses. Heightened senses of smell and hearing. Vulnerable to silver weapons.

Fire Magic.name: Fire Magic
Fire Magic.description: You can call fire from your hands and bend it where you want it. A spark, a bolt, a wall of flame. (Intelligence)
abilities: Spark Cast

Air Magic.name: Air Magic
Air Magic.description: Wind answers when you call it. A gust to knock someone off their feet, a cushion to slow a fall, a crack of thunder from a clear sky. (Intelligence)
abilities: Static Snap

Water Magic.name: Water Magic
Water Magic.description: You can pull water from the air, shape it, freeze it, drive it forward like a fist. Where there's moisture, you have a weapon. (Intelligence)
abilities: Rushing Water

Earth Magic.name: Earth Magic
Earth Magic.description: Stone listens to you. You can crack rock, raise walls from bare ground, and make the earth move under someone's feet. (Intelligence)
abilities: Stone Fist

Divine Magic.name: Divine Magic
Divine Magic.description: You can channel holy power — a smite that burns the unholy, a ward that holds evil at bay, a blessing that strengthens the faithful. (Wisdom)
abilities: Sacred Word

Dark Magic.name: Dark Magic
Dark Magic.description: You've learned to wield shadow, decay, and worse. It's dangerous, it's forbidden in most places, and it works. (Intelligence)
abilities: Umbral Lash

Illusion Magic.name: Illusion Magic
Illusion Magic.description: You can make people see things that aren't there and miss things that are. A useful trick that scales as far as your imagination does. (Charisma)
abilities: Splinter Image

Necromancy.name: Necromancy
Necromancy.description: You can speak to the dead and, when needed, make them get back up. Most people find this unsettling. The dead don't seem to mind. (Intelligence)
abilities: Grave Touch

Conjuration.name: Conjuration
Conjuration.description: You can reach across the veil and pull something through — a servant, a beast, a thing that follows your commands. What comes depends on what you call. (Intelligence)
abilities: Lesser Servant

Divination.name: Divination
Divination.description: You can see what others can't — glimpses of what's coming, traces of what's hidden, the thread connecting a question to its answer. (Wisdom)
abilities: Read The Pattern

Nature Magic.name: Nature Magic
Nature Magic.description: You can coax a seed to sprout in seconds, command roots to bind, and call on the green world to fight for you. (Wisdom)
abilities: Thorn Lash

Blood Magic.name: Blood Magic
Blood Magic.description: You can turn living blood into power — your own or someone else's. It costs something every time. It's always worth it in the moment. (Constitution)
abilities: Blood Siphon

Healing Magic.name: Healing Magic
Healing Magic.description: You can close wounds, purge poison, and mend what's broken. In a world full of things that hurt people, you're the one who puts them back together. (Wisdom)
abilities: Healing Touch

Enchanting.name: Enchanting
Enchanting.description: You can pour magic into an object and make it hold. A blade that glows hot, a lock that won't open, a shield that turns spells. (Intelligence)
abilities: Bright Edge

Tactics.name: Tactics
Tactics.description: You read a fight before it starts — who's exposed, where the line will break, when to press. You make everyone around you more dangerous. (Intelligence)

Medicine.name: Medicine
Medicine.description: You know how to stop bleeding, set bones, and brew a poultice from roadside plants. Steady hands, hard-won knowledge, and no magic required. (Intelligence)

Investigation.name: Investigation
Investigation.description: You notice what other people walk past — the scratches on the lock, the footprint under the window, the thing that doesn't belong. (Intelligence)

Animal Handling.name: Animal Handling
Animal Handling.description: Animals trust you. You can calm a spooked horse, read a dog's warning, and earn the loyalty of creatures most people can't get near. (Wisdom)

Riding.name: Riding
Riding.description: You're at home in the saddle — riding hard, fighting mounted, and handling beasts that throw anyone else. (Constitution)

Warhammer.name: Warhammer
Warhammer.description:
Two hands, a long haft, and an iron head that turns plate armor into a coffin. Warhammers are specialist weapons for people who like to solve problems in one swing.

Starting Weapon: Iron Warhammer (Damage type: impact).
Attribute: Strength.
abilities: Armor Bell

Criminal.name: Criminal
Criminal.description: You grew up on the wrong side of the law — picking pockets, running jobs, learning which alleys go through and which ones are traps. You know how the underworld works because you worked in it.
Criminal.quirk: Known in criminal circles; fences, thieves, and underworld figures may recognize a fellow operator.

Soldier.name: Soldier
Soldier.description: You did a stint in a company, a garrison, or a lord's retinue. Long enough to learn camp discipline, formation drill, and how to sleep in the rain. Short enough that you're nobody's sergeant.

Outlaw.name: Outlaw
Outlaw.description: You lived outside the law and outside the walls — in the hills, the forest, the places where the king's writ doesn't reach. Banditry, smuggling, poaching, whatever kept you fed. You know how to survive and how to make people do what you want when asking nicely isn't an option.

Herbalist.name: Herbalist
Herbalist.description: You learned which plants close wounds and which ones stop hearts. Village healer, roadside apothecary, hedge-doctor — whatever the title, people came to you when they were hurt and the nearest temple was three days' ride away.

Hunter.name: Hunter
Hunter.description: You fed your family from the forest. Tracks, traps, patience, and a clean kill. You know animal behavior the way a merchant knows prices — instinct backed by experience.

Acolyte.name: Acolyte
Acolyte.description: You were raised in a temple or monastery. Maybe you believed, maybe you just needed a roof. Either way, you learned to read, studied history and scripture, and came out knowing more about the world than most people who've actually seen it.

Entertainer.name: Entertainer
Entertainer.description: You've worked stages, tavern floors, and street corners. Singing, juggling, acting, telling stories — whatever held the crowd. You know how to walk into a room full of strangers and leave with their attention, their coin, or both.

Mark of Rhadan.name: Mark of Rhadan
Mark of Rhadan.description: You are a sworn Shadow of the Tower of Shadow in Ardan's Watch, counted among the order that hunts the supernatural threats Larion would rather not know about. The Mark of Rhadan is the sign of that bond: it ties you to the country of the dead, lets you feel the Shadelands pressing through into the living world, and marks you to other Shadows as one of their own.
Mark of Rhadan.quirk: The player is a sworn Shadow of the Tower of Shadow, able to sense where the dead and the Shadelands press into the living world. Other Shadows recognize them as one of their own; the Tower's inner doors are open to them, and the work of hunting hauntings, the undead, and worse is theirs.

Shade-Bonded.name: Shade-Bonded
Shade-Bonded.description: Through the Pit at the heart of the Tower of Shadow, you have been bonded to a Shade — a spirit drawn from the Shadelands, the grey country of the dead. It stands at your side as your second and your shield: a figure almost a person, the shape right but the details wrong, like a reflection in dark water. No mortal survives the work the Shadows do without one. The bond is permanent, and through it you feel the dead and the Shadelands pressing into the living world.
Shade-Bonded.quirk: The player is bonded to a Shade, a spirit drawn from the Shadelands that fights at their side and shields them from what the dead and demons do to mind and soul. It appears as a figure almost human, its details wrong like a reflection in dark water. Through the bond the player can feel where the dead and the Shadelands press into the living world.
abilities: Shade-Sight

Path of the Blade.name: Path of the Blade
Path of the Blade.description: You are trained in serious weapon combat. This path covers soldiers, duelists, guards, mercenaries, knights, pit fighters, and anyone who has learned to stay alive when steel comes out.
Path of the Blade.quirk: Weapon trainers, soldiers, guards, and sellswords may recognize the stance and habits of someone trained on the Path of the Blade.

Path of the Bow.name: Path of the Bow
Path of the Bow.description: You are trained to fight at range with patience, sight, and steady hands. This path covers hunters, scouts, battlefield archers, skirmishers, roof marksmen, and monster hunters who prefer to strike before danger closes.
Path of the Bow.quirk: Hunters, scouts, fletchers, and veteran guards may notice the way you judge distance, cover, and lines of fire.

Path of the Cloak.name: Path of the Cloak
Path of the Cloak.description:
You are trained in quiet work: moving unseen, opening what is locked, scouting danger, following without being seen, reading exits, handling secrets, and wearing a false face when needed. This path covers thieves, spies, scouts, infiltrators, trap-breakers, rebel couriers, noble agents, guild hands, and careful survivors.

To become Cloakwise, find a suitable contact or patron, take a small quiet job, return with proof, and show that you can work with discretion.
Path of the Cloak.quirk: Scouts, guards, fixers, spies, criminals, handlers, and sharp-eyed travelers may recognize the habits of someone used to watching exits and keeping their hands free.

Cloakwise.name: Cloakwise
Cloakwise.description:
You have shown practical competence in quiet work. You are not merely sneaky. You understand how to move, watch, open, follow, listen, retrieve, and leave without turning every problem into a fight.

To become Vouched, complete quiet work cleanly enough that a credible contact, scoutmaster, fixer, handler, League officer, rebel agent, noble agent, guild contact, or underworld patron is willing to put their name behind you.
Cloakwise.quirk: Contacts, scouts, handlers, guards, and underworld people may treat you as someone who knows the practical rules of quiet work.

Vouched.name: Vouched
Vouched.description:
Someone credible has put their word behind you. In the quiet trades, that matters more than boasting. A contact, handler, officer, patron, or guild name now says you can be trusted with work that needs proof and discretion.

To become Ghost-Hand, complete dangerous quiet work: infiltrate a guarded place, recover something without open violence, expose a secret, smuggle someone out, sabotage a threat, map a dangerous route, or bypass traps without raising alarm.
Vouched.quirk: Fixers, scouts, spies, guild contacts, noble agents, and underworld patrons may take you more seriously because someone credible has vouched for you.

Ghost-Hand.name: Ghost-Hand
Ghost-Hand.description:
You are trusted with dangerous quiet work. You can get in, get out, and leave less behind than others. People who deal in secrets may bring you jobs they would not risk on an ordinary sneak.

To become Master of the Cloak, complete high-stakes quiet work that serious powers thought impossible, then be named, promoted, hired, sought out, or openly recognized as someone who can handle impossible entries, exits, secrets, and recoveries.
Ghost-Hand.quirk: Handlers, guilds, rebel cells, noble agents, League specialists, and underworld patrons may seek you out for guarded places, missing things, hidden routes, and secrets no one else can reach.

Master of the Cloak.name: Master of the Cloak
Master of the Cloak.description:
You are known as someone who can handle impossible entries, exits, secrets, and recoveries. When a door cannot be opened, a route cannot be crossed, a prisoner cannot be reached, or a truth cannot be found, your name may be spoken as the answer.

This is the highest core Cloak milestone. Further growth should come through specific branches, contacts, guild standing, titles, tools, safehouses, companions, secrets held, or places where your name opens hidden doors.
Master of the Cloak.quirk: Serious powers may seek your help for quiet work that cannot survive noise: impossible infiltrations, guarded rescues, dangerous secrets, and exits no one else can see.

Path of the Vessel.name: Path of the Vessel
Path of the Vessel.description:
You are learning to channel magic through your body and will. In Larion, the vessel is the capacity of body and will that lets someone safely carry magic. This path covers studied mages, bloodline casters, pact-bound spellworkers, hedge witches, temple miracle-workers, druids, clerics, and anyone whose power depends on deepening the vessel.

Those on the Path of the Vessel grow through study, meditation, careful channeling, surviving overdraw, learning recovery, working under pressure, and being examined or guided by mages, healers, spirits, shrines, groves, towers, patrons, or power sites.
Path of the Vessel.quirk: Mages, healers, enchanters, and spirit-wise folk may sense the strain and care of someone who has begun training their vessel.

Opening Vessel.name: Opening Vessel
Opening Vessel.description:
Your vessel has begun to stir. Magic has started answering when you reach for it, but your vessel is not fully open yet.

To become an Opened Vessel, complete the opening through learning, training, meditation, practice, guided channeling, examination, ritual, or help from a mage, healer, enchanter, priest, druid, spirit, patron, shrine, tower, grove, ley site, or other magical authority.
Opening Vessel.quirk: Mages, healers, enchanters, and spirit-wise folk may notice the first signs of a vessel beginning to open: focus, fatigue, strain, and small changes in how magic gathers around you.

Opened Vessel.name: Opened Vessel
Opened Vessel.description:
Your vessel has opened. You can sense magic moving through your body and will, and you have begun learning how much power you can safely hold.

To become a Deepened Vessel, deepen your vessel through serious magical training, meditation, guided channeling, careful recovery after overdraw, surviving magical strain, completing a demanding lesson, or being told by a magical teacher that your vessel can safely hold more power than before.
Opened Vessel.quirk: Mages, healers, enchanters, and spirit-wise folk may notice the early signs of a trained vessel in how you focus, tire, and recover after magic.

Deepened Vessel.name: Deepened Vessel
Deepened Vessel.description:
Your vessel has deepened. You can safely carry more magic than a novice, though overreach still leaves you hollow, shaking, or dangerously spent.

To become a Steady Vessel, hold magic under pressure, stabilize after strain, shape magic carefully instead of forcing it, resist magical backlash, complete a controlled working while afraid, wounded, opposed, hurried, or exhausted, or receive recognition for discipline and control.
Deepened Vessel.quirk: Trained casters may recognize that your magic no longer sits shallow. They may expect you to handle stronger workings, harder lessons, or more serious risks.

Steady Vessel.name: Steady Vessel
Steady Vessel.description:
Your vessel has steadied. You can hold magic under pressure, shape it with more care, and recover from strain with discipline instead of panic.

To become a Mastered Vessel, be recognized by a serious magical authority as ready for high workings, rare instruction, large magic, complex rituals, opposed magic, dangerous bargains, or hard magical responsibility.
Steady Vessel.quirk: Mages, priests, druids, and warlocks may trust you with magic that requires control, patience, or a clear head under pressure.

Mastered Vessel.name: Mastered Vessel
Mastered Vessel.description:
Your vessel is strong, disciplined, and known to serious casters. You can bear large, complex, or opposed magic without letting it tear through you unchecked.

This is the highest core Vessel milestone. Further growth should come through specific branches, schools, patrons, rituals, relics, magical orders, power sites, hard bargains, or rare instruction.
Mastered Vessel.quirk: Magical orders, powerful spirits, temple authorities, and dangerous patrons may treat you as someone ready for high workings, hard bargains, and rare instruction.

Path of the Oath.name: Path of the Oath
Path of the Oath.description:
You are bound to a vow, order, god, cause, or sacred duty. This path covers temple guardians, sworn healers, paladins, exorcists, holy knights, and grim protectors who draw strength from what they have promised.

To become Oathsworn, swear, renew, accept, or dedicate yourself to a solemn vow before a sacred witness: a temple, shrine, chapel, holy order, grave, community, god, saint, spirit, or power.
Path of the Oath.quirk: Priests, knights, pilgrims, and oath-bound orders may treat your promises as weightier than ordinary words.

Oathsworn.name: Oathsworn
Oathsworn.description:
You have made or renewed a vow before a sacred witness, sworn order, community, shrine, temple, grave, or power. Your word has weight now, and breaking it should matter.

To become Oath-Proven, uphold your vow under pressure, then report, pray over, present proof of, ask judgment about, or receive recognition for the deed from someone or something that matters.
Oathsworn.quirk: Priests, paladins, pilgrims, judges, and common folk may treat your promises as more serious than ordinary speech.

Oath-Proven.name: Oath-Proven
Oath-Proven.description:
Your vow has been tested by action. Someone or something that matters has recognized that you kept faith when it would have been easier to turn away.

To become a Bearer of the Oath, accept greater sacred duty from a temple, shrine, holy order, priest, paladin, village, rescued community, sacred witness, or oath-bound authority.
Oath-Proven.quirk: Oath-bound orders, temples, village leaders, and people in danger may look to you as someone whose word has already held under pressure.

Bearer of the Oath.name: Bearer of the Oath
Bearer of the Oath.description:
You are trusted with harder sacred work. Temples, orders, and communities may ask you to protect, cleanse, heal, judge, escort, or stand between ordinary people and things that should not reach them.

To become a Paragon of the Oath, complete great sacred service and be publicly blessed, titled, named, called upon, or widely recognized as a living example of your vow.
Bearer of the Oath.quirk: People who know sacred law, old vows, and temple custom may treat you as someone with recognized authority, not merely good intentions.

Paragon of the Oath.name: Paragon of the Oath
Paragon of the Oath.description:
You are known as a living example of your vow. When corruption spreads, the dead stir, fiends walk, or frightened people need someone to stand firm, your name may be spoken as an answer.

This is the highest core Oath milestone. Further growth should come through specific vows, branches, sacred duties, faction standing, relics, titles, companions, or places you are sworn to protect.
Paragon of the Oath.quirk: Temples, orders, frightened settlements, and oath-bound heroes may seek your blessing, judgment, protection, or command.

Path of the Wild.name: Path of the Wild
Path of the Wild.description: You are trained to live with the wild world rather than merely pass through it. This path covers rangers, druids, beast-speakers, wardens, herbalists, trackers, and wanderers who know that every forest, marsh, hill, and desert has rules.
Path of the Wild.quirk: Hunters, druids, trappers, herders, and borderfolk may recognize someone who knows how to read weather, tracks, and animal temper.

Path of the Song.name: Path of the Song
Path of the Song.description: You are trained to move hearts, steady allies, twist a room, carry stories, and turn performance into power. This path covers bards, heralds, court players, tavern heroes, battle-singers, and silver-tongued wanderers.
Path of the Song.quirk: Innkeepers, performers, nobles, soldiers, and regular folk may expect you to know songs, stories, gossip, and the right thing to say when a room turns tense.

Path of the Tinker.name: Path of the Tinker
Path of the Tinker.description: You are trained to solve problems with tools, craft, alchemy, small devices, traps, repairs, and clever hands. This path covers tinkers, artificers, smiths, alchemists, siege-workers, locksmiths, and practical makers.
Path of the Tinker.quirk: Smiths, engineers, alchemists, miners, and mechanics may recognize the marks of someone who thinks with their hands.

Path of the Warden.name: Path of the Warden
Path of the Warden.description: You are trained to protect people, places, roads, gates, and borders. This path covers shield-bearers, caravan guards, monster wardens, town watch veterans, oath guards, and anyone who has learned that holding ground can matter more than chasing glory.
Path of the Warden.quirk: Guards, caravan masters, villagers, and travelers may look to you when danger comes near a door, road, bridge, or campfire.

Path of the Hearth.name: Path of the Hearth
Path of the Hearth.description: You are learning to build a life, gather people, earn trust, keep a home, lead a crew, run a place, or become someone a settlement remembers. This path covers homesteaders, innkeepers, local heroes, guild factors, patrons, companions, and anyone whose power grows through belonging.
Path of the Hearth.quirk: Regular folk, companions, neighbors, and local leaders may judge you by whether you keep promises, remember names, and leave places better than you found them.

Iron Shortsword.name: Iron Shortsword
Iron Shortsword.description: A well-balanced iron blade for quick cuts in close quarters.

Wooden Arrows.name: Wooden Arrows
Wooden Arrows.description: Straight-shafted arrows fletched with feathers and tipped with iron points.

Battle Axe.name: Battle Axe
Battle Axe.description: A heavy iron war axe built for brutal two-handed cuts through armor gaps and bone.

Light Crossbow.name: Light Crossbow
Light Crossbow.description: A compact crossbow with an iron prod and simple trigger mechanism.

Crossbow Bolts.name: Crossbow Bolts
Crossbow Bolts.description: Short, thick bolts with iron points designed to punch into armor and hide.

Iron Dagger.name: Iron Dagger
Iron Dagger.description: A simple double-edged iron knife for close combat, utility work, and desperate moments.

Short Spear.name: Short Spear
Short Spear.description: A short wooden shaft tipped with leaf-shaped iron, useful one-handed or from behind a shield.

Wooden Staff.name: Wooden Staff
Wooden Staff.description: A length of seasoned hardwood reinforced with iron bands at each end.

Throwing Knife.name: Throwing Knife
Throwing Knife.description: A balanced iron blade made for accurate throws at exposed flesh and weak points.

Lockpick Set.name: Lockpick Set
Lockpick Set.description: A worn leather roll containing tension wrenches, rake picks, and hook picks.

Leather Cuirass.name: Leather Cuirass
Leather Cuirass.description: A hardened leather chest piece scarred by old blows but still reliable.

Iron Warhammer.name: Iron Warhammer
Iron Warhammer.description: A flanged iron hammer built to crush armor, bone, and brittle bodies.

Short Bow.name: Short Bow
Short Bow.description: A compact bow suited to quick draws and close-range archery.

Trick Blade.name: Trick Blade
Trick Blade.description: A concealed spring blade disguised as an innocuous object.

Loaded Dice.name: Loaded Dice
Loaded Dice.description: A pair of bone dice weighted to favor certain outcomes.

Silver Binding Chain.name: Silver Binding Chain
Silver Binding Chain.description: Enchanted silver links that resist breaking and gleam brighter near supernatural creatures.

Linen Tunic.name: Linen Tunic
Linen Tunic.description: A plain tunic of undyed linen, comfortable but not protective.

Cloth Trousers.name: Cloth Trousers
Cloth Trousers.description: Rough-spun wool breeches patched at the knees from long wear.

Worn Breeches.name: Worn Breeches
Worn Breeches.description: Once-luxurious velvet breeches, aged but still elegantly cut.

Faded Doublet.name: Faded Doublet
Faded Doublet.description: A well-tailored but aging silk doublet whose rich colors have softened with time.

Woven Robe.name: Woven Robe
Woven Robe.description: A robe of overlapping leaves and bark that shifts color to match nearby foliage.

War Bow.name: War Bow
War Bow.description: A tall laminated bow that requires strength to draw and drives arrows with force.

Long Bow.name: Long Bow
Long Bow.description: A tall laminated bow with superior range and penetrating power.

Mongo's Medium Health Potion.name: Mongo's Medium Health Potion
Mongo's Medium Health Potion.description: A vial of crimson liquid that restores vitality when consumed.

Maple Lute.name: Maple Lute
Maple Lute.description: A well-used pear-shaped lute with delicate fretwork and the marks of countless performances.

Smith's Hammer.name: Smith's Hammer
Smith's Hammer.description: A well-balanced steel hammer used for shaping hot metal on an anvil.

Woodworking Tools.name: Woodworking Tools
Woodworking Tools.description: A collection of saws, chisels, planes, and measuring tools for working wood.

Leatherworking Tools.name: Leatherworking Tools
Leatherworking Tools.description: A set of knives, awls, needles, and edge tools for cutting and stitching leather.

Sewing Kit.name: Sewing Kit
Sewing Kit.description: A compact pouch containing needles, thread, scissors, and pins for textile work.

Battle Mace.name: Battle Mace
Battle Mace.description: A heavy flanged mace with a reinforced steel head for crushing armor and bone.

Artificer's Toolkit.name: Artificer's Toolkit
Artificer's Toolkit.description: A leather satchel of fine screwdrivers, jeweler's loupes, wire, calipers, hammers, and arcane scribing tools.

Leather Jerkin.name: Leather Jerkin
Leather Jerkin.description: A vest of hardened leather that protects the torso without restricting the arms.

Linen Robe.name: Linen Robe
Linen Robe.description: A simple ankle-length robe suitable for scholars, clergy, or common folk.

Iron Breastplate.name: Iron Breastplate
Iron Breastplate.description: Hammered iron plates riveted over leather backing, heavy enough to turn serious blows.

Leather Leggings.name: Leather Leggings
Leather Leggings.description: Treated leather leggings that resist tearing on thorns and rough ground.

Wooden Round Shield.name: Wooden Round Shield
Wooden Round Shield.description: A round shield of layered wood bound with iron at the rim.

Leather Boots.name: Leather Boots
Leather Boots.description: Sturdy tanned leather boots for long journeys and rough roads.

Craftsman's Toolkit.name: Craftsman's Toolkit
Craftsman's Toolkit.description: A broad set of hammers, saws, chisels, needles, and punches for everyday craftwork.

Wool Cloak.name: Wool Cloak
Wool Cloak.description: A simple undyed wool cloak that keeps off cold wind and light rain.

Leather Gloves.name: Leather Gloves
Leather Gloves.description: Supple leather gloves that protect the hands while preserving fine control.

Iron Helm.name: Iron Helm
Iron Helm.description: A hammered iron helmet with cheek guards and a nasal bar.

Cloth Hood.name: Cloth Hood
Cloth Hood.description: A rough-spun cloth hood that protects against wind and weather, not weapons.

Iron Greaves.name: Iron Greaves
Iron Greaves.description: Iron plates strapped over leather to guard the shins and knees.

Vampire Hunter's Crossbow.name: Vampire Hunter's Crossbow
Vampire Hunter's Crossbow.description: A compact crossbow of dark wood and steel, ward-etched for quiet work against undead prey.

Silver-Tipped Bolts of Radiance.name: Silver-Tipped Bolts of Radiance
Silver-Tipped Bolts of Radiance.description: Blessed crossbow bolts with silver heads that burn undead, fiends, and shadow-bound creatures.

florins.name: florins
florins.description: The standard trade coin throughout Larion.

Herbalist's Kit.name: Herbalist's Kit
Herbalist's Kit.description: A canvas satchel of dried herbs, poultice cloth, small bottles, twine, shears, mortar, pestle, and field notes for hedge medicine and simple remedies.

Ardan's Watch.name: Ardan's Watch
Ardan's Watch.basicInfo: Ardan's Watch is a prosperous, cosmpolitan city along the banks of the Silverflow and Great North rivers. Named for the ancient hero Ardan who founded it in a former age, it stands beholden to no king or crown, governed by a Lord Mayor on behalf of a Council of Champions made up of meritorious retired adventurers. Dark elderstone towers jut from its varied, colorful skyline; these  structures, which retain some of the otherwise lost powers of their long-dead builders, have been repurposed as bases of the famous orders that protect Larion from threats mundane and otherwise. The best-known of these orders is the League of Adventurers, headquartered in the great Tower of Venture.
Ardan's Watch.hiddenInfo:
The Lord Mayor runs the city day to day; the Council of Champions sets its course.

Most of the city is ordinary homes and markets, shrines and shops, but on any street you might pass someone exotic, powerful or famous: an archmage, Champion, foreign envoy in a curtained litter. All species are common on the streets, and a pair of horns or wings isn't worth a second glance. Criers and heralds are common, announcing festivals, events, crimes, and other major news, as are street peddlars and entertainers.

The City Guard, a competent force that answers to the Council of Champions, keeps the peace in most of the city, but Old Town is run by the Thieves' Guild and its member street gangs. Across the river to the east, in derelict Muckside, no one's in charge.

The Foundations undercity is dangerous; it connects to the subterranean region of the Underworld called Grell Hollows, which runs under all Highvale. The tournament grounds called the Field of Valor lie to the west, the necropolis known as the City of Ancestors spreads to the south, and the High Road passes through bandit-ridden Gallows Wood to the north.

Ardan's Landing.description: A river dock area outside the city walls at the confluence of the Great North River and the Silverflow. Wharves throng with vessels from countless lands. Caravans queue at the gates. Pilgrims, merchants, adventurers, refugees, coming and going. Ardan's statue stands facing the city he built, his foot worn smooth by centuries of hands touching it for luck.

Thousandlights Market.description: An old colosseum, repurposed. The old walls still stand in a vast stone circle, their tiered arches built out into permanent shops and tenements three stories high. The arena floor below is a maze of stalls from every region of the Worlds. From every beam and wire hang lanterns — brass and copper and tin, perforated in stars and moons and animal shapes. Crowded at all hours; it never closes.

The Hearthfire.description: A social space at the center of Thousandlights Market around a great stone firepit lined with common benches. Food and drink are brought by servers from the stalls and taverns built into the colosseum arches. Strangers share benches; a traveler who eats here once is rarely a stranger by the time they leave.

The Way of Coin.description: The commercial spine of the city: banks, fancy shops, merchant houses. Dominated by the flamboyant merchant princes of the Syndicate, whose parties no one important misses, and who will hire adventurers for anything coin can solve.

The Roost.description: A tall stone building off the Way of Coin where the Greater Owls roost. The upper floors are theirs — perches, handlers, the apparatus of a realm-wide messenger network. The ground floor is a tavern that grew up to serve people waiting to dispatch or receive news. Messengers descend through here at all hours, and anything not marked secret gets called out on their way through. News from across Highvale passes through — harvest reports from Corwin's lands, tournament results from Halcyon, trouble on the eastern frontier near Greyhall.

Venturers' Way.description: The street where the Tower of Venture stands. Outfitters, mercenary recruiters, and inns where parties form up before riding out and stumble back to spend their pay. Notice-boards carry fresh postings. Newly-formed companies haggle over gear in the shop doorways; veterans chat outside the Drake's Head and watch the new ones go by.

The Tower of Venture.description: Headquarters of the heroic League of Adventurers. A bustling contract hall fills the ground floor — a great common room lined with colorful stained glass windows depicting famous adventurers. Mages, berserkers, shamans, warlocks and adventurers of exotic lands and crafts mill about in front of the job boards, forming parties to pursue the constant supply of opportunities for heroism, glory or just a quick florin. Chipper ratfolk scribes help adventurers get oriented, claim their pay, and get treatment and equipment. Postings rotate constantly, with jobs in the Foundations, Gallows Wood, Wolfwood, Broken Hills, Sinkwood, and volunteer calls for Blackbarrow Camp frequently posted.

The Drake's Head.description: A prestigious adventurer's tavern in a tall timbered house. A drake's skull hangs over the door. The common room is crowded at all hours, its walls lined trophies from countless hunts. Veterans swap stories from the Broken Hills, the Blackbarrow, and worse places. New arrivals looking for leads buy a round and listen.

The Yards.description: The martial quarter, a sprawl of training pitches, armorers, weaponsmiths, and the houses of mercenary and knightly orders.

The Tower of Swords.description: Home of the Blades, who hunt monsters and answer threats to settled lands. The training hall fills the ground floor — a great vaulted space ringed with trophies from past hunts, each plaque naming the Blade who brought the creature down, where Blades old and new spar at all hours. The quartermaster will outfit a Blade going out at any hour, day or night. Current deployments run from Wolfwood beast culls to the creature reports coming in from Keep Corwin's lands.

The Blacksands Arena.description: An amphitheater floored in black sand hauled up from the riverbank. A venue for matches between gladiators, traveling champions from every region of the Worlds, and adventurers with something to prove. Bouts run from first-blood duels to monster-fights. Betting on bouts is a common pastime.

The Way of Wonders.description: The magical quarter, a brightly colored row of alchemists, enchanters, and dealers in magical curiosities.

The Tower of Spells.description: Home of the Mage-Wardens, who mind magical threats and maintain the Elder portal network. The library runs the full height of the tower, shelves spiralling along the inside wall. Apprentices brew, copy, and argue in the lower halls. The portal sits on the roof, the most stable in the city. Mage-Wardens coordinate closely with the Academy of the Arcane. Reports of Pact violations, rogue warlocks, and demon cults keep both institutions busy.

The Sacred Way.description: A long terrace of temples, shrines, and holy houses — every god with a following has a place here, from the great houses of the Lord of Justice and the Shieldbearer to small shrines of foreign gods. Priests and order witnesses here receive vows, renew old ones, bless sacred service, and hear accounts from those trying to keep faith under pressure. Funeral processions for the wealthy pass along the Sacred Way toward the City of Ancestors east of the walls, stopping at the Shrine of the Grey Lady, who shepherds the souls of the worthy dead safely through the Shadelands. Dawnblade knights in white cloaks are a regular sight, stopping in the city between deployments from Whitecliff Temple.

The Two Rivers Bathhouse.description: A great domed bathhouse of vaulted halls of pale stone, mosaicked pools fed by the city's two rivers, and scented steam rooms.

The High City.description: The grandest district, built on rising ground with views across the rivers and the plains beyond. Noble houses with exotic gardens, embassies from every region of the worlds, and the Lord Mayor's Palace at the summit.

Champion's Row.description: A row of grand townhouses overlooking the rivers from the High City's edge. Each belongs to a Champion of Larion. The Temple of Champions stands at the head of the row.

The Temple of Champions.description: A vast domed chamber open to bare sky through a wide opening in the roof called the Eye of Heaven. The walls are decorated with carvings of ancient battles from the Age of Heroes. At the far end, an elderstone construct stands guard before a sealed archway — the Watchman, ten feet tall, shaped like a faceless armored knight, guarding entrance to the Council Chamber beyond.

The Council Chamber.description: A long hall with seats for council members lining both sides. Statues of former Champions line both walls. At one far end lies Ardan's tomb. Sessions of the Council of Champions are held here.

The Heartwood.description: An Elder garden of ancient trees, winding paths and pools fed by old fountains, tended by an order of druids. At its center stands a tree older than the city, known simply as the Heart Tree.

Old Town.description: Old Town is a cramped but vibrant neighborhood of narrow old tenements, warehouses, and less reputable taverns and inns along the riverfront. The member gangs of the Thieves' Guild it claim it as their turf, and the City Guard rarely ventures here. Pawnshops and fences operate openly along the alleys. Bare-knuckle pits and gambling halls draw rough crowds.

The Riverwalk.description: The Riverwalk is a long market-promenade lining the river. Stalls sell goods straight off the boats — Highland wool, Bright Kingdoms spices, Spice Isle silks; food carts sell fried river-fish and skewered meats. Buskers play for coins, fortune-tellers read palms at folding tables, and street performers draw crowds where the walk widens. Across the river, Muckside's silhouette looms.

The Trouble Brewing Tavern.description: The Trouble Brewing Tavern is a low-ceilinged, old, dark tavern with sawdust floors. The crowd skews rough — dockhands, off-duty guild runners, foreigners who don't want to answer questions. The beer is more honest than the clientele, and you can find work here that won't make it onto a League board.

Lowlamp Lane.description: Lowlamp Lane is a narrow Old Town street home to the secretive Tower of Shadow. To appease common superstition, the Lane's alchemical lamps are kept turned low so that no one casts a sharp shadow after dark, since crossing paths with another's shadow at night here is said to bring bad luck, though the Shadows themselves make light of this folk tale. At the end of the lane, behind guarded gates, stands the dark elderstone Tower. Harran's Place, a tavern frequented by Shadows, and Silver & Salt Outfitters, a shop they patronize, bookend the Lane's buildings.

Harran's Place.description: Harran's Place is a tavern on Lowlamp Lane frequented by members of the Tower of Shadow. It is bright and cozy, with low roof beams, a warm fire in the hearth, music always being played. Named for Harran, one of the greatest Shadows of all time, who vanished while putting down a rising of the dead in the City of Ancestors. In his memory, anyone who comes back from a successful mission for the Tower of Shadow drinks on his tab for the night and toasts to continuing Harran's work.

Silver & Salt Outfitters.description: Silver & Salt Outfitters is a gear shop on Lowlamp Lane, outfitter to the Shadows. Silvered shot and blades, blessed salt, holy oil, ghost-iron, binding circle chalk, alchemical binding traps — kit for work against the dead and worse. The proprietors, a pair of retired Shadows, a half-orc named Deryn and a dragonkin named Iskarel, offer advice about dealing with hauntings and undead as well as gear.

The Hall of Shadow.description: The Hall of Shadow is the main hall of the Tower of Shadow. It is off-limits to all but the members of the Tower of Shadow, the Shadows. Senior members of the Tower are present at all hours to provide mission briefings and help Shadows find party members for dangerous work. A stair winds down into the Pit of Shadow below.

The Foundations.description: The Foundations are a warren of halls and chambers that once stood on the surface but was long ago built over, becoming the city's buried foundation layer. Recluses, criminals and worse squat in its depths, and various member gangs of the Thieves' Guild make it their turf; the City Guard does not go here. The Foundations connect to the city above through basements, sewers and drainage canals, and to the subterranean realm of the Underworld below through descending tunnels and shafts.

The Pit of Shadow.description: The Pit of Shadow is a vast circular chamber at the heart of the Tower of Shadow bult around an immense shaft dropping into a bottomless abyss. The Pit is a magical gateway that connects to the otherwordly realm of the dead known as the Shadelands. A viewing area runs around the edge of the chamber, and a narrow stone outcrop juts over the edge. Members of the Tower of Shadow enter the Shadelands by jumping into the Pit, and return by materializing in a flash of light on the outcrop. The Pit can also be used to scry for information about escaped Shades, ghosts, undead, and other threats pursued by the Tower of Shadow.

Muckside.name: Muckside
Muckside.basicInfo: A condemned district across the river from Ardan's Watch proper. Old warehouses and dockhouses list at bad angles where the ground has slowly given way beneath them. Most have partially collapsed. The city wrote it off generations ago rather than shore it up. No one lives here by choice — squatters, outcasts, and gang crews claim the sturdier ruins and fight over what's left. The bridges from the city side are unofficial crossings, rotting timber and rope.
Muckside.hiddenInfo:
No Watch patrols cross the river. The district polices itself, badly. Squatter camps and a few small gangs claim the sturdier buildings — they answer to the Thieves' Guild loosely if at all. Violence is common but small-scale. Most people here just want to be left alone and will be wary but not hostile to outsiders unless provoked. Ratkin are common here — more than anywhere else in the city.

Beneath the ruins is a foundation layer called the Muckwarren, and below that, sealed plague crypts from a long-ago epidemic. Strong local taboo forbids entering the crypts — even the gangs won't go near. The bridges to Ardan's Watch are the only lifeline. Old Town's gangs treat Muckside as overflow territory.

The Dregs.description: A muddy clearing ringed by leaning warehouse shells, the closest thing Muckside has to a town square. Fire pits mark where squatter camps have staked their ground. Across the river, Ardan's Watch rises clean and lit — close enough to hear the bells.

The Still Standing.description: A squat dockhouse that earned its name by not falling down. The ground floor is a tavern — one long room, a tapped barrel, benches made from old cargo pallets. The crowd is Muckside locals: squatters, gang runners, people between worse places. Not welcoming, but not looking for trouble with strangers who buy a round.

The Junkheap.description: An open-air market in a rubble-strewn yard where sellers lay out goods on tarps and blankets. Dented armor, mismatched boots, lockpick sets, unmarked bottles. No one asks where anything came from. Prices are negotiable and so are the weights.

The Muckwarren.description: The old foundation layer beneath Muckside, reached through cracked cellar floors and half-collapsed stairwells. Low ceilings, standing water, the sound of the river filtering through the walls. Smuggler caches and fugitive camps in the drier chambers. The passage deeper — toward the plague crypts — is marked with old warnings scratched into the stone.

The Plague Crypts.description: Sealed chambers where Muckside's dead were entombed during an epidemic rather than carried across the river to the City of Ancestors. The old seals are iron-banded stone, marked with faded ward-signs. The air is dead and dry. Niches line the walls, still occupied. No one from Muckside comes here willingly.

The Hall of Skulls.description: The deepest chamber beneath Muckside. A vast crypt with a vaulted ceiling held up by pillars of stacked stone sarcophagi. The dead here are old — older than the epidemic, older than the district above.

The Revels of Ur.name: The Revels of Ur
The Revels of Ur.basicInfo: An ancient palace long buried beneath Highvale's northern hills, entered through a rift in the rock opened by a recent earthquake. Inside lie immense baroque halls said to have once hosted the revels of the demon-princes of Ur and their mortal governors. The site sprawls deep into the mountain — enormous banquet chambers, gardens, baths, throne rooms, all sealed underground for centuries and now infested by monsters and defended by ancient constructs.
The Revels of Ur.hiddenInfo: The fissure enters through what was a feast hall, not the front entrance. The palace is vast in scale - as if built for beings far larger than today's mortals - and its layout is puzzling, with halls and chambers placed without apparent logic. Golem constructs walk patrol and attack any intruders on sight. Vicious gargoyles roost among the statuary and in the high-ceilinged halls. Giant spiders have laid web-traps throughout the upper chambers. Oozes breed in the flooded sections.
