The Forest Edge.description: Where farmland meets woodland. Stone walls marking ancient field boundaries disappear into the undergrowth. A few hunting lodges and woodcutter shacks, some maintained and some abandoned, dot the clearings. The King's Road runs along the southern border.

The Winding Tracks.description: A maze of game trails and old logging roads winding through the forest. Trees press closer here, their branches interweaving overhead. Thornbushes grow thick between the trunks, forcing travelers to follow the established paths. Small clearings appear unexpectedly.

The Oak Groves.description: Where the truly ancient trees stand - massive oaks with trunks wider than a man can reach around. Their roots create natural walls and hollows. The canopy blocks most light, leaving the forest floor in perpetual twilight. Moss grows thick on everything.

The Deep Woods.description: The forest's wild heart, where the trees grow so dense that paths disappear. Rocky outcroppings break through the undergrowth. Natural springs form pools among the roots. Caves and hollows open in the hillsides.

The Circle Stones.description: Clearings scattered through the deep woods where ancient standing stones rise from the earth. The trees around these places grow in perfect circles, and the stones themselves bear worn carvings in no known script. Some circles are small, just a handful of stones. Others are vast rings that must have taken generations to raise.

The Outpost Ruins.description: The remains of a ranger outpost, its wooden palisade rotted and collapsed. Stone foundations mark where buildings once stood. A tower of fitted stone still rises three stories, its interior open to the sky. Equipment racks, sleeping quarters, and an armory can still be identified among the ruins.

Halcyon.name: Halcyon
Halcyon.basicInfo: An elegant riverside castle famous for hosting tournaments and festivals at the nearby Halcyon Festival Grounds. House Halcyon sponsors champions and entertainers, funding their training in exchange for glory and prestige. The castle's Master of Games runs combat schools for warriors and the College of Bards teaches bards.
Halcyon.hiddenInfo: House Halcyon's obsession with competition stems from cutthroat rivalry between family members who sponsor competing champions. Fixed matches, poisoned blades, and sabotaged performances are common. The Festival Grounds spread below the castle walls.

Castle Gates.description: Grand entrance with colorful banners and House Halcyon's crest. Guards in ceremonial armor hail and announce arrivals.

Great Hall.description: Elegant hall where Duke Halcyon holds court. Walls display weapons and trophies from past tournaments. Musicians and entertainers gather here seeking patronage.

College of Bards.description: Where bards learn their craft under Master Lyrian. Performance halls, music rooms, and a library of songs and stories. Training costs 200 gold but includes room and board.

Champion's Quarter.description: Training yards where the Master of Games runs combat schools. Fighters spar while trainers critique form. Equipment shops sell quality weapons and armor. Training costs 150 gold per week.

Games Guild.description: Officially: gambling den and entertainers' guild. Unofficially: recruiting grounds for spies and rogues to service House Halcyon clandestinely.

Duke's Chambers.description: Private quarters where Duke Halcyon meets with favored champions and sponsors.

Halcyon Festival Grounds.name: Halcyon Festival Grounds
Halcyon Festival Grounds.basicInfo: A sprawling fairground beside Halcyon Castle where tournaments, festivals, and competitions run year-round. The Tournament Arena hosts combat contests daily, the Performance Pavilion stages bardic competitions, and the Games District offers every gambling opportunity imaginable. Champions come here to make their names, gamblers to make their fortunes, and bards to win audiences. Prize money flows freely, as do opportunities for glory—and trouble.
Halcyon Festival Grounds.hiddenInfo: Beneath the colorful spectacle, rival family members sabotage each other's champions, Games Guild enforcers rough up cheaters (and competitors), and someone's been fixing high-stakes matches. Recently, several champions have disappeared after winning too much money. The Duke knows there may be black sheep in the family but can't act without proof.

Festival Gate.description: Main entrance bustling with merchants, performers, and crowds. Sign-up tables for various competitions stand beneath colorful awnings. Criers announce upcoming events.

Tournament Arena.description: Large wooden arena where fighters compete for prize money. Current brackets posted on boards.

Performance Pavilion.description: Open-air stage where bards compete for audiences and silver. Performances judged by crowd reaction. Winners earn money plus noble patronage.

Games District.description: Gambling tents and dice games. Games Guild enforcers keep order—their definition of order, anyway. Rumor has it skilled cheats can earn special 'guild attention' - recruitment to spy for House Halcyon.

Merchant Camps.description: Temporary stalls selling weapons, armor, performance instruments, and festival goods. Some merchants offer 'special services' for those with coin and discretion.

Champion's Rest Inn.description: Large inn catering to competitors. Private rooms available for those who can afford them.

Greyhall Fortress.name: Greyhall Fortress
Greyhall Fortress.basicInfo: A dark stone fortress overlooking the eastern approaches, seat of House Ashford and headquarters for mercenary operations along the frontier. Lord Ashford runs Greyhall like a military camp, hiring fighters, rangers, and specialists. The War College trains soldiers in battlefield tactics, while the Scout's Guild teaches wilderness survival and tracking. The Specialist's Hall trains rogues in infiltration, spycraft, and assassination
Greyhall Fortress.hiddenInfo: Lord Ashford faces troubling choices. The eastern border faces goblin, gnoll and ogre raids.

Fortress Gate.description: Entrance where mercenaries report for duty. A sergeant welcomes recruits. Notice board lists jobs.

Parade Ground.description: Open yard where soldiers drill in formation. Lord Ashford occasionally inspects troops from the reviewing stand. Strict military discipline—follow orders or face consequences.

War College.description: Where fighters learn battlefield tactics, unit coordination, and military strategy. Master Sergeant runs intensive training.

Scout's Guild.description: Rangers train in tracking, survival, and reconnaissance. Guildmaster teaches wilderness skills and border patrol tactics.

Specialist's Hall.description: Unassuming building where rogues learn infiltration, sabotage, quiet elimination..

Contract Office.description: Where Ashford's adjutant issues contracts and handles payments.  Desertion means bounty on the deserter's head.

Armory.description: Well-stocked with military-grade weapons and armor. Contract soldiers can requisition gear. Civilians can purchase at steep prices.

Mercenary Quarter.description: Barracks and taverns where off-duty mercenaries drink, gamble, and swap war stories. Rough crowd. Good place to hear about upcoming missions or join existing units.

Millbrook.name: Millbrook
Millbrook.basicInfo: A small farming village built around an old mill the Silverflow River just to the west of the Wolfwood. Millbrook serves the surrounding farms and mills grain for the region. The village is home to around two hundred souls, mostly human farmers and craftsmen, with a handful of halfling merchants and a dwarven blacksmith. Wooden houses with thatched roofs cluster around a central common, while the great millwheel turns endlessly beside the brook.
Millbrook.hiddenInfo: The mill sits on the Sweetbrook where it's deep and fast. Bodies and debris from upriver sometimes catch in the sluice gates. The wheel is old and breaks down regularly - when it stops, grain spoils and the village loses money. The millpond is deep and murky. Hunters and travelers use the road through town regularly, and not all of them are what they seem. The Wolfwood starts a few miles east - close enough that predators occasionally come out to take livestock. Ruins in the forest draw treasure seekers and trouble. Village feuds over property, marriage, and old grievances simmer beneath the surface and sometimes boil over. The village handles its own justice for minor crimes but serious matters go to Ardan's Watch.

The Weary Traveler.description: Millbrook's only tavern, a humble two-story building with a common room that serves as the village's informal meeting place. Rough wooden tables and benches fill the space around a large stone hearth. Serves simple fare and local ale to travelers and villagers alike. A narrow staircase leads to a handful of small guest rooms above.

Village Common.description: The heart of Millbrook, a grassy square surrounded by the most important buildings in the village. A stone well sits at the center, with an old oak tree providing shade. The common serves as a marketplace on fair days and a gathering place for village meetings. Wooden stocks stand near the oak tree, rarely used but maintained as a symbol of village justice.

Mill Quarter.description: The area around the great mill that gives the village its name. The massive wooden wheel turns constantly in the Sweetbrook's current, grinding grain from the surrounding farms. Miller's Lane runs alongside the brook, lined with granaries, the blacksmith's forge, and workshops. The sound of rushing water and grinding stones fills the air.

The Alderman's Residence.description: The largest house in Millbrook, a two-story stone and timber structure with glass windows and a slate roof that speaks to the alderman's prosperity. A well-tended garden surrounds the house, and a stable houses the alderman's horse and cart. The building serves as both private residence and informal village hall where official business is conducted.

Village Gates.description: The main entrance to Millbrook, where the High Road passes between two wooden watchtowers connected by a simple gate. Usually left open during daylight hours.

Marrenford.name: Marrenford
Marrenford.basicInfo: A bright and prosperous town at the great river-crossing on Highvale's western edge, where the richest farmland in the realm meets the eaves of the Everwood. Its estates grow more than grain: enchanted orchards, herb-gardens, and reagent-fields prized by alchemists across Highvale. The town is the seat of House Corwin, proud defenders of the Heartlands, whose castle Westvigil looks west over the water and whose great unfinished wall — the Greatwall — rises along the river to guard the crossings against what comes out of the Everwood. People come to Marrenford to trade, to train under the house's institutions, to swear an oath on the famous Truthstone, and to make a wish at the Mere.
Marrenford.hiddenInfo:
Marrenford is a warm, lively, hopeful town. House Corwin rules it: an old, proud, land-rich family, beloved but overextended. They defend the surrounding lands, pay bounties on the monsters the rich land breeds, and feed the realm — and out of pure noble pride they attempt all of it grandly. The house pulls in several directions at once.

The Greatwall is needed — the river is the one barrier the dark elves of the deep Everwood cannot tunnel beneath, so the crossings are where smuggling and worse must pass — but the Corwins build it grander than need or purse allows. Publicly the house keeps a proud friendship with the high elves of the Everwood. Greenmeadow, Briarton, Millbrook, and Silverleaf all look to Marrenford for protection, and each is a fire the house cannot quite put out.

Marrenmere Market.description: The heart of the town and the way in from the road, a broad market that runs right down to the edge of the Marrenmere, the sacred lake. Stalls sell grain and fruit beside rarer goods — enchanted herbs, reagents, charms. At the water's edge stands a statue of Ithunya, goddess of harvest and earned wishes, and the open ground before her is a gathering place where folk drink, dance, and make their wishes over the water. It is held lucky to wish here for a better life, after working and suffering for it.

The Elfstone Tavern.description: An ancient inn beside the Marrenmere, beloved by the whole town, warm and crowded at every hour. One whole wall of it is a single slab of pale elder stone — the Elfstone, also called the Truthstone, said to have been set here by an elf-lord in elder days to mark a pact, long built over and only lately uncovered again. It is said that an oath sworn upon it cannot be unsworn, and that the stone refuses a false one — though it has never been seen to do anything at all. Great vows have been sworn here over drink and kept, Talion Corwin's among them.

Castle Westvigil.description: The seat of House Corwin, a fine castle of pale stone standing over the water and facing west toward the Everwood. Its great hall is bright and busy — petitioners, village elders, and the comings and goings of a working noble house. A board there carries the bounties and calls for aid the house puts out. The Corwins keep an open, welcoming court, grander than their purse can really bear.

The Wallworks.description: The Greatwall where it stands half-built along the river, a long stone fortification meant to guard every viable crossing from the Everwood. Scaffolds, work-camps, and idle stretches where the coin ran out before the stone did. It is grander than any wall in Highvale, carved and towered well past what defense requires, and it is nowhere near finished. Wardens and laborers work the parts that stand.

House Westblade.description: The chapterhouse of the Blades in Marrenford, a hall built generations ago by Talion Corwin, the house's founding ancestor and a Master of Swords in his day. It trains and lodges monster-hunters, for the rich land breeds plenty of beasts to hunt. The hall was grand once and shows it, but it has fallen into disrepair for lack of funds, its glories fading while the house spends its coin elsewhere.

The Rangers' Lodge.description: A timber lodge at the town's edge, home to the wardens who patrol the river-line and the Everwood's eaves. They are the ones who actually know what comes across. They hunt the smugglers who move soulstones and other cursed dark-elven things up out of the Underworld below the Everwood, and they teach tracking, survival, and beast-lore to those who want to learn the wild.

The Grove Temple.description: A green shrine among old trees near the Mere, tended by druids who keep the health of the land and the sacred water. They understand why Marrenford's fields grow so rich, and they are quietly uneasy about how much of the good grainland the house has turned over to luxury reagent-estates. They teach the nature arts to those with the affinity.

The Wannmere Foothills.name: The Wannmere Foothills
The Wannmere Foothills.basicInfo: The Wannmere Foothills rise into jagged peaks at the northern edge of Highvale, where mountain passes lead towards the Highlands region. Fort Ravenwatch guards the main pass, a grim stone fortress built to guard Castle Wannmere—a cursed keep that looms on a distant rise across a narrow alpine valley. The foothills are sparsely populated, with only a few hardy villages clinging to the lower slopes.
The Wannmere Foothills.hiddenInfo: Fort Ravenwatch houses a rotating garrison of conscripts—criminals, debtors, and military deserters sentenced to serve. The scattered villages maintain minimal populations of shepherds and miners who avoid the upper valleys. Castle Wannmere was once a proud border fortress until something terrible occurred within its walls a generation ago. Now it stands empty but somehow alive. Fort Ravenwatch was hastily constructed to contain whatever emerges from the castle. The fort has become a dumping ground for those the realm wants forgotten. Conscripts serve six-month rotations if they live that long. The garrison commander maintains discipline through fear while trying to keep morale from complete collapse. Villages below provide minimal supplies in exchange for protection, though they trust the fort's criminals little. Strange incidents increase during winter months when fog fills the valley.

Fort Ravenwatch - South Gate.description: The main entrance to Fort Ravenwatch, with iron-bound doors and a portcullis. Adventurers and explorers welcome.

Fort Ravenwatch - Courtyard.description: A muddy square surrounded by barracks, armory, and mess hall. A gibbet stands as warning to deserters.

Fort Ravenwatch - Ramparts.description: The fort's walls overlook the foggy valley toward Castle Wannmere. Braziers provide warmth for the watch.

Fort Ravenwatch - Barracks.description: Cramped quarters where conscripts sleep in shifts. The smell of unwashed bodies and despair hangs heavy.

Fort Ravenwatch - North Tower.description: The watchtower facing Castle Wannmere directly. A looking glass is kept here for observing the cursed keep.

Fort Ravenwatch - Commander's Tower.description: Where the garrison commander maintains quarters and office. Few conscripts are summoned here for good reasons.

Fort Ravenwatch - Mess Hall.description: A low building where watery gruel and hard bread are served twice daily. The only warm room besides the commander's.

Mountain Trail.description: A narrow path winding down toward the villages below. Heavily watched to prevent desertion.

Shepherd's Crossing.description: A small village of stone houses huddled against the mountainside.

Castle Wannmere.name: Castle Wannmere
Castle Wannmere.basicInfo: Castle Wannmere stands on a rocky promontory overlooking a high mountain pass, its towers and walls still intact despite decades of abandonment. A perpetual mist clings to its base while shadows pool even at noon.
Castle Wannmere.hiddenInfo: Lord Wannmere achieved lichdom through corrupted Old Empire knowledge, but the ritual killed everyone in the castle, raising them as undead bound to his will. His wife wanders the halls as a banshee, the servants shamble as zombies, and his knights patrol as death knights. The lich lord still continues his research, sending undead to raid tombs and libraries for more forbidden knowledge. He's discovered that consuming the life force of the living can temporarily restore his human appearance and mind - for a few hours after draining someone.

The Approach.description: Mist-shrouded moorland stretching before the castle walls. The ground is boggy with treacherous pits hidden by fog. Scattered bones mark the road. Skeletal warriors patrol in groups of three. Will-o'-wisps float above the moors at night.

The Gatehouse.description: Twin towers flanking iron gates, with murder holes above and a portcullis behind. Two death knights stand guard at the gates. Six skeleton archers man the murder holes. The gate mechanism is in the left tower.

The Courtyard.description: A walled courtyard with stables on the left, barracks on the right, and the keep straight ahead. Zombie servants shamble between buildings. Four skeletal guards patrol the walls. Lady Wannmere's banshee appears in the tower window above.

The Great Hall.description: A long hall with two rows of pillars, collapsed tables, and a raised dais at the far end. Zombie servants stand along the walls. Three death knights guard the doors to the throne room. Wraiths drift through the shadows between pillars.

The Throne Room.description: A chamber with the throne on a stepped dais at the back. Lord Wannmere the lich sits on his throne. Two death knights flank him. Shelves of tomes and scrolls line the walls. A mirror behind the throne shows disturbing visions.

Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.name: Spice Isles League Chapterhouse
Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: A Gold Empire brass warship grounded on a reef in the Bay of Veils, converted into the League of Adventurers' Spice Isles headquarters. The Copperclad, as everyone calls it, is enormous—a vessel built for a civilization that thought in grander proportions than the present age. Its hull still gleams where the League polishes it. The rest has gone green with centuries of salt air. Docks and gangways connect it to the shore. The League operates from the upper decks; the lower holds are sealed, flooded, and full of things no one has catalogued.
Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The Copperclad ran aground centuries ago under circumstances no one agrees on. Gold Empire ships were built from alchemical brass that resists corrosion, which is why the hull is still structurally sound when any normal vessel would be reef-dust. It is the largest intact Gold Empire artifact in the Spice Isles and multiple factions would very much like to own it. The League's claim holds because they maintain a garrison and because removing them would mean war with the League across Larion.

The Master of the House runs the chapterhouse and is always a veteran of island operations—someone who knows the currents, the politics, and which merchant princes to trust, which is none of them. The posting is considered glamorous by League standards. Competition for it is fierce.

The upper decks are fully converted: quarters, storerooms, a forge, a contract board fed by bounties from every island and merchant house in the region. The main deck is large enough to stage a small expedition. Adventurers form up here before heading to ruins, pirate holdouts, or Gold Empire sites across the isles.

The lower holds are the Copperclad's open secret. Three decks down, seawater seeps through hull breaches and the brass corridors go dark. The League has mapped some of it. Gold Empire mechanisms still function in places—locked doors, active wards, things that hum behind walls. Official League policy forbids unauthorized exploration below the third deck. Unofficial League culture treats sneaking down there as a rite of passage.

A Greater Owl roost sits atop the main mast. The birds handle the sea winds well. A portal stone was hauled up and installed on a platform built around the crow's nest—high enough for stable passage, dramatic enough to suit the location.

Local pirates give the Copperclad a wide berth. The merchant princes of the isles treat the League as a useful neutral party and a source of deniable problem-solving.

The Gangway.description: A floating dock complex lashed to the Copperclad's hull, where small boats tie up and a broad gangway climbs to the main deck. The dock has grown over the years—planks, rope bridges, a harbormaster's shack, vendors selling provisions to departing adventurers. The hull rises above like a brass cliff, green below the waterline and gleaming above where the League keeps it polished. Barnacles and sea-growth crust the lower plates. Gulls roost on every horizontal surface the owls haven't claimed.

The Main Deck.description: The Copperclad's upper deck, open to the sky and large enough to muster a company. The League has built a wooden operations hall amidships where the contract board, quartermaster's stores, and the Master of the House hold court. The original brass deck is still underfoot—warm in the sun, etched with Gold Empire patterns. Cargo cranes adapted from the ship's original rigging haul supplies from the docks below. Fore and aft, stairs and hatches lead to the converted interior decks. A few hatches are chained shut and marked with League warnings.

The Quarterdeck.description: The stern section, once the officers' quarters of whatever Gold Empire captain sailed this thing. High ceilings by any standard—the Gold Empire built tall. Brass-framed windows look out across the bay. The League converted it into a taproom and common area. A long bar runs beneath the windows. Hammocks and bunks fill the adjacent cabins for adventurers between jobs. The walls are brass plate that someone decorated decades ago with carved wooden panels, trophy mounts, and flags taken from defeated pirates. A mounted sea-serpent skull hangs above the bar. The drink of the house is whatever came off the last prize ship.

The Crow's Nest.description: The top of the Copperclad's main mast, reached by a stair that spirals up the mast's interior—original Gold Empire construction, wide enough for two abreast. The platform at the top has been expanded with a reinforced wooden deck. The portal stone stands here, lashed down against the sea wind, connecting to the Tower of Venture and other chapterhouses. The Greater Owl roost wraps around the mast just below—a purpose-built wooden structure where the handlers keep the birds. Views stretch to the horizon in every direction. On clear days you can count islands.

Gavarra.name: Gavarra
Gavarra.basicInfo: Gavarra: City of Fortune, Stormglow Isle. A terraced island rising from the sea, crowned by towers that blaze with stormlight — ancient Gold Empire structures that draw lightning from the sea-storms and turn it to colored light. Five great Houses compete along the terraces, each an exclusive gambling hall, social club, and spectacle venue in one. Storm-ray races in the harbor, sanctioned duels on the terraces, and spectacles staged by the Houses to outdo each other. The dangerous, the powerful, and the ambitious come from across Larion to wager, deal, and play.
Gavarra.hiddenInfo:
Governed by the Masters of the five High Houses and the Master of the Tower as chair. The Houses compete fiercely for patrons and standing through spectacle, luxury, and reputation. House politics shift constantly — rivals become partners when it suits them, and today's alliance is tomorrow's betrayal. The Surety — Gavarra's enforcers — are embedded citywide, often in disguise. They protect the city's prosperity, not its people. Open violence outside sanctioned contests is crushed. Street crime against nobodies is ignored. Entry to the Houses is by chip — tokens each House mints for its patrons. Your collection of chips tells everyone how high you've climbed. The Tower's chip is the rarest on the island. All debts are honored. Those who cannot pay may enter bonded service. Gold Empire tunnels connect the tower foundations beneath the city.

Everyone in Gavarra is either running an angle or being run by someone else. Visitors are merchants, nobles, crime lords, adventurers, spies, exiles, and fortune-seekers of every species and from every corner of Larion. Custom here is to buy a stranger a drink before asking their name — it's considered crude to talk to someone who doesn't have a glass in their hand.

The Landings.description: Sea-level docks and the lowest terrace. Piers jammed with merchant vessels and island traders. Taverns, street gambling, food stalls, moneychangers. Loud, jostling, in-your-face. Sailors losing pay at dice beside adventurers fresh off the boat.

The Float.description: A raft-and-pontoon market lashed to the docks below the Landings. By day, traders sell provisions, curios, and island goods from bobbing stalls. By night the legitimate merchants pack up and the sketchy ones arrive — boats lashing to the edges to hawk exotic goods, stolen merchandise, unlicensed potions, and things that don't have names in Common.

The Slick.description: The harbor racing course. A rough oval marked by buoys and floating obstacles, with wooden stands built out over the water on pilings. Riders race tamed storm-rays — broad, flat-bodied sea creatures that skim the surface at speed. Riders crouch or stand on their backs. Contact between riders is legal and encouraged. The crowd wagers furiously. Races run most afternoons when the harbor is calm enough. Bookmakers work the stands. Would-be riders can try qualifying bouts on training rays for a fee.

The Promenade.description: Middle terraces. Broad stone walkways connecting the great Houses of chance, entertainment halls, and public venues. Gavarra's heart — where serious money moves and spectacle draws crowds. The Houses line the Promenade like rival palaces, each trying to outshine the others. Bookmakers, food vendors, and shops fill the gaps between venues.

The Long Odds.description: A bookmaking club and scrying parlor on the Promenade, owned and run by Malaimona,  a tall, striking demonborn woman usually found working the room in expensive silks and gold jewelry. The centerpiece is the Eye — a gnomish scrying system that projects scenes into the air above the central bar. Fights, races, duels, and high-stakes games from across Gavarra, spellcast live. Scrying mirrors line the walls showing secondary feeds. The crowd watches, drinks, and wagers furiously. Ratkin messengers dart between tables carrying slips and updates. A staff of gnomish wizards maintain the equipment in a back room that no one else is allowed into.

The All Inn.description: A well-kept inn on the Promenade, popular with visitors who want comfort without House prices. Dark wood, copper lamps, a warm taproom, decent kitchen, clean rooms, rooftop terrace with a view of the Tower. The crowd is the draw — players cooling off between sessions drink alongside ship captains, sell-swords, and merchant factors. Work finds people here. A drink bought for a stranger is usually an overture.

Brightlights Terrace.description: An open-air rooftop bar on the Promenade with a view of the storm-towers and the harbor. Live musicians play every night, and a bound lightning spirit provides the light — shifting color, shape, and intensity to match the music. People come to drink, dance, watch the lights, and be somewhere beautiful.

The Gilded Cage.description: A multi-story open interior ringed with balconies, built around a massive ornate cage suspended in the center of the floor. The cage hosts pit fights — armed contestants against deadly magical creatures sourced from across Larion. You can watch from anywhere in the house while drinking and gambling at the tables. Standard games of chance on every level, but the cage is what packs the room. Run by Luthaire, a moon elf dhampir who curates the matchups personally.

The Dueling Ground.description: Open terrace on the Promenade, overlooking the sea. Broad stone floor marked with a circle. Weapon racks along the walls — dulled steel for honor duels, live blades for blood duels. A judge's platform and tiered seating, always at least half full. Anyone can challenge, anyone can refuse. The crowd wagers. Skilled fighters attract attention from the Houses fast.

The Heights.description: Upper terraces. Quieter. Private salons, invitation-only Houses, walled estates where the wealthy keep residences for the season. Nobles wager land holdings over card games. Trade magnates bet shipping routes over wine. Conversations here shape Spice Isles politics. The views are extraordinary — the whole city below, the sea beyond, the archipelago on the horizon.

The Atrium.description: Ground floor of the Tower of Fortune. A Gold Empire hall — vaulted, columned, proportioned with an elegance no modern builder could match. Silk banners, soft light, princely furnishings. A small army of black-and-gold staff — attendants, porters, and enough well-built personnel standing at ease to make the point. The bursar's desk at the center, where aspiring entrants present credentials. Gracious, welcoming, completely inflexible.

The Mezzanine.description: First floor of the Tower. High ceilings, Gold Empire columns, long tables and private alcoves. Where new entrants are tested — not formally, but by exposure. The games are real, the stakes significant but not ruinous. Card games, dice, tiles, wagers on contests elsewhere in the city. Regulars, House agents, and Tower staff circulate and watch. How you play matters as much as whether you win.

The Gavarra Arena.description: A small stone amphitheater carved into the Promenade terrace, seating perhaps a thousand. Intimate enough to see sweat and hear steel. Armed combat, unarmed bouts, obstacle courses, beast fights, and spectacles that defy easy naming. The schedule changes daily. Contestants enter voluntarily and compete for purses, but the real money is in the stands. Open to all comers willing to sign the terms.

Falmastra.name: Falmastra
Falmastra.basicInfo: A small island city-state home to the thrallmages. White stone and terracotta climbing steeply from a busy harbor, gardens on every terrace, clean streets, honest markets. Falmastra is beautiful, prosperous, and extraordinarily well-ordered. The thrallmages rule openly as patrons and protectors, and the citizens regard them with genuine pride. Visitors are welcome. Trade is encouraged. The island is a functioning port with excellent facilities and fine hospitality. It is also the seat of the most feared psionicists in Larion, and everyone who sets foot here knows it.
Falmastra.hiddenInfo:
The thrallmages govern through a council of senior members. Day-to-day administration is handled by a civil authority that answers to the council. The citizens are prosperous, well-treated, and largely content — they see the thrallmages as benefactors. Crime is rare but not nonexistent. The thrallmages concern themselves with threats, contracts, and power, not the small details of city life.

Visitors are watched. Not overtly, not aggressively, but the thrallmages are aware of who arrives and why. Clients seeking contracts are received at the Consul's House. Those who come with hostile intent tend to find their business known before they state it.

Falmastra trades normally with the Spice Isles and beyond. Many merchant captains consider it one of the safer ports — no piracy, no protection rackets, no crooked harbor officials.

The White Harbor.description: A deep natural harbor ringed by white stone quays. Well-maintained, busy with merchant traffic. Warehouses, chandlers, customs offices staffed by polite and efficient officials. Visiting crews speak well of the port — fair tariffs, clean berths, no trouble. Taverns and boardinghouses line the harborfront for sailors and short-stay visitors.

The Pomella.description: The main city, climbing the island's slopes in terraces of white stone and terracotta. Markets, workshops, residences, gardens heavy with citrus and bougainvillea. A handsome central square with a fountain. The people are friendly, the food is good, the streets are clean. Visitors move freely here. It feels like a prosperous Spice Isles town that simply works better than most.

The Consul's House.description: An elegant villa on the upper edge of the Pomella, where Falmastra receives official visitors and clients. Airy rooms, shaded courtyards, attentive staff. Business is conducted here — trade negotiations, diplomatic matters, and private consultations for those seeking the thrallmages' services. The consul is a civil appointment, not a thrallmage, and handles most affairs with practiced charm.

The Masters' Quarter.description: The upper slopes of the island, where the thrallmages keep their residences. Fine villas set among gardens and old trees, quiet and unhurried. Not walled or guarded in any visible way. Visitors are not forbidden, but few come here without invitation. The views over the harbor and the open sea are extraordinary.

Allhaven.name: Allhaven
Allhaven.basicInfo: A fortified harbor city serving as haven for pirates, privateers, and mercenary companies. Ships crowd the docks while treasure hunters outfit for expeditions to sunken Gold Empire ruins. The Water Arena draws crowds betting on gladiatorial combat, and the Floating Market sells everything needed for underwater exploration. Storm mages in the lighthouse guild guide ships safely to port while captains display their wealth in hillside villas.
Allhaven.hiddenInfo: The city maintains profitable relationships with seafolk and deeper things, trading salvage rights and water-breathing magic for information about Gold Empire sites. Several captains are actually licensed privateers serving various Spice Isles city-states, making Allhaven a neutral ground for shadow wars. The Stormcallers' Guild sells weather magic to anyone who can pay, sometimes to both sides of a conflict. Orcs, half-orcs, half-elves, catkin and demonborn make up nearly half the population, with humans, halflings, and various other races comprising the rest.

The Docks.description: A sprawling harbor where ships crowd against stone piers. A monumental bridge lands at the northern edge of the harbor. Crews unload cargo while merchants hawk supplies from waterfront stalls.

Captains' Row.description: Hillside villas overlooking the harbor, each a fortified display of plundered wealth. High walls surround estates decorated with marble columns from looted temples, gilded ship figureheads, and gardens full of exotic plants. Each successful captain competes to have the grandest mansion. Guards patrol the gates.

The Water Arena.description: A massive floating platform anchored in the bay where gladiators fight for prize money and glory, whether with each other or trained sea creatures - sharks, giant octopi, and stranger things brought up from the deep. Betting booths line the edges while spectators crowd the docks around the bay.

The Floating Market.description: A market held on dozens of barges lashed together in the harbor. Famous for its water-breathing potions and magical diving equipment. Con men hawk forged Gold Empire treasure maps to the gullible. The market shifts and reorganizes constantly as barges come and go.

The Yards.description: Massive shipbuilding docks where vessels are constructed and repaired. Workers hammer planks, tar hulls, and raise masts while foremen shout orders.

The Stormcallers' Guild.description: A tall lighthouse tower converted into an academy for storm mages. The light at the top guides ships safely to harbor while students practice weather magic on the balconies below. Inside, instructors teach wind-calling, fog-summoning, and storm-reading to paying students.

The Wreck.description: A massive galleon that ran aground and now serves as Allhaven's most famous tavern. The ship is beautiful despite being unseaworthy - ornate carvings cover the hull and the captain's quarters have been converted into private meeting rooms. The gun deck serves as the main drinking hall where crews gather, the hold stores barrels of rum and wine, and the crow's nest offers views of the entire harbor.

Crescent Bay Isle.name: Crescent Bay Isle
Crescent Bay Isle.basicInfo: A sheltered island built around a warm, shallow bay where merfolk and surfacers live side by side. The merfolk herd fish in the bay's waters while the surfacers tend the coral reefs and shoreline. A village of whitewashed stone lines the crescent shore, its docks and platforms built low to the water so merfolk can haul themselves up and join the life of the town.
Crescent Bay Isle.hiddenInfo: The arrangement is old and practical — merfolk drive fish into the bay during spawning runs, surfacers maintain the reef health with druidic tending that keeps the waters rich. The village has adapted over generations: the tavern has a saltwater pool, the market stalls are at wading depth, and children from both communities grow up swimming together. Some surfacer families have intermarried with merfolk — their half-mer children are equally at home on land or in the water. The merfolk settlement in the bay is visible from shore on calm days: shell-roofed structures anchored to the reef, gardens of cultivated kelp, lit at night by lanterns that glow beneath the surface. Mersene, frogkin, and a handful of half-sea-elves round out the aquatic population. The mood is unhurried and generous. Visitors are welcomed with food, drink, and the assumption that anyone who made it here probably needs a rest. The biggest ongoing dispute is whose fish stew recipe is better — the surfacers' or the merfolk's.

The Low Docks.description: Stone docks built barely above the waterline, where fishing boats tie up alongside merfolk who rest on the warm rocks. Steps descend into the shallows so that arriving visitors wade the last few feet to shore. Merfolk lounge at the water's edge, trading gossip with surfacers mending nets on the quay.

The Anchorage.description: The village tavern, built half on shore and half over the water on sturdy pilings. The main room has a long bar and a broad saltwater pool fed by the bay, where merfolk float with drinks balanced on their chests while surfacers sit at tables along the pool's edge. On warm evenings the shutters open wide and the whole place becomes one conversation — merfolk singing from the water, surfacers joining in from the rail, frogkin croaking percussion from the rafters.

The Reefmarket.description: An open-air market where the shore meets the shallows. Surfacer stalls on the sand sell bread, fruit, and cooked food. Merfolk vendors float in waist-deep water behind stone counters, offering fresh catch, pearl jewelry, coral carvings, and kelp-woven goods. Haggling happens in a cheerful mix of Common and the merfolk trade-pidgin that locals learn as children. At midday the whole market breaks for a shared meal — long tables set at the waterline where both communities eat together.

Wildflower Island.name: Wildflower Island
Wildflower Island.basicInfo: An island of terraced hillsides where rare flowers and aromatic plants grow in profusion, cultivated by perfumer families who have worked the land for generations. Stone cottages with clay-tile roofs climb the slopes between gardens of lavender, jasmine, sea-rose, and dozens of rarer blooms. The perfumes produced here are prized across the Spice Isles and beyond.
Wildflower Island.hiddenInfo: Five or six old perfumer families tend the island's gardens, each guarding blends and growing secrets passed down through generations. These rival houses is compete at the annual Bloom Festival where the year's finest perfumes are judged, and the winning house earns bragging rights and the most lucrative contracts with Tevantian and Water-Lily merchants. The families are proud, hospitable, and will talk your ear off about perfumes if you let them. Halflings and gnomes are well-represented among the perfumers — their sharp noses make them natural talents. A few of the rarer blooms on the upper terraces have mild magical properties. Nobody makes a fuss about this. It's just what the island grows. Workers come from neighboring islands during harvest season, and the population swells with seasonal labor, traveling merchants, and buyers from the city-states.

The Landing.description: A small stone harbor where flower garlands hang from the dock posts. A cart track climbs through garden terraces toward the village above. Porters with handcarts wait to haul goods and luggage uphill for a coin.

The Village Square.description: A sun-warmed plaza shaded by old fig trees, where townsfolk gather in the evenings. Stone benches ring a central fountain. A communal oven produces bread all day. The tavern on the square's east side — The Pestle — is small, loud, and serves wine infused with local herbs.

The Stillhouse.description: The island's largest distillery, a long stone building open on one side to let the breeze through. Copper stills line the walls. The master perfumers work here during the day, surrounded by racks of drying flowers and shelves of glass bottles in every color. A shop at the front sells the island's products, from common lavender water to rare blends that cost more than a sword.

Parrot Island.name: Parrot Island
Parrot Island.basicInfo: The loudest island in the Spice Isles, home to a community of parrotfolk whose bright plumage and brighter personalities fill the air with color and noise from dawn to well past midnight. Three great flocks — the Greens, the Reds, and the Blues — roost in cliff-face dwellings and treetop platforms connected by rope bridges and ziplines. The parrotfolk are famous across the archipelago for their music, their opinions, and their absolute refusal to stop sharing both.
Parrot Island.hiddenInfo: The three flocks are extended family groups distinguished by plumage color. The rivalry between them is passionate, constant, and mostly harmless — they compete over everything from who has the best singers to whose cliff-roost has the finest view, and disputes are settled through musical contests called Squawks where each flock performs and the crowd (meaning everyone on the island, since nobody would miss it) judges by volume of applause. Greens consider themselves the wittiest. Reds claim the strongest voices. Blues insist they have the best ear for harmony. All three are wrong and right depending on who you ask, which is everyone, because they will all tell you without being asked. Parrotfolk are natural polyglots who pick up languages effortlessly and mimic accents perfectly, which makes them sought-after as translators, messengers, and entertainers across the Spice Isles. Their instruments are as colorful as their feathers — drums made from hollow driftwood, shell-harps, reed flutes, and a local invention called the screech-pipe that sounds exactly like its name suggests. Visitors are welcomed enthusiastically and talked at without pause. Silence is considered rude. If a parrotfolk stops talking to you, you've caused genuine offense. The island exports music, messenger services, and handmade instruments. It imports patience.

The Docks.description: A rickety harbor at the base of sea-cliffs. Parrotfolk in every color shout greetings, unsolicited advice, and commentary. Rope ladders and a creaking lift provide access to the cliffs.

The Stage.description: A natural amphitheater formed by a ring of tall palms on the clifftop, their crowns lashed together with rope bridges where parrotfolk perch during performances. The stage is a broad wooden platform at the center. Somebody is always performing — soloists practicing, flock choirs rehearsing for the next Squawk, young parrotfolk busking for attention. On contest nights the whole island crams into the surrounding trees and the noise carries for miles across the water.

The Market.description: A sprawling bazaar across multiple cliff platforms where the three flocks sell their crafts — hand-painted instruments, feather jewelry, dyed textiles in vivid greens, reds, and blues. Each flock's stalls cluster together and compete for customers by being louder than the neighboring section. A groundling tavern called The Earplug occupies a sheltered nook at the market's edge, run by a half-deaf dwarf who claims it's the quietest spot on the island. It is not.

Earthsky Island.name: Earthsky Island
Earthsky Island.basicInfo: A volcanic island famous for its glassworkers, who turn the mineral-rich black sand into everything from common bottles to enchanted mirrors in the forges that line its terraced shore. At dusk the islanders light thousands of handmade glass lanterns in every color, and the island becomes a second sky — a constellation of earthbound stars visible for miles across the water. Earthsky glass is traded across Larion.
Earthsky Island.hiddenInfo: The black sand beaches contain volcanic minerals that produce glass of unusual clarity and strength, and the island's glassworkers have refined techniques over centuries that no one else has matched. Common glass — bottles, windows, tableware, lanterns — is the island's bread and butter, but the real money is in the magical work. Enchanted mirrors that show true reflections (useful for detecting shapeshifters and illusions), spirit-bottles that can trap or contain ethereal beings, glass weapons that hold enchantments better than steel, scrying orbs, and stranger commissions for wealthy clients. The magical glass workshops are run by master artisans who are equal parts craftsman and mage — you can't blow a spirit-bottle if you don't understand both the glass and the binding. Frogkin make up a significant part of the workforce. Their tolerance for heat lets them work closer to the forges and for longer shifts, and some of the best masters are frogkin whose families have been glassblowers for generations. The island also has a community of fire elemental folk drawn by the volcanic forges. The Lantern Lighting at dusk is a daily ritual the whole island participates in — each household lights its own lanterns, and the tradition is that a dark house is an empty house. Visitors are given a lantern to hang outside wherever they're staying. You leave Earthsky with a lantern. Everyone does.

Black Sand Beach.description: A harbor of dark volcanic stone where the beach glitters with mineral-flecked black sand. Glass lanterns in colored clusters hang from every post and railing, unlit during the day but promising something at nightfall. The air is warm from the forges further up the shore. A glassblower's shop at the dock sells lanterns to new arrivals — tradition says you need one before you go any further.

Forge Row.description: A long stone terrace cut into the hillside where the island's forges and workshops operate in the open air. Glassblowers shape molten glass on long pipes while apprentices tend the fires. One end of the terrace handles common glass — bottles, panes, tableware. The other end is where the enchanters work, shaping mirrors, spirit-bottles, and stranger things.

Second Sky Square.description: The island's heart — a broad plaza where thousands of glass lanterns hang from iron frames, wooden arches, and lines strung between buildings. During the day it's a market and gathering place where glassworkers sell their wares and families eat together at long tables. At dusk the Lantern Lighting begins and the square transforms. Every lantern is lit by hand — no magic, just flame passed from wick to wick until the whole plaza blazes with color. The tavern on the square, The Fallen Star, is run by a fire elemental folk whose drinks are served in glass cups of her own making.

Waverider Isle.name: Waverider Isle
Waverider Isle.basicInfo: A small island with a dormant volcanic peak, famous for its boatyards and its waves. The eastern shore builds the finest small vessels in the Spice Isles. The western shore has a permanent swell — perfect rolling waves that never stop — and a waveriding culture that treats the ocean like a playground.
Waverider Isle.hiddenInfo: The boatyards produce canoes, skiffs, and small ships built from timber hardened with volcanic resin, prized across the archipelago for their speed and durability. The board-shapers who carve waveriding boards are the same families who build the boats — it's all the same craft to them, just different scales. The annual Crown Wave tournament draws competitors and spectators from across the Spice Isles and is the island's biggest event. The locals have a dozen explanations for the eternal swell. The most popular is the oldest: a sea elemental fell in love with a fire spirit who sleeps in the volcanic peak, and the waves are her endless attempt to reach the summit. The fire spirit stirs in her sleep sometimes — the volcano vents steam — and the waves surge higher for days. The islanders call the big swells love-sick days and the surfing is spectacular. Population is mixed — humans, halflings, mersene, a handful of sea elves.

The Boatyards.description: The eastern harbor where skiffs and canoes line the beach in various stages of construction. Boatbuilders work in open-sided sheds, shaping hulls and fitting rigging while buyers from the city-states inspect finished vessels. Board-shapers work here too, carving waveriding boards from the same wood with the same hands.

Big Swell Beach.description: The western shore where the eternal swell rolls in. Golden sand, warm water, perfect waves breaking one after another with no sign of stopping. Waveriders are in the water at all hours. On the sand, spectators lounge, board-shapers wax their products, and mersene sun themselves between runs.

The Sea Drift Inn.description: A driftwood tavern on the beach where the boatyards meet the surf shore. Sand floor, no walls, tables, chairs and verandas made from elegantly carved driftwood. Evenings here are the island's social life — waveriders tell lies about the waves they caught, boatbuilders argue about hull shapes, and someone always has a guitar. On love-sick days when the big swells roll in, the whole tavern turns to watch.

Heartwine Isle.name: Heartwine Isle
Heartwine Isle.basicInfo: A lush island of terraced vineyards where alchemist-vintners produce heart-wine — potions brewed in fine wine that dissolve the barriers preventing you from seeing beauty in others. People come here to fall in love, to rekindle what's faded, and to open their eyes after heartbreak. The vineyards are stunning, the wine is excellent, and the islanders are the warmest folk in the Spice Isles.
Heartwine Isle.hiddenInfo: The vintner families have worked these hills for generations, growing grapes in unusual colors — pale gold, deep rose, dusky violet — from alchemically enriched soil. The wine is excellent on its own. Heart-wine is something more: a glass shared between two people clears away fear, pride, old hurt, and stubbornness, letting them see what was always possible between them. It doesn't create feelings from nothing. It opens doors that were already there. The locals live the philosophy — generous, open, quick to welcome strangers. Heart-wine etiquette is specific: you always tell someone what's in the glass, and you never drink it alone. Plain wine flows freely. The cellars beneath the vineyards are where the real blending happens — warm stone rooms lined with oak casks where vintners combine wine and alchemy with the care of artists. Harvest season is the island's great festival — weeks of picking, pressing, feasting, dancing, and more than a few romances that started over a shared glass. The unaccompanied come during harvest when the island is full of visitors and the evenings are long. The lovesick arrive hoping for a cure, and the cure is always the same: open your eyes, there's beauty everywhere, maybe in someone you haven't noticed yet.

The Landing.description: A small stone harbor where the vineyards are visible the moment you step off the boat — jewel-colored rows climbing the hillside in every direction.

The Trellis and Vine.description: The island's tavern, its sign showing a vine curling around a wooden trellis. A stone building half-buried in actual grapevines, with a courtyard shaded by a living canopy of leaves. Long tables inside and out where vintners, farmhands, and visitors share meals and wine. The vintners bring bottles from their own cellars and argue about the vintage. Harvest season turns the courtyard into a nightly festival. The rest of the year it's just a place where good food and good company come easy.

The New Leaf.description: An inn for visitors who came alone and don't intend to stay that way. Communal dining at long tables where the host seats you next to someone you haven't met. A shared courtyard garden with sunset views and two-person benches. Rooms are simple and clean. The innkeeper has seen a thousand love stories begin over her breakfast table and still tears up at every one.

Coral City.name: Coral City
Coral City.basicInfo: An underwater city on a shallow reef, home to merfolk who welcome surface visitors. Crystal domes and coral chambers filled with air rise from the reef floor, connected by flooded coral passages. Surfacers buy waterbreathing potions at the Skiff Landing above, swim down, and find a city where life happens much as it does on the surface — except the walls are crystal and the ocean is on the other side.
Coral City.hiddenInfo: The merfolk built the air-filled domes generations ago to facilitate trade with surfacers, and the practice grew into a whole way of life. Most public activity happens in the aired spaces — markets, taverns, gathering halls. The merfolk move freely between air and water. Surfacers with potions can swim the connecting passages or stick to the domes and let the merfolk come to them. The city is bright — shallow water means sunlight filters through crystal ceilings, throwing shifting patterns across everything. At night, lanterns and actual fire light the domed spaces. The merfolk are proud and hospitable. They think Coral City is the most beautiful city in Larion and they will tell you so. Visitors are welcomed, fed well, and gently mocked for being slow swimmers. The economy runs on deep-ocean goods surfacers can't get elsewhere — pearls, coral craft, rare alchemical materials, exotic fish. Population is mostly merfolk, with mersene, frogkin, and sea elves. A few surfacers have settled permanently, taking daily potions because they fell in love with the place or someone in it.

The Skiff Landing.description: A floating wooden platform in open water, mooring posts for small boats ringing its edge. A potion-seller does brisk business from a stall hung with painted signs in six languages. First-time visitors drink their potion, peer over the edge at the crystal domes glimmering on the reef below, and jump. Experienced visitors don't bother looking — they just jump.

The Sun Dome.description: The city's largest air-filled space — a crystal dome the size of a market square where sunlight pours through the curved ceiling and the ocean is visible in every direction. Merfolk traders sell pearls, coral jewelry, and deep-ocean curiosities from stalls. Surfacers drip on the coral floor, gawking at the fish swimming past the walls. Merfolk lounge on the edges of the flooded entry pools, half in water and half out, conducting business in both directions at once.

The Hearthstone.description: A tavern in an air-filled coral chamber with a fireplace. That's the joke and the pride of the place — a real fire, burning real wood, at the bottom of the sea. The merfolk barkeep considers this the funniest thing in Larion and never tires of watching surfacers react to it. The food is grilled fish and kelp-bread cooked over the open flame. Merfolk and surfacers sit together at stone tables while the ocean presses against crystal windows. On busy nights the flooded passage outside is packed with merfolk waiting for a table, chatting in the water while the air-breathers eat inside.

Confluence.name: Confluence
Confluence.basicInfo: A floating city where ocean currents meet, made of hundreds of boats, barges, and rafts lashed together in loose clusters with channels of open water between them. The currents bring rich waters here — fish, kelp, warm calm seas — and the sea-peoples followed. Every seafolk species in Larion mixes here alongside humans and halflings who chose the sea over the land. Nobody founded it. Nobody governs it. The city has no center and no permanent structure. Vessels come and go. The whole thing shifts and rearranges daily.
Confluence.hiddenInfo: Sea-turtle folk moor their great shell-boats at the edges during migration season — slow, patient, ancient mariners whose deep-voiced stories draw crowds when they arrive. Sealion folk are loud, barrel-chested, and physical — they swim the channels between platforms rather than walk and bark when they laugh. Shark-folk are quiet, sharp-eyed, and run the best fish markets. Mersene live as much in the water beneath the city as on it. Frogkin crouch on every available surface. The population swells and shrinks with the seasons as different species migrate through. Directions are given by vessel names and neighbors — the layout changes too often for anything else. Nobody cares where you're from. The currents brought you here, same as everyone else.

The Edge.description: Wherever there's space to tie up, that's where you arrive. The outer ring of Confluence is a shifting jumble of newly arrived vessels, departing boats casting off lines, and locals shouting directions across the water. Someone will catch your rope. Someone else will tell you where to find food. A third person will try to sell you something before you've finished tying off.

The Catch.description: A broad cluster of platforms and boat-decks where the day's fishing is sold, cooked, and eaten in one sprawling open-air operation. Shark-folk gut and fillet the catch on their hulls. Frogkin fry it on coal-braziers balanced on wooden rafts. Turtle-folk serve stews from enormous shell-pots. You eat wherever there's room to sit — on a crate, a coil of rope, the edge of someone's boat. The food is the freshest in the Spice Isles because the ocean is right there between your feet.

The Raft.description: The closest thing Confluence has to a tavern — a big flat barge with a canvas awning, barrels of drink lashed to the railings, and no seating plan whatsoever. Sealion folk take up twice the space of anyone else and don't apologize for it. Mersene float in the channel alongside with drinks balanced on their chests. Turtle-folk tell long stories that everyone pretends to have heard before but listens to anyway. When the barge gets too crowded, someone lashes another boat alongside and the tavern gets bigger. When the crowd thins, it gets smaller. Like everything in Confluence, it fits whatever it needs to be.

Water-Lily Isle.name: Water-Lily Isle
Water-Lily Isle.basicInfo: A lush island whose bays and estuaries are choked with enormous water-lilies, white blooms opening wider than a man's armspan. The Republic of the White Lily spreads along its shores—a wealthy, flamboyant free city of gleaming white stone, deep harbors, and citizens who dress extravagantly and ask few questions. Within the Republic's walls, rising in white and gold above everything else, stands the Center of All Maps: the seat of the Syndicate of the Commonweal, where High Delegates make decisions that shape all of Larion. Water-Lily Isle is the most prosperous and dangerous port in the Spice Isles - and also the most fun.
Water-Lily Isle.hiddenInfo:
The Republic is nominally governed by the Council of Merchants. In practice the High Delegates of the Syndicate run everything worth running. The city knows this and has made its peace—the arrangement makes Water-Lily Isle extraordinarily prosperous, and prosperity covers many sins.

Deep within the Center, beyond the Hall of Maps, lie the Lily Gates: enchanted arches connecting to Syndicate counting houses in major capitals across Larion. Travel through them is instantaneous. Access requires either Delegate credentials or fees that would ruin a merchant. They are how the Syndicate moves information faster than any king can respond.

Old Harbor is tolerated for the same reason the Isle of Masks exists: controlled vice is manageable vice. The Syndicate knows everything that happens there. They simply prefer not to officially know.

New Harbor.description: A grand harbor ringed by white stone quays, water-lilies pressing against the walls where estuary meets sea. Ships from every corner of Larion crowd the berths—Spice Isles galleons, Syndicate flagships flying the white lily, Bright Kingdoms dhows with their painted sails. The harbor authority is impeccable: duties assessed, papers checked, berths assigned without delay or argument. Behind the quays the city rises in tiers of white stone, and above it all the gold-touched towers of the Center catch the light like a second sun.

Grand Colonnade.description: The Republic's great spine: a long arcaded boulevard running from the harbor to the Center gates, its stone arches sheltering markets, outfitters, moneychangers, and taverns. Adventurers taking Syndicate contracts mingle with foreign merchants and off-duty Grey Guards. Contract boards line the colonnade walls—postings from Factors, private commissions, bounties, escort work. The gear here is good and expensive. Rumors are cheaper and flow freely.

The Nymphaeum Tavern.description: The sign shows a water-nymph rising from white petals. Inside: cool stone, fountains, long tables. The private rooms upstairs are where arrangements get made that can't happen in the Hall of Maps.

The Strand of Worthies.description: The grand boulevard where Delegates and those who've earned the title Worthy maintain their palaces. Tall facades, private gardens visible behind ornate gates, servants in white and gold. Each house strives to outdo its neighbors in extravagance. At night the Strand glitters, music drifting from windows.

The Isle of Masks.description: A small islet in a central lagoon, reached by a narrow footbridge. Those who cross it don their masks, setting aside their cares and identities. The isle is home to a theater staging productions too scandalous for the mainland, marble bathhouses with pools fed by heated springs, gambling rooms with serious stakes, and establishments offering whatever entertainment a visitor might prefer. What happens on the Isle stays there.

The Hall of Maps.description: The Center's public face and its most spectacular room. Vaulted ceilings rise forty feet above a floor of white and black marble. Every wall is covered in enormous frescoes—maps of every land the Syndicate has charted, painted in astonishing detail, updated constantly by a staff of painters. The most accurate maps in existence. Factors receive visitors here, post contracts, manage the endless business of pan-Larion commerce. Locked doors at the hall's far end, guarded by Grey Guards in full arms, lead deeper into the Center.

Old Harbor.description: The eastern harbor silted up generations ago and the city moved west. What's left: rotting quays that take only shallow-draft skiffs, warehouses nobody officially owns, and everything the Republic prefers not to see. Dreamsmoke dens and their cheaper cousins. Stolen cargo moving through hands that don't ask questions. People who've sold their names and are living on whatever's left.

The Water-Lily Arena.description: The Arena seats thousands and the Syndicate fills it regularly—champions fighting for prize money, glory, and the favor of watching Delegates. Some fighters are professionals who've trained for this. Some are paying off debts. Some are criminals offered an alternative to execution. The crowd is well-dressed, well-informed, and bloodthirsty. Significant coin moves on the outcomes. Winning here means something in the Republic—a champion the Delegates applaud can find doors opening that were previously closed.

Tevantia.name: Tevantia
Tevantia.basicInfo: The wealthiest of the Free States, where marble palazzos line the Grand Canal and the golden dome of the Ducal Palace gleams above the harbor. Merchant families compete through trade, through champions in the great Amphitheater, and through formal duels on the city's bridge-plazas. The Tevantines are passionate folk — quick to anger, quick to embrace, proud to wear their hearts on their sleeves. They say the cold-blooded folk of other lands don't know how to live.
Tevantia.hiddenInfo: The Duke is the First Peer of Tevantia — not a king, but the foremost among the great houses, elected for life by the Electors. In peacetime the Duke mediates between the feuding merchant families and upholds the Compact between the Free States. In crisis, the Duke commands the city's defense. The great families feud constantly — spies, sabotage, mercenaries — but open violence is gauche. Disputes are settled through formal duels on the bridge-plazas, and the whole city turns out to watch. Wealthy nobles hire champions rather than risk their own skins. Humans and dwarves dominate the great houses, with dwarven banking clans controlling much of the city's gold. The common folk are as proud as their betters: boisterous, opinionated, quick to tell you exactly what they think. Social mobility defines Tevantine culture — patents of nobility can be purchased outright, impoverished houses sell adoption into their lineage, and sponsorship by established families offers a more prestigious path upward.

Tevantia Harbor.description: Where the great bridge to the island terminates in a fortified plaza overlooking the harbor. Ships from every land crowd the docks while cargo is unloaded under the watch of customs inspectors. Mercenary companies recruit openly, ship captains seek crew, and merchants hawk passage to distant ports.

The Grand Canal.description: The main waterway cutting through Tevantia's heart, lined with marble palazzos and crossed by ornate bridges. Gondolas weave between gilded barges while feast-boats drift past trailing music. The bridge-plazas are where formal duels are fought — crowds gather at the first sign of drawn steel, cheering favorites and cursing rivals. Street-level walkways bustle with the city's famously loud common folk, who conduct their lives outdoors and have opinions about everything. Vendors sell from boats, performers work the bridges, and criers announce duels, scandals, and opportunities.

The Black Palace.description: The burned-out ruin of the Palazzo Morvaine, left standing since the Burning of Tevantia as a reminder of what ambition costs a city. The walls are blackened stone, the roof long gone, the grand staircase climbing to nothing. The city rebuilt around it but never touched it.

The Amphitheater.description: A massive Gold Empire arena where merchant families settle their greatest disputes through gladiatorial champions. Stone seats rise in tiers around the sand-covered fighting floor. Ancient mechanisms can flood the arena for mock naval battles — crews clash while crowds roar from above. Fight schedules are posted at every entrance. Bookmakers work the stands, and mercenaries seeking fame challenge the house champions for a chance at patronage.

The Exchange.description: A vast covered marketplace where Tevantia's famous goods change hands — spices in rainbow colors, bolts of silk that shimmer, rare alchemical components, salvaged Gold Empire artifacts, and stranger things for those who know who to ask. The merchants here deal in volume and quality, their goods bound for distant markets across Larion.

Golden Row.description: Where marble palazzos of the great merchant families compete in splendor, each facade more elaborate than the last. Private gardens glimpsed through iron gates, armored guards in house livery, palanquins and porter-trains carrying nobles who wouldn't dream of walking. Servants hurry between households while the nobility parade their wealth, their entourages, their champions. The Ducal Palace sits at the row's end, its golden dome visible from anywhere in the city.

The Arsenal.description: The massive shipyard where Tevantia's war galleys and privateers are built. The clang of hammers echoes across dry docks. Rope-walks stretch hundreds of feet. Warships in various stages of construction fill the yards. Mercenary captains recruit crew here, adventurers book passage on dangerous voyages, and those seeking to outfit an expedition can commission a vessel.

The Arcanum.description: A prestigious magical academy housed in a converted Gold Empire palace. Courtyards serve as practice grounds where students demonstrate their skills. The academy sells magical instruction, offers identification of artifacts, and maintains a marketplace where wizards trade spells and alchemists sell potions. The library holds extensive records of Gold Empire magic.

The Two Swords.description: 'What two swords decide is law' — the old Tevantine proverb gives this upscale establishment its name. The walls display crossed swords from famous duels. Ship captains, merchant factors, and minor nobles conduct business in private booths while aspiring champions seek wealthy patrons. The proprietor, a retired champion herself, maintains strict neutrality: no drawn blades indoors, grudges settled outside, and anyone caught tampering with a rival's wine answers to her personally.

The Feast-Barges.description: Gilded pleasure-barges that drift the Grand Canal, hired for celebrations, negotiations, or simply to be seen. Canopied decks, musicians, tables laden with delicacies, wine flowing freely. Some circuit the canal for hours; others anchor while their passengers revel. Hiring a feast-barge announces one's wealth and taste — or pretensions to both.

The Red Pillory.description: A raucous tavern and theater where satirical performers mock the powerful. The centerpiece is a red-painted stockade on stage where effigies of nobles, merchants, and public figures are placed and subjected to theatrical abuse — songs, accusations, pelted fruit, elaborate mock trials.

The Precipitate.description: An upscale establishment where the drinks are potions that warm you for hours, elixirs that sharpen the senses, infusions that shift color as you drink them. The mixologists take their craft seriously, and so do the regulars. A good place to meet interesting people or discuss matters you'd rather not shout over tavern noise.

Isle of Union.name: Isle of Union
Isle of Union.basicInfo: A fortified estate on a small island in the strait off Tevantia, seat of the private court of the Duke of Tevantia. Stone walls rise from the waterline to terraced gardens, with the great tower commanding the heights. The Duke receives guests, conducts private business, and hosts state functions here away from the noise of the city. When a Duke dies, the isle serves a graver purpose: the Electors seal themselves within its walls to choose a successor in a ritual called the Exclave.
Isle of Union.hiddenInfo: The isle is run by a household steward and a permanent staff who maintain the estate, gardens, and tower year-round. The Duke's personal guard garrison the walls. Visitors arrive by boat from Tevantia — an invitation from the Duke or his court is required, and the dock guards turn away anyone without one. The estate hosts private negotiations between merchant houses, state dinners for foreign dignitaries, and discreet meetings the Duke prefers to hold away from the city's eyes and ears. The gardens and pavilions are used for entertaining; the Great Hall for formal occasions. Ambitious Tevantines scheme for invitations — being received at the isle signals the Duke's favor, and being seen there by the right people can make a career. The Burning Tower is the isle's landmark, visible from Tevantia's harbor. In peacetime its beacon is dark.

The Dock.description: A stone quay beneath the estate walls where boats from Tevantia moor. The Duke's personal banner flies above the landing when he is in residence. Dock guards check invitations and turn away the uninvited. A covered waiting area with benches serves those arriving early for audiences or dinners.

The Great Hall.description: A vaulted marble hall where the Duke hosts state dinners, receives petitions, and conducts the formal business of the Duchy. The Duke's chair sits elevated at the head of a long table. Seating is unassigned at feasts, which makes it intensely political — who sits beside whom is noted by everyone. A gallery above the main floor allows attendants and lesser guests to observe without being part of the proceedings.

The Gardens.description: Terraced grounds descending from the estate toward the water, threaded with paths between hedgerows, fountains, and stone pavilions. A walled rose garden offers seclusion. Servants bring wine and food to the pavilions.

The Dueling Court.description: A paved courtyard with a sand-floored ring, weapon racks along the walls, and tiered stone benches for spectators. The Duke's champions train here daily, and visiting guests are welcome to spar — or to issue formal challenges under the watch of a Master of Arms. Bouts draw idle spectators with nothing better to do, and a strong showing in the ring has launched more than one career in Tevantine society.

The Salon.description: A richly appointed suite of connected rooms — card tables, a wine bar, a music alcove, deep chairs by tall windows overlooking the strait. This is where the isle's guests relax, or pretend to.

The Burning Tower.description: The isle's great tower, its beacon dark in peacetime, visible from Tevantia's harbor across the strait. The base houses a ceremonial chamber of pale marble and iron lantern-racks — empty and quiet between occasions of state. The tower stairs climb to the beacon platform, open to any guest willing to make the ascent. The view from the top shows all of Tevantia spread across the water.

The Quarters.description: A wing of private apartments for the Duke's guests. Corridors between apartments are narrow and well-lit. A shared servants' passage runs behind the apartments.

Altemea.name: Altemea
Altemea.basicInfo: The largest port in the Spice Isles and the city that keeps the sea lanes open. Altemea's monster-hunting crews kill the serpents, leviathans, and stranger things that plague the archipelago's shipping. The money pours in from every merchant house that wants its cargo to arrive. The city sprawls across a large island — six noble families each control a district, monster hunters are celebrities, and the gap between the hilltop villas and the dockside hammock-houses gets wider every year.
Altemea.hiddenInfo: The six noble families each run their district as a personal fiefdom — their own enforcers, their own rules, their own grudges. The boundaries between districts are where trouble happens. The families fund monster-hunting expeditions the way Tevantian houses sponsor duel-champions: for profit, for prestige, and to outdo each other. Successful hunters are courted with sponsorship offers, lavish parties, and rival families trying to poach them. Failed expeditions are quietly forgotten. The Water Arena is where new hunters prove themselves — fighting captured sea creatures for crowds and potential sponsors. The betting is serious and the outcomes aren't staged. The city's population is heavily human and half-orc, with sealion folk dominating the heavy dockwork, shark-folk involved in the hunting trade, and the usual Spice Isles mix of mersene, frogkin, halflings, and dwarves. Altemea is rough, loud, stratified, and alive. It doesn't pretend to be fair. But the monster-hunting gives the city a spine of genuine heroism — these people do something dangerous and necessary, and the best of them are admired for good reason.

The Docks.description: A massive harbor that never closes. Monster carcasses are unloaded alongside regular cargo — a serpent skull on one pier, crates of spice on the next. Hunting crews recruit openly, shouting for harpooners and battle-mages. Sealion folk haul the heavy loads, barking at each other across the water. Ship captains argue with dockmasters over berth fees.

The Water Arena.description: A massive seawater arena built into the harbor where hunters fight captured sea creatures for glory and sponsorship. Stone seats rise around a flooded pit deep enough to hold things that shouldn't be held. The crowds are rowdy. The betting is serious. Aspiring hunters fight here to prove themselves to the noble families watching from private boxes above.

The Net and Harpoon.description: A big dockside tavern in the narrow strip of territory between two family districts — neutral ground, which is why everyone drinks here. A sea-serpent jawbone hangs above the door. Inside, monster hunters swap stories with dockworkers, family agents size up new arrivals, and crew captains buy rounds for prospects they're trying to recruit.

Siren's Landing.name: Siren's Landing
Siren's Landing.basicInfo: A pirate haven built across an archipelago of rocky islands rising from treacherous sea-channels, connected by bridges and skiffs. Said to have been founded long ago by the legendary Ligeia, a siren queen exiled by her kind. Now the City of Second Chances, where anyone can make a new life for themselves and the only law is the Swordlaw - the justice free folk carve for themselves with the edge of a blade.
