Siren's Landing.hiddenInfo: Inhabitants: Humans make up most of the population but hold no special status. Mersene (semi-aquatic seafolk) guide pirate ships through the perilous shoals and channels and tend the city's many shrines to Queen Ligeia, whom they consider one of their own. Noisy, rowdy, messy birdfolk claim an entire island and loudly mock anyone who complains. Half-sea elves, demonborn, dark orcs, and others who found no welcome elsewhere blend into the crowds. Here, folk's name and fame are all. The hauls fenced here to factors of mainland buyers are legendary, but the parties thrown with the proceeds - profit one, profit all! are even more legendary. Violence does break out - such is the way of the Swordlaw, no denizen of the Landing would dare trade the blade for the corrupt justice of the so-called 'Free States' - but open warfare is bad for everyone's business. Swordlaw keeps disputes personal - if someone wrongs you, it's on you to correct them, Lord of Fortune be praised!

The Crossing.description: Crowded waystation on an island across the channel from the city where travelers embark on ferries to make the dangerous crossing into the city. There is no fixed price for passage, only a chaotic shouted auction where strange bargains are often struck in return for passage. Mersene pilots are the most skilled but also the most expensive. To add to the confusion, buskers, conmen and recruiters harangue those seeking passage in every tongue known to Larion.

Ligeia's Landing.description: A broad square occupying the space between cliff and sea on the city's main island where ferries bring new arrivals. A cliffside sea grotto holds the city's most famous statue of the great siren queen, offerings piled at her feet. Street performers work the new arrivals. Hawkers sell food and drink. Criers advertise taverns and other entertainments. Recruiters seek the brave or gullible for dubious ventures.

Tidecaller Isle.description: The mersene quarter on a low rocky island. Grottoes at and beneath the waterline where they actually live. Colorful buildings on the surface serve as shops where mersene offer traditional seacrafts and goods from beneath the sea. Linked to the other islands by rickety bridges that the mersene don't use, since they swim.

The Above Board.description: Largest tavern in the city, built from a merchant prince's massive, gilded galleon, captured and run aground long ago. Frequent rows are settled with fisticuffs - or, if matters escalate, in the Swordlaw way, with good steel - but the loser walks the plank rather than dies on the deck. The drop is high, the rocks are real, and the sea decides who climbs back up the stairs to buy the next round, Lord of Fortune be praised!

The Handmedown.description: Sprawling bazaar where crews fence their hauls. Stalls crowd the waterfront. Everything that can be stolen by pirates is sold here, with the exception of slaves, which are forbidden by Swordlaw custom.

The Perch.description: Birdfolk isle, reached only by ferry or swimming since they knocked down the bridge to spite the groundlings from whom they took the place. Ramshackle stilted structures of the kind favored by the birdfolk are stacked precariously across every surface. Birdfolk tussle in midair, shout across gaps, come and go at all hours. Groundlings are tolerated as long as they don't put on airs or complain about all the climbing they have to do to get around.

The Drowned City of Orichala.name: The Drowned City of Orichala
The Drowned City of Orichala.basicInfo: Ruins of a Free State that sank beneath the waves within living memory. Orichala declared itself heir to the Gold Empire, pouring its wealth into recovering lost secrets. They succeeded well enough to build soaring towers, underwater districts accessible through waterbreathing magic, and vaults filled with recovered treasures. Then came the Doom—in a single night, the sea rose and swallowed everything. The island tilted as it sank, leaving only its highest point above the waves, sloping down into a maze of flooded palaces and crooked towers where predators hunt among the bones of ambition.
The Drowned City of Orichala.hiddenInfo:
Orichala's recovered artifacts and waterbreathing enchantments are still down there — the vaults weren't emptied, just drowned. Salvage crews mount expeditions regularly; few return with much, fewer return at all.
 Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps, lairs in the great dome now lying at the bottom of the sunken city. She is the Dragon Queen's Harbinger in the Spice Isles — something between sea serpent and dragon, vast, armored in barnacle-crusted scale, too large and too changed to ever come ashore. She commands every predator and leviathan in the deep water around the Spice Isles. She enters the dome through a breach in the wall and holds court in the lake, presiding over a stolen surface world.

The Dome of All Worlds is a colossal sealed habitat beneath the ruins. Ancient magic keeps the air breathable and sustains the biomes within. The specimens have gone feral — centuries of breeding with no control. Sabrecats, gore lizards, a basilisk, and ironshell crabs inhabit the overgrown jungle. A hydra hunts the lakeshore. Young sea serpents — Ophialtha's brood — patrol the lake.

Merfolk cultists of the Scaled Covenant serve Ophialtha in the waters around the city. They are partially transformed — finned, scaled, some with shark-like features or bioluminescent markings. They fight with tridents, harpoons, and water magic. Sea trolls and giant crabs infest the flooded corridors of the descent. Drowners hunt the tidal ruins above.

The High Point.description: The exposed tip of Orichala, where salvage crews make camp and plan their descents. The island tilted when it sank; this is the part that stayed above water. Broken foundations and toppled columns mark where buildings once stood. A few have been shored up into rough shelters by expeditions past. From here you can see the Breach — and beyond it, the drowned streets of the Shallows, half-visible beneath the churning water.

The Breach.description: A gaping wound in Orichala's walls where a section collapsed into rubble. The walls still stand to either side, slanting seaward, crusted with salt and barnacles up to the old waterline. Salvagers have rigged rope lines and crude ladders through the gap. Beyond lies the city proper — or what remains of it.

The Shallows.description: The outskirts of old Orichala, now half-claimed by the sea. Streets slope downward into murky water. Buildings stand open to the sky, their lower floors flooded, their walls slick with growth. At low tide you can wade through; at high tide, you swim or drown. Drowners lurk in flooded cellars and sunken doorways — pallid, web-fingered things that drag the unwary under.

The Tidetraps.description: Deeper into the city, where the streets run crooked and the towers lean. The island's tilt left these buildings standing but wrong — doorways at odd angles, stairs that climb sideways, windows that face the sea floor. Water fills the lower reaches but doesn't stay put. Surges no one can predict flood streets to the rooftops, then drain away as suddenly as they came. The drowners hunt here too, riding the surges.

The Descent.description: Sealed Orichalan corridors leading deeper into the sunken city. The builders designed these passages for underwater use — heavy doors, air-locked chambers, walls of reinforced stone. The magic that keeps air in them still holds, mostly. Some sections are flooded where seals have failed. Sea trolls nest in the flooded chambers — huge, regenerating, adapted to salt water. Giant crabs pick through the ruins alongside them.

The Dome Gate.description: The monumental entrance to the Dome of All Worlds. Massive double doors of bronze and glass, still sealed, still intact. The Orichalans built them to impress and they still do. A stone golem stands in an alcove beside the doors — an Orichalan guardian construct, still functional, still following its ancient orders. Beyond the doors, warm air and green light.

The Wilds.description: The interior of the Dome of All Worlds. A vast enclosed space — the curved glass walls rise to a peak far above, the ocean dark beyond them. Ancient magic floods the dome with pale warm light. What was once a carefully curated habitat is now a dense, overgrown jungle. Centuries of unchecked growth and breeding have made everything bigger, stranger, more aggressive. Sabrecats — massive, tusked, the apex predators of this enclosed world — prowl the undergrowth. Gore lizards hold territory in clearings. A basilisk has been sighted in the deeper growth. Ironshell crabs the size of dogs scuttle through the canopy and drop on prey from above.

The Shattered Shore.description: Where the dome's great lake once had a manicured shore, now a muddy, overgrown waterline where freshwater and seawater mix. The dome wall is breached here — a jagged opening where the ocean has pushed in, flooding the lake and connecting it to the open sea. A hydra has claimed the shallows, hunting anything that comes to drink. Beyond the breach, the lake stretches into darkness. Something much larger than the hydra moves out there.

Ophialtha's Court.description: The great lake at the dome's center, half freshwater, half ocean. Islands of tangled vegetation float on the surface. Young sea serpents — Ophialtha's brood — coil among them, watching. The water is deep and black. Ophialtha herself is below, vast and ancient, surfacing when she chooses. She holds court here in her stolen world — a sea creature presiding over a pocket of land and sky, too changed to ever rule the surface, unwilling to stop pretending.

Four Elements Isle.name: Four Elements Isle
Four Elements Isle.basicInfo: A mountainous island dominated by the Four Elements Monastery, where monks train in elemental martial arts. Stone buildings with blue-tiled roofs climb the mountain's slopes, connected by steep stairs and terraced gardens. Ships dock at the beach below, where a small fishing village serves the monastery's needs. The monks here master earth, fire, water, and air - channeling elemental power through disciplined combat techniques.
Four Elements Isle.hiddenInfo: The monastery welcomes students who prove their dedication, but the training is harsh and many fail. Masters can perform impossible feats - walking on water, striking with flame, moving like the wind, standing immovable as stone. The Sea Caves beneath the island descend far deeper than anyone has fully explored. The Grandmaster believes something sleeps in the deepest caves - something that predates the monastery and perhaps even the Gold Empire ruins scattered across the Spice Isles.

The Temple Steps.description: A long stone stairway climbs from the beach landing, ascending hundreds of feet through switchbacks and rest platforms. Small shrines mark every hundredth step - earth at the base, water at the first platform, fire at the second, air near the summit. Stone lanterns light the way at night.

Beach Village.description: A small fishing village where boats dock and supplies are unloaded. Simple wooden houses with thatched roofs cluster around a protected cove. The village serves the monastery but maintains its own rhythms - children play on the beach, women gut fish at cleaning tables.

The Grand Dojo.description: A massive training hall with high ceilings and open sides that let in the sea breeze. The floor is divided into four sections - stone for earth forms, a fire pit for flame techniques, a shallow pool for water training, and an open space for air movements. Monks in colored sashes practice their forms while masters walk among them, correcting stances. Weapon racks line the walls. A great bell hangs from the central beam, struck to mark training sessions. Steps lead upward toward living quarters and shrines, downward toward the springs and caves.

The Refectory.description: A long hall with wooden tables where monks gather to eat and drink. The kitchen serves rice, fish, vegetables, and tea throughout the day, but after evening training the mood shifts - rice wine appears, voices rise in laughter and argument, games of dice emerge from pockets. Masters drink with students, travelers share tales from the city-states, and the warmth of the fire pit drives out the evening chill. This is where friendships form and gossip spreads, where monks become human again after hours of discipline.

The Healing Springs.description: Natural hot springs pool in terraced stone basins built into the mountainside. Steam rises into the cool air while monks soak away training injuries. The water smells of minerals and supposedly carries healing properties from deep within the island. Stone benches and changing rooms surround the pools. Masters often give lecture here, the heat and relaxation making students more receptive to wisdom. At night, lanterns reflect off the water's surface.

The Summit Shrine.description: The monastery's highest point, where a simple stone shrine honors the elements. Four pillars mark the cardinal directions - carved with symbols of earth, fire, water, and air. The view spans the entire Spice Isles archipelago - city-states visible in the distance, ships moving between islands, the sun rising from the sea. Masters meditate here at dawn. Students make the climb to clear their minds or seek guidance. On clear days you can see the storm clouds gathering over the Sea of Sorrows to the west.

The Sea Caves.description: Cave entrances near the beach lead into passages beneath the island. The caves are used for training - learning to fight in darkness, practicing water techniques in underground pools, testing courage in the deep places. But the passages go deeper than any training exercise requires. Some tunnels show signs of ancient construction - smooth walls, carved stairs descending into black water. Initiates are sometimes sent into the depths on ordeals, told to meditate until they find clarity.

The Sunken Palace.name: The Sunken Palace
The Sunken Palace.basicInfo: A massive Gold Empire palace half-submerged in the bay, its marble columns and golden domes rising from the water. The upper floors can be reached by boat through collapsed archways, their halls open to sky and long looted of treasures. The lower levels descend into flooded darkness where something glitters just beyond the reach of torchlight.
The Sunken Palace.hiddenInfo: The merfolk inhabiting the flooded lower halls are actually the descendants of Gold Empire citizens transformed by ancient magic. They capture surface dwellers, trapping them with enchantment and transformation magic, to swell their number. The real imperial vault lies deeper still, past their court.

The Flooded Hall.description: A vast hall where boats can navigate between massive columns rising from murky water. Statues of Gold Empire nobles stand on pedestals above the waterline, faded but still visible. Mosaics catch the light filtering through collapsed sections of roof. Sealed doors and archways lead to other chambers - some above water, some submerged.

The Sunken Court.description: The passages slope downward into complete darkness, the water growing deeper until swimming becomes necessary. Down here the halls remain intact - sealed by magic against the sea. Phosphorescent coral grows on the walls, casting everything in ghostly blue-green light.

The Serpent's Maw.name: The Serpent's Maw
The Serpent's Maw.basicInfo: A massive sea cave on a rocky island, its entrance a jagged opening that resembles a serpent's mouth full of stone teeth. Inside, the cavern opens into a vast chamber littered with broken ships, scattered debris, and enormous shed scales. Passages lead deeper into the island and down into flooded tunnels that connect to the undersea network.
The Serpent's Maw.hiddenInfo: A colossal sea serpent makes this its lair, hoarding treasure from sunken vessels and raided ruins. The creature is ancient and cunning - intelligent enough to understand speech and treacherous enough to offer false bargains. Its hoard contains centuries of plunder, but taking anything risks waking it.

The Serpent's Basin.description: The main cavern stretches hundreds of feet across. The remains of ships lie scattered across the rocky floor - broken masts, shattered hulls, rusted anchors. Massive shed scales, each the size of a shield, lean against the walls. Multiple passages lead deeper - some rising into the island's interior, others descending toward the sound of water.

The Serpent's Lair.description: The passages slope down into chambers where water laps at stone ledges.Something massive rests beneath the surface, its breathing causing the water level to rise and fall in slow rhythm.

The Titans' Gate.name: The Titans' Gate
The Titans' Gate.basicInfo: Two massive carved figures, fifty feet tall, flank a grand stairway descending into the earth. The Gold Empire titans are weathered but intact, their stone faces gazing outward across the sea. The stairs lead down through worked halls lined with empty alcoves and sealed doors, eventually reaching passages that slope toward dark water. Stone guardians still patrol the deeper sections, and ancient traps remain armed after centuries.
The Titans' Gate.hiddenInfo: The titans aren't just statues - they're dormant constructs that served as guardians. The site holds both the empire's treasures and something they sealed away in the flooded depths, something they needed the titans to watch over. The same mechanisms that could bypass the vault seals might also wake the guardians. Whatever lies at the bottom of the flooded passages was important enough to justify this level of protection.

The Titan Plaza.description: A wide stone platform between the two colossal figures. Each titan stands fifty feet tall, carved from single blocks of dark stone and covered in intricate armor patterns and inscriptions. Their faces are stern and identical, each holding a massive stone sword planted point-down before them. Between them, broad stairs descend into the earth. The first steps are open to sky, but they quickly pass under a stone lintel carved with warnings in the Gold Empire glyph script. Stone benches ring the plaza where treasure hunters rest before attempting the descent.

The Descent.description: The stairs lead down through worked stone halls lined with empty alcoves and sealed bronze doors. Every fifty steps, the passage opens into small chambers with strange mechanisms built into the walls - gears, levers, panels. Some doors bear intact seals, others have been breached by past treasure hunters. The deeper sections are flooded, dark water blocking further progress. Stone guardians stand motionless in alcoves, their eyes dark.

Beggarsbridge.name: Beggarsbridge
Beggarsbridge.basicInfo: An anarchic shantytown built along and beneath a mile-long Gold Empire bridge between two major islands. No single power controls it; instead the Tollmaster holds the north gate while the Chainmaster controls the south gate and harbor passage with massive chains. Buildings of every description cling to the ancient stonework - rotting shanties on stilts, brick tenements, even ruined watchtowers. Mercenaries recruit openly, smugglers trade stolen goods, and everyone follows the unwritten rules: pay your debts, never look weak, and don't bring city-state conflicts onto the bridge. Official government representatives get thrown off the Plank. It's where the Spice Isles' dirty work gets done - a place of violent opportunity for those with the stomach for it.
Beggarsbridge.hiddenInfo: The bridge exists in perpetual violent equilibrium between the Tollmaster and Chainmaster's proxy wars, which prevents anyone else from consolidating power. Hidden spillway hatches behind false walls allow quick disposal of evidence or enemies into the canal where Guttermouths wait. Gold Empire pneumatic tubes run through the stonework, still functional for those who know the valve combinations. Sea-Changed crawl up at night to trade with Underhang residents. The bridge hosts colonies of Ratkin in the old Customs House ruins, Frogkin in the Underhang platforms, Crowkin atop the watchtowers, and occasional Fishfolk in flooded lower levels. Guttermouths patrol the waters below, feeding on whatever falls. Serpentkin operate from hidden dens in the bridge's hollow supports. A few Changelings work as information brokers, while a Rakshasa runs high-stakes games in a hidden gambling den. Water Weirds serve as assassins for hire, entering through the canal. Mindworms occasionally infest the darker corners, requiring expensive extraction at Mother Clench's.

North Gate.description: The fortified gatehouse where the current Tollmaster controls land access to the bridge. Guards extract fees from anyone who looks like they can pay, though ordinary folk without coin can pass if they pledge obedience to the Tollmaster. The gate's ironbound doors can seal the bridge during serious conflicts.

Tollmaster's Tower.description: A fortified tower rising above the north gate where the current Tollmaster holds court. The position changes hands every few years when someone stronger throws the incumbent to the Guttermouths below. From here, the Tollmaster's lieutenants coordinate protection rackets and proxy wars against the Chainmaster.

The Chain Station.description: At the bridge's southern end, ancient Gold Empire machinery allows the Chainmaster to raise massive chains across the harbor channel, blocking all ship traffic until appropriate fees are paid. The position is as unstable as the Tollmaster's, changing hands through violence every few years.

Mother Clench's Hospice.description: A rickety hospital on stilts over the water, run by a hideously scarred former pirate whose shattered jaw left her face in a permanent grimace. She patches up anyone who can pay and some who can't, asking no questions about knife wounds or stranger injuries. The hospice's neutrality is absolute - violate it and every gang on the bridge will feed you to the Guttermouths.

Chandler's Row.description: Ship supply stores selling everything a captain might need, from honest rope and tar to containers with false bottoms and charts marking which customs officers take bribes. The shops stay open all hours, catering to vessels that prefer to resupply under cover of darkness.

Gold & Glory Pawnshop.description: A sprawling operation occupying three connected buildings where anything can be bought or sold - stolen spice shipments, Gold Empire artifacts, deeds to ships currently at sea, contracts of indenture, mysterious sealed boxes. The proprietor, an ancient crowkin known only as 'The Appraiser,' can evaluate anything from a glass eye to a kraken egg.

The Midmarket.description: The bridge's widest section hosts a permanent maze of market stalls selling everything the legitimate markets won't touch: forged guild credentials, stolen cargo, dreamleaf in all its varieties, poisons that leave no trace. Blockades frequently erupt here when gangs put up barriers and demand concessions.

Forgery Row.description: A cluster of scribes and document specialists creating everything from false noble patents to shipping manifests that will pass any inspection, even the arcane watermarks used by the banking houses. The forgers work behind reinforced doors - their skills make them valuable targets for kidnapping.

Three Sisters Smoke House.description: A dreamleaf parlor run by three fey sisters known as Dream, Desire, and Sleep. Private chambers offer narcotic hazes mixed with subtle enchantments, creating visions that sometimes reveal truth and sometimes beautiful lies. Patrons often stumble out days later, unsure what was real.

The Chop Shop.description: A massive butchery processing 'seafood' of unusual size and origin - seamonster steaks, kraken tentacles, organs that glow. If it comes from the water and someone will pay for it, the Chop Shop will carve it up. They also discretely dispose of other things that need carving, no questions asked.

The Devil's Advocate.description: A narrow office where a well-dressed fiend provides legal consultation on the letter of various laws, treaties, and contracts. For the right price, she'll find the loophole that lets you murder someone without technically violating a peace treaty, or explain exactly how to word a contract so your partner doesn't realize they've sold their soul.

Madame Splice's Fleshmarket.description: A surgical theater where a mage-biomancer offers 'improvements' to those who can pay. Gills for breathing underwater, extra eyes grafted into the back of your head, fingers that extend into tentacles - Madame Splice can arrange it all, though her work sometimes has unexpected side effects months later.

The Plank.description: A reinforced platform jutting from the bridge for public executions by whoever claims enough power to drag someone there. Below it, the Drowning Cells - iron cages suspended by chains that can be lowered into the water at high tide for execution, torture, or making a point.

The Water Arena.description: A floating platform tethered beneath the bridge where blood sports entertain crowds and settle disputes. Fighters battle each other or captured sea creatures while spectators bet on outcomes. Champions who survive enough bouts sometimes earn sponsorship from the city-states.

The Waterline Inn - Above Board.description: The upper level of the Waterline Inn, a rowdy sailor's tavern where ship captains recruit crew, pirates fence stolen goods, and mercenaries negotiate contracts over rotgut rum. Everyone keeps one hand on their purse and another on their blade.

The Waterline Inn - Below Board.description: The inn's lower level, built into the bridge's massive support pillars, floods at high tide through specially designed vents. When the waters rise, Fishfolk, Sea-Changed, and other aquatic criminals swim up through submerged doorways to conduct business impossible in daylight. Deals made here involve things dredged from the harbor bottom, people who need to disappear beneath the waves, and information from the deep channels. Barnacle-covered furniture and walls slick with algae mark the high-water line.

The Perch.description: Atop a ruined watchtower, Crowkin gather to trade information and gossip. The climb is nearly impossible without wings, making it one of the few secure meeting spots on the bridge. The Crowkin see everything from up there and sell their knowledge to anyone who can make the climb or pay their messenger fees.

The Customs House.description: A grand building from an ambitious attempt to civilize the bridge, now a crumbling ruin infested with Ratkin and desperate squatters. Its basement connects to Gold Empire pneumatic tubes that still function for those who know the right valve combinations. The Ratkin can get a message to anyone, even during blockades.

The Underhang.description: Rickety platforms and walkways suspended beneath the bridge where Frogkin and other amphibious peoples maintain hideouts just above the high tide line. Rope ladders trail into the water for quick escapes. At night, Sea-Changed sometimes crawl up to trade or seek help with their transformations.

The Butcher's Guild.description: Everyone knows the shop with the pig sign is more than just a butchery, but nobody says it out loud. The Guild takes contracts for wet work anywhere except Beggarsbridge itself - they don't shit where they eat. Their cleavers are sharp, their aprons somehow always clean, and their sausages extremely popular despite nobody asking what's in them.

The Nest.name: The Nest
The Nest.basicInfo: A cluster of small islands connected by rope bridges and salvaged planking, where privateers and pirates anchor openly between raids. Rickety docks accommodate vessels of every size while shore camps provide drink, repairs, and fencing for stolen goods. No city-state claims The Nest, yet no navy moves against it - an arrangement that profits someone, though who remains unclear.
The Nest.hiddenInfo: The Nest operates through payments to Tevantian merchant houses who use it to move contraband, hire deniable violence, and conduct business they can't do openly. Several houses compete to control which captains get the best information and contracts. Pirates who break the unwritten rules - attacking protected ships, killing the wrong people, or talking too much - are found floating face-down. The bridges between islands create natural chokepoints that the resident captains defend when necessary. Some islands serve specific functions - one for repairs, one for fencing goods, one for recruitment. Most captains spend only a few weeks here between voyages, but there is a permanent population of shipwrights, fences, and tavern-keepers. Tensions rise when too many ships anchor at once and rivalry gets violent.

The Docks.description: Ramshackle wooden piers where ships of all flags moor side by side, their crews drinking and gambling while shore merchants hawk supplies and repairs. The Harbor Master's shack sits at the main pier's end, where he collects mooring fees and settles disputes with a loaded crossbow.

The Shore Camp.description: A sprawling collection of tents, lean-tos, and salvaged buildings where pirates rest between voyages. Multiple taverns compete for business while fences buy stolen goods and information brokers trade secrets in shadowed corners.

The Bronze Wreck.name: The Bronze Wreck
The Bronze Wreck.basicInfo: A shallow reef off just off the coast where a storm has recently surfaced what is believed to be an ancient Gold Empire warship, its bronze-clad hulls visible just beneath the surface. Tidal changes expose masts and upper decks at low water, then submerge them again.
The Bronze Wreck.hiddenInfo: Salvagers using magical diving spells pull up bronze fittings, intact ancient weapons, and occasionally bits of machinery. The deeper parts of the wreck contain the most valuable prizes but also the greatest dangers - things that made homes in the hulls. Sea elves claim the deep as their territory and attack divers who venture too far down. The deepest parts of the wreck connect to underwater tunnels that lead to drowned temple complexes.

Salvage Camp.description: A sprawling campsite where salvage crews from various noble houses of Tevantia and other organizations have set up their tents and bases of operation. Arguments and fights regularly break out among the crews, and each band has its own soldiers to protect them and their finds. Merchants, traders and the curious visit, and items change hands daily.

The Shallows.description: Where the reef breaks the surface at low tide, exposing the upper decks and masts of the wrecked warship. The presence of soldiers from the various crews dissuades sea elf attacks here, though occasional raids have claimed surface lives. This is where crews use their magic diving spells to dive beneath the surface in search of salvage and plunder.

Sea-Devil Isle.name: Sea-Devil Isle
Sea-Devil Isle.basicInfo: A rocky island surrounded by treacherous shoals where sea-devils emerge from the water to attack anyone who sets foot on shore. The ruins of an ancient villa stand abandoned on the heights. Ships give the island wide berth - those who anchor here rarely leave. Yet certain individuals are said to come and go safely, trading with the creatures.
Sea-Devil Isle.hiddenInfo: The sea-devils consider the island sacred. They tolerate no trespassers but may negotiate. Smugglers, exiles, and desperate individuals have made pacts with the sea-devils - offering weapons, surface goods, or doing their bidding. The sea-devils are not unified; different bands control different parts of the island. Some have been sea-changed in reverse, gaining the ability to breathe air for longer periods, making them even more dangerous. The villa ruins contain valuable artifacts that the sea-devils consider holy and will allow no surfacer to touch, but reaching them means either fighting or bargaining. The sea-devils sometimes attack ships that pass too close, boarding to take prisoners or cargo.

The Shoals.description: Treacherous shallow waters studded with jagged rocks where ships run aground if they venture too close. Sea-devils emerge from the waves here to challenge any who dare approach the island.

The Landing Beach.description: A narrow strip of dark sand littered with wreckage from ships that tried to land. Sea-devil sentries watch from the rocks above, ready to attack or parley depending on who approaches.

The Spawning Pools.description: Shallow tidal pools among the rocks where sea-devil eggs float in the brackish water. The creatures guard this sacred site with fanatical fury, though some bands may negotiate for offerings.

The Ruined Village.description: Abandoned stone buildings on the island's heights, their roofs collapsed and walls crumbling.

Wolfpack Lodge.name: Wolfpack Lodge
Wolfpack Lodge.basicInfo: A fortified warrior lodge built of massive rough-hewn timbers located at the edge of the Wolfwood. Headquarters of the Wolfpack, a warrior society and mercenary company of berserkers and shock troops who hire out for hunts against legendary beasts and other threats too great for ordinary warriors. The compound serves as both home and training ground.
Wolfpack Lodge.hiddenInfo: Rowdy, competitive, intense, reverent. Constant sparring matches, feats of strength celebrated, walls hung with trophies of slain monsters. The rumors of lycanthropy are true, but misunderstood by outsiders. The 'Moon Blood' is not a curse but a coveted rite of passage for the inner circle. The Alpha, Hrothgar, selects only the most disciplined warriors to receive the gift. The compound acts as a sanctuary for this transformation. While they accept gold, their loyalty lies with the Pack and their primal traditions. Beneath the lodge, secure cells hold members who have lost control of their fury.

The Palisade Gate.description: The sole entrance to the compound, guarded by heavy wooden gates and sharpened log walls. Watchtowers flank the entry, manned by archers. This area acts as the checkpoint for potential clients and new recruits seeking entry.

The Training Grounds.description: A large, muddy central courtyard dominated by sparring circles and wooden practice dummies. This is the hub of daily activity where warriors train, duel for status, and maintain their gear. It connects all major buildings within the compound.

The Great Lodge.description: A massive longhouse serving as the mess hall, sleeping quarters, and meeting place. A central fire pit burns constantly. Mission contracts are posted here, and it serves as the primary social hub for rumors, drinking, and gambling.

The Iron Forge.description: An open-air smithy dedicated to repairing heavy plate and sharpening oversized weapons. The equipment here is designed for durability rather than aesthetics. It is a place for trade, repair, and crafting.

The Beast Kennels.description: Enclosures housing large war-wolves that fight alongside the mercenaries. Handlers train here to bond with the animals.

The Alpha's Quarters.description: A separated section at the back of the Great Lodge reserved for high command. It contains maps of the region, the company's treasury, and records of contracts. Negotiations for major campaigns happen here.

The Moon Cellar.description: A stone basement beneath the Alpha's Quarters with reinforced iron cages and heavy chains bolt to the walls. Used to contain members during transformation cycles or to imprison severe rule-breakers.

The Red Chantry.name: The Red Chantry
The Red Chantry.basicInfo: An elegant fortified manor house situated just outside the jurisdiction of Ardan's Watch in Highvale. It serves as the regional chapter house for the Bloodhunters, an elite order of hemomancers and duelists dedicated to eradicating vampires and other undead threats.
The Red Chantry.hiddenInfo: Stylish, aristocratic, precise, dangerous. The Chantry feels less like a barracks and more like a dark academic salon mixed with a fencing school. Members wear fine apparel and carry antique weaponry. Training available in blood magic specifically designed to counter vampires: blood-tracking (vampires leave traces in victims), mental wards (your blood protects your mind from compulsion), and flesh-bane rituals (undead tissue can't regenerate when marked with life-magic drawn from fresh blood). Every hunter knows that the blood magic they use risks corruption but are proud to bear this burden for the great old cause of the Chantry. They are fiercely protective of their honor and reputation: when a hunter goes rogue, the Chantry's most elite hunters are dispathced to eliminate them before word of the incident spreads to preserve their charter.

The Iron Gates.description: Tall wrought-iron gates reinforced with silver filigree and flanked by stone gargoyles. All who show no signs of undead taint are granted entry.

The Courtyard.description: A manicured garden within the walls with a central fountain. It serves as an open-air meeting space and a buffer zone between the public gates and the inner sanctum.

The Grand Hall.description: A lavishly appointed main chamber with high vaulted ceilings, velvet drapery, and a massive strategy map of the region spread across a central table. This is the social heart of the Chantry where contracts are distributed, intel is shared over fine wine, and hunters socialize.

The Duelist's Ring.description: A precise, circular stone platform dedicated to fencing and combat drills. Here, hunters practice their distinctive style—a mix of swordplay and somatic magic. It is a place of competition, instruction, and display of skill.

The Armory.description: A workshop with equipment for etching runes onto blades, mixing volatile alchemical compounds and preparing other undead-hunting gear.

The Library.description: A quiet, multi-level archive containing texts on necrology, bloodlines, and forbidden anatomies. It is a hub for research and investigation, critical for identifying specific vampire strains or tracking ancient threats.

The Chapter Master's Study.description: A private office overlooking the Grand Hall, reserved for the ranking officer. It houses the order's records, high-value bounties, and the 'Black Book' of rogue members.

The Undercroft.description: A stone basement protected by heavy wards. This area is used for the teaching of blood magic, the cleansing of minor corruption, and the interrogation of captured undead. Access is restricted to sworn members.

The Oubliette.description: A secure holding area with reinforced, rune-carved cells designed to nullify magic. It holds high-value monster captives or, on rare occasions, hunters who have succumbed to corruption and await judgment or study.

Fawnhollow.name: Fawnhollow
Fawnhollow.basicInfo: A quiet clearing in the deep Everwood where a stream spills over a mossy cliff into a small lake below. Wildflowers and ferns crowd the banks. Old elven waymarks are carved into stones along the water, worn smooth by age.
Fawnhollow.hiddenInfo: A hidden entrance behind the waterfall leads to a vast underground cavern containing a lightless inland sea. A towering column of dark porous stone — the Black Eyrie — rises from the water's center, riddled with tunnel openings at every level. The cavern ceiling glimmers with veins of pale crystal like a false night sky. Astarieth the Nether Wyrm lairs at the top of the column. She is the Dragon Queen's Harbinger in the Everwood — a vast serpentine dragon of obsidian scale, many-limbed, eyeless. She perceives the world through tremorsense and dark magic. She moves through the column's porous rock freely, emerging from any opening, any wall, any crack. She prefers not to emerge from her lair in the Black Eyrie, trusting the cavern's inhabitants will make it difficult for potential adversaries to reach her. Dark elf loyalists camp on the shore — warriors in dark armor, envoys, zealots. They come bearing intelligence and tribute, waiting days for audience. They control the boats, the only safe way across the water. The descent tunnels are infested with fellbats — colonial, aggressive when disturbed, dangerous in swarms. The underground sea is home to pale, eyeless aquatic predators that hunt by vibration.

The Falls.description: A quiet clearing in the deep Everwood where a stream spills over a mossy cliff face into a pool below. Wildflowers, birdsong, dappled light. Old elven waymarks are carved into stones along the stream — worn smooth, easy to miss. A path leads behind the waterfall.

The Waterfall Cave.description: A natural cavern behind the falls, damp and cool. Dark elf script is scratched into the walls — fresher than the waymarks outside. Signs of recent use: a fire pit, bedrolls, supplies.

The Descent.description: A narrow tunnel angling steeply downward through raw stone. Fellbats roost in clusters along the ceiling — leathery, sharp-toothed, aggressive when light or noise disturbs them. The tunnel branches and rejoins, but all paths lead down.

The Shelf.description: A wide stone ledge where the tunnel opens onto the cavern. A black sea stretches below, its surface faintly phosphorescent. The cavern ceiling glimmers with pale crystal veins like distant stars. At the center of the water, a column of dark stone rises from the surface — enormous, porous, riddled with dark openings. The shore is visible far below, where dim lights mark a camp. A carved stair descends the cavern wall.

The Shore.description: A strip of flat stone at the water's edge where a camp has been struck. Tents, supplies, boats pulled up on the rock.

The Black Water.description: The underground sea. Still, black, and cold. The water is deep and the bottom is not visible. Pale shapes move below the surface — eyeless predators that hunt by vibration.

The Base.description: A rough landing where the column meets the waterline. The stone is dark and porous, full of holes and openings at every height. Some are large enough to walk through. Others are narrow cracks. A main passage spirals upward into the interior.

The Warren.description: Honeycombed tunnels inside the column, spiraling upward. The walls are thin between passages — sound carries, echoes distort. Side tunnels branch in every direction, some too narrow for anything human-sized, others opening into small chambers.

The Eyrie.description: The highest chamber in the column. A domed space of dark stone open on multiple sides to the cavern air, high above the black water. The crystal-veined ceiling is close here. The floor is scored deep by claws and coils. Bones are piled in the corners — offerings dragged up from below..

Everwood League Chapterhouse.name: Everwood League Chapterhouse
Everwood League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: A living Greatheart — one of the Everwood's sacred trees — that has slumbered since before human memory. It hasn't stirred in an Age, but it isn't dead. The bark is whole, the roots drink deep, and the trunk holds warmth in winter. Somehow, it keeps the League of Adventurers the way any great tree keeps birds and squirrels — naturally, without apparent effort or intent. The League didn't choose this tree. The tree chose the League, or at least allowed them. No one really knows why. Locals call it the Hearthoak.
Everwood League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The Hearthoak is a true Greatheart — one of the ancient trees where druids commune with the forest's deepest spirit. But this one has not spoken or stirred in living memory or elven memory before that. It sleeps. Its heartwood is alive but still, growing so slowly that centuries pass between visible changes. And yet it plainly tolerates the League's presence in a way it does not tolerate others.

The League discovered the tree decades ago when a Blade company sheltering beneath its roots during a storm found the bark had opened overnight — a gap in the trunk wide enough to enter, leading to hollow chambers within that seemed shaped for habitation. They moved in. The tree has never objected. It has also never explained.

The chambers inside are not carved or rotted. They appear to be natural hollows in the living heartwood — spaces the tree grew around or grew for some unknown purpose. The wood is warm, faintly fragrant, and alive. New growth occasionally appears overnight: a shelf of wood where someone needed a shelf, a curve in the wall that wasn't there last season. The changes are slow, subtle, and possibly coincidental. The League has learned not to drive nails into the walls. Rope and friction-fit fixtures only.

Both elven factions leave the Hearthoak alone. A living Greatheart is sacrosanct — neither high elves nor dark elves will provoke one regardless of who shelters inside it. This makes the chapterhouse the safest place in the Everwood for Outsiders. The Wardens watch from a distance but do not interfere.

The druids of Velantami are deeply curious about the Hearthoak. They visit regularly, press their hands to the bark, listen. They hear something — they won't say what. Their best theory is that the tree is dreaming, and the League is part of the dream. Their worst theory is that the tree is waiting for something, and the League is bait.

High elves post contracts here through intermediaries. Dark elf dead drops appear in the root system. The Master of the House manages the most delicate neutrality in the Everwood — both sides use the League without acknowledging each other's presence. The Hearthoak's sacred status is the only reason this arrangement survives.

A Greater Owl roost occupies the upper branches where living wood meets open sky. The portal stone was placed in the tree's crown where branches cradle it like cupped hands — the League swears the branches shifted to hold it after it was hauled up.

The Rootstair.description: The Greatheart's roots arch from the earth in ridges taller than a person, covered in moss and fern. The League cut steps into the largest root, following its curve up to a gap in the trunk where the bark opened years ago and has never closed. The gap looks grown rather than broken — smooth edges, living bark on both sides. The forest here is dense and quiet. Other trees press close to the Greatheart as if drawn to it. League trail-markers guide the way from the nearest road, but the tree is easy to find — it rises above the canopy like a green tower among green towers, distinguishable only by its size.

The Heartwood.description: The main chamber inside the trunk. The space rises three stories within living wood — curved walls, a natural column where the heartwood is densest, and hollow alcoves that might have been grown on purpose. The League built timber platforms at three levels connected by stairs that follow the trunk's inner contour, all fitted without nails. Ground level holds the contract board, quartermaster's stores, and the Master of the House's desk set into a deep alcove that appeared one spring. Second level: bunks and gear storage. Third: private quarters and map room. Light enters through natural splits in the bark. The wood is warm under every hand that touches it.

The Knothole.description: A round chamber where a great branch joins the trunk, enclosed on all sides with a low opening back into the Heartwood. The League's taproom. A plank bar curves along the far wall. Lanterns hang from rope-slung hooks. The space holds thirty before it gets close. The regulars are the Everwood's working adventurers — rangers, sell-swords, druids between pilgrimages, the occasional elf who won't say which kind. Someone cut a window through to the forest side and the tree allowed it — green light and canopy sounds come through. In winter, the living wood holds warmth without a fire. Regulars swear their drinks stay warmer here than anywhere else in the Everwood.

The Crown.description: Where the trunk opens into the living crown, branches spreading against the sky. The climb up is through the trunk's interior — a stair fitted into the wood's natural spiral. The Greater Owl roost fills the largest branch-hollows, the birds launching from living perches into open air. The portal stone sits in a cradle of branches that the League swears were not in that position when they hauled the stone up. Wind moves through the leaves. The canopy stretches to the horizon in every direction, green and unbroken. The Hearthoak stands taller than its neighbors by just enough to see over them.

Garden.name: Garden
Garden.basicInfo: A wood elf settlement, ancient beyond memory, spread across clearings in the deep Everwood, where homes of stone and wood stand among towering trees. Gardens grow beside wildflowers along the banks of a river that feeds a clear lake. Halflings, humans, and gnomes live here alongside the wood elves. Its name comes from a traditional wood elf proverb: the forest tends its own, and its own tend the Garden.
Garden.hiddenInfo: The wood elves of Garden say that the settlement dates to the Sundering of elvenkind. In the Kindred War, those who would become wood elves refused to fight their brethren. A legendary figure called He Who Walks Beside is said to have guided them to this safe place and then to have perished in its defense. In his memory, wood elves mark births, arrivals and deaths by repeating his final words: 'May you walk in beauty.' Today Garden has no formal leadership — decisions happen by discussion at the Hearth until consensus forms. The forest around Garden is safe — not walled, not warded, just tended. Wood elves are the finest rangers and herbalists in the Everwood and their hospitality is genuine but unhurried. Visitors are fed, given a place to sleep, and left to find their own pace. Those who stay are welcome to stay. The rangers know the deep forest better than anyone alive and are the best source of news about what stirs beneath the canopy.

The Trackless Way.description: One minute, forest. The next, the trees widen, light softens, running water burbles. A tended herb bed here, a woodpile stacked neatly against a trunk there. As if out of nowhere, cottages with smoke rising from them. No one can say exactly where the forest ends and Garden begins.

The Hearth.description: The gathering place at Garden's center, where an ancient oak shades long tables and stone fire pits. Meals are communal and everyone is welcome. A small shrine to the Lady of the Hearth stands where the oak's roots break the surface, tended with wildflowers. Nearby, a carved stone bench marks a shrine to the Lord of the Open Hand.

The Green.description: Garden plots and orchards spread between the trees. Vegetables, herbs, berry bushes, medicinal plants — some cultivated, some wild. Halflings have claimed the best sun-patches and grow things that shouldn't survive this far north. The healers work here, drying herbs on racks and brewing tinctures in an open-air workshop.

Lakeside.description: A clearing where the river opens into a still lake. The water is clear to the bottom, cool and bright. Smooth rocks line the shore. Wood elves swim here at every opportunity.

The Lodge.description: A long timber hall where Garden's rangers gear up before heading out. Bows and traveling cloaks hang from pegs. A map of the region covers one wall, marked with sightings and warnings. The rangers are the village's connection to the world beyond — they bring news, escort travelers, and deal with whatever the forest sends.

Roothold.description: A grove of ancient trees where the eldest wood elves teach whoever wants to learn, for as long as they want to learn it. Druidic arts, old magic, herbcraft, the language of birds, the patterns of weather, the slow deep knowledge of the forest. A few carved benches. A fire circle.

Newgarden.description: A clearing at Garden's edge where a few homes stand and space remains for more.

The Ashfield.name: The Ashfield
The Ashfield.basicInfo: A stretch of blighted ground where nothing grows right. The trees are blackened but alive, the soil is warm to the touch, and ash falls like snow from a sky that's clear everywhere else. A great battle of the Kindred War was fought here and elves of both sides consider it cursed ground, forbidden to enter.
The Ashfield.hiddenInfo: Fire cultists have been seen in the area repeatedly, but when Wardens break taboo to investigate, they find only cold campfires and scorched ritual circles. The cultists always return. The battlefield's curse is real — hundreds of elves died here by fire magic, and the ground remembers. The ash that falls is not natural; it's a magical echo of the battle, endlessly repeating. Fire magic is amplified here and all other magic is suppressed, which is why the fire cult treats it as holy ground. They call it a Crucible — a place where the boundary between the material world and elemental fire thins. Their rituals here are slowly making the effect worse, widening the blighted area year by year. The elven taboo against the place is genuine and deeply felt — both high elves and dark elves lost kin here, and both sides' histories record the battle as shameful. This makes it politically impossible to garrison. The cultists know this and exploit it. They don't live here permanently — they rotate in for rituals and withdraw into the forest or underground. Their camps are designed to be abandoned quickly. The real danger isn't the cultists themselves but what they're working toward: if the Crucible fully opens, the blighted area could spread across a vast stretch of the Everwood.

The Ashfall.description: Where the blight begins. Healthy forest gives way to blackened trunks and warm ground over a few dozen paces. Ash drifts down from nowhere. The air tastes of smoke. Elven ward-markers ring the perimeter — not to keep things in, but to warn their own people away.

The Burning Ground.description: The battlefield's heart, where the earth is bare and cracked and warm enough to feel through boot soles. Scorched ritual circles mark where the cultists work. Old bones surface and re-bury themselves as the ground shifts.

The Scar Pit.description: A crater at the field's center where the killing blow of the battle landed. The air shimmers with heat. The cultists have ringed it with iron stakes driven into the earth, each one carved with fire-script..

Tusker Wallow.name: Tusker Wallow
Tusker Wallow.basicInfo: A boarfolk encampment dug into a muddy hollow. Log palisades ring a churned-earth settlement of rooting pits, forges fed with stolen tools, and pens for the giant boars the Tuskers ride. War-bands range from here, raiding trade roads and settlements for metalwork, weapons, and anything else the Tuskers can't make themselves.
Tusker Wallow.hiddenInfo: The current chief is smarter than most and has held the wallow for three seasons, uniting war-bands with calls for daring raids and great plunder. The Tuskers need a steady supply of iron and steel but are inept at metallurgy and disdain mining. The Tuskers hail from the Wild Grass Sea, from which they were displaced by the defeat of their tribes. They are outsiders here in the Everwood, but regard the elves as weak and the region ripe for conquest. Tusker culture involves challenge customs, gift protocols, the fact that respect is earned by fighting first and talking after. The wallow also sits on a tunnel entrance that goes down into passages that connect to the wider underground network.

The Rut.description: The churned-mud approach to the wallow, where boar-tracks and boot-prints overlap in a wide swath of destroyed undergrowth. Tusker sentries watch from platforms in the trees. The palisade gate is a rough affair of whole logs lashed together.

The Mud Ring.description: The wallow's center, a trampled clearing where Tuskers gather, argue, eat, and settle disputes by fighting. A forge cobbled together from looted parts bangs away constantly — the camp's most prized possession. The chief holds court from a log throne, surrounded by their best fighters.

The Loot Pit.description: A roofed dugout where raided goods are stored and divided. Metal tools, weapons, bolts of cloth, barrels of ale — everything the Tuskers take and can't make.

Mount Dragonseal.name: Mount Dragonseal
Mount Dragonseal.basicInfo: A cave network in the side of a mountain in the deep Everwood where the bones of ancient dragons are said to have been sealed behind wards placed in ancient times by the legendary Suleyvan the Sealer. But now the great seal-stone at the entrance has been shattered — split clean through by something powerful enough to break magic that held for millennia.
Mount Dragonseal.hiddenInfo: The caves were a nesting ground for dragons in an age before elves. When Suleyvan sealed the extraplanar breaches, he also sealed places where draconic power had soaked into the world itself. The Scaled Covenant found this place and broke the seal — one of the few acts that could draw a Harbinger's personal attention. They've established a hidden camp deeper in the caves where recruits undergo transformation by bathing in pools of liquid suffused with ancient draconic power. The caves themselves are dangerous independent of the Covenant — the residual magic has warped the wildlife near the entrance, and deeper in, things that fed on draconic power for millennia are waking up. The broken seal is also a political crisis: Suleyvan's wards are supposed to be unbreakable. If this one fell, others could too.

The Shatterseal.description: The cave mouth, where the two halves of Suleyvan's seal-stone lie cracked apart like a split skull. The carved wards are dark.

The Bone Gallery.description: A long cavern where dragon skeletons are embedded in the walls and ceiling — enormous ribcages arching overhead, skulls the size of carriages jutting from the stone.

The Nests.description: The innermost caves, where the draconic residue is strongest. Pools of dark liquid gather in hollows worn smooth by creatures that slept here for ages.

Whitehill Kobold Warren.name: Whitehill Kobold Warren
Whitehill Kobold Warren.basicInfo: A network of tunnels and crude fortifications dug into a rocky hillside. Kobolds have infested this place for generations, but recently they've become organized — patrols, watch-posts, even a gate.
Whitehill Kobold Warren.hiddenInfo: A goblin expeditionary squad from the Greater Goblinate has taken command of the warren, 'recruiting' the local kobolds into the empire's service. The goblin captain sees the warren as a forward outpost — a foothold in the Everwood far from Goblinate territory. The kobolds are split: some welcome the weapons, training, and sense of purpose; others resent taking orders from outsiders who treat them as expendable labor. The goblins are expanding the tunnels aggressively. One branch is heading toward Sanctuary's Underhalls, though neither the dwarves nor the goblins have broken through yet. The captain has also been sending kobold scouts to map the Everwood's trade roads — preparation for something bigger. The whole operation is small enough to go unnoticed for now, which is exactly how the Goblinate prefers to work.

The Scrabble.description: The hillside approach, where the ground is pocked with small holes and loose rock. Crude snare traps and pit traps line the obvious paths. A reinforced gate — new, and better-built than anything kobolds usually manage — blocks the main tunnel entrance.

The Workings.description: The warren's main hub — a tangle of low tunnels opening into a central cavern where kobolds eat, sleep, and squabble. Goblin-issue crates are stacked against one wall. A crude banner of the Greater Goblinate hangs over the captain's alcove, which is the only space with a proper door.

The Deep Dig.description: The newest tunnels, where kobolds dig under goblin direction. Shored with timber — another new addition. The air is bad and the tunnels branch in several directions.

Sanctuary.name: Sanctuary
Sanctuary.basicInfo: The only major non-elven city in the Everwood. It exists by high elven permission within ancient walls and wards that hold the forest back. Every species and culture in Larion has found a foothold here — dwarves below, Tevantines on the canals, halflings in the eastern hills, elves in the Heartwood where the original forest still stands. Packed tight, built upward and downward for lack of room to sprawl.
Sanctuary.hiddenInfo: Sanctuary exists because the high elves allow it, and not all of them agree that they should. Radicals among the high elves want the Outsiders expelled and the forest reclaimed. The Char - a city block gutted by magefire - may well have been their warning shot, though the Wardens have never proven it. The city is a major hub of the soul-stone trade. Dark elves control the supply from below and their agents move it through Sanctuary's underworld to buyers across Larion.

Eastgate Square.description: A vast plaza just inside the main gate, where the road from Highvale enters the city. Press of bodies, calls of hawkers and criers, chaos of cultures colliding.

Canal Square.description: The heart of the Tevantine quarter, where the main canals converge around a broad stone plaza. Merchants in bright silks haggle from gondolas while warehouses and trading offices line the water's edge.

The Floating World.description: A grand tavern-barge drifting through the canals, its musicians and callers inviting the city aboard. Lanterns sway from silk canopies. Below deck are private cabins.

Forge Row.description: A narrow street of smithies and armories, the clang of hammers constant from dawn to dusk. Dwarven craftsmen and gnome tinkers work side by side. The street slopes toward heavy stone doors carved with clan symbols — the entrance to the Underhalls.

The Underhalls.description: The dwarven quarter beneath the city — stone halls, clan holdings, taverns serving mountain ale. The clans handle their own disputes down here. Deeper passages wind into darkness.

Triumph Square.description: The government heart of Sanctuary, where a Kindred War memorial stands before Council Hall. The Watch Tower looms nearby. Banks and guild halls ring the square.

Goldmount.description: A low hill lined with the tower compounds of Sanctuary's great merchant Houses. Behind iron gates and private guards, these estates contain everything their owners need to avoid mixing with common folk.

The League Chapterhouse.description: Where adventurers register with the League for passage through the Everwood. The great board lists bounties, expeditions seeking escort, and warnings about what's been spotted this halfmoon.

The Heartwood.description: Ancient oaks that were never felled, where the original forest still stands within the walls. This is high elven ground. The Wardens keep their headquarters here beneath the great trees. Outsiders enter only with permission.

The Greenwarren.description: A winding maze of halfling terrace-mounds, each level connected by paths and shortcuts only locals know. Behind the round doors lie trading offices, cozy taverns, and produce markets.

Grave Street.description: Where all quarters meet and laws get thin. A maze of tenements, unlicensed taverns, and back-alley dealings. If something needs to move quietly through Sanctuary, it passes through here.

The Char.description: A whole block gutted by fire. The Watch keeps the streets barricaded. Whatever's living in the ruins, it isn't friendly, and the sounds at night keep the neighboring streets empty after sundown.

The Last Stand Tavern.description: Built into Westgate Tower where Sanctuary's defenders held the line in the Kindred War. Trophies from legendary kills hang beneath the Great Bell, which still rings when something tests the wards. The best tables face windows overlooking the Everwood.

Hillhome.name: Hillhome
Hillhome.basicInfo: A halfling village built into the gentle hills of the Everwood Valley, where round doors and grass-roofed hill-homes peek from every slope and plots of vegetables, herbs and fruit trees fill the dells between them. Kids and dogs run loose and someone is always calling someone else across the lanes or stopping by unannounced. Doors are unlocked or, better yet, open, and not knowing someone's name is downright rude.
Hillhome.hiddenInfo: Hillhome is a halfling-majority village in the Everwood Valley, with a steady minority of humans and elves who've settled in over the years. Halflings here run to large families and longstanding rivalries between them: the Mossbottoms and the Sweetmeads, the Honeycutts and the Tickbottoms, the Greenbottles and everyone else. Most of these rivalries are friendly — bragging at the Fair, friendly insults at the Saucy Chicken, the occasional pie thrown in jest. The exception is the Balewoods and the Goldspoons: the Goldspoons own most of the village mill-rights and lend coin at hard terms, and have been trying to acquire The Saucy Chicken for thirty years. Swapping gossip is an act as natural as breathing; silence for high elves and the dearly departed. The Newmoon Fair is the village's main event, contested in pies, ale, archery, sheep-shearing and singing, with the Balewoods holding the pie-crown for as long as anyone alive can remember. Beyond the village, the Truffle-Woods supply the moonbloom truffles that anchor the local cuisine.

Hill Street.description: The main lane of Hillhome, winding between the hills that the village is built into. Round doors and round windows on every slope, gardens spilling between them.

Hearthhome.description: A cozy hill-home on Hill Street with a bright blue door and window boxes full of flowers. Inside, wooden beams cross the ceiling of the warm living room, while a study nook offers views across the village. The cellar stays cool enough for storing food, and the small garden outside provides fresh herbs.

The Saucy Chicken Tavern.description: The Balewood family's tavern and the heart of the village, where halflings come together in the evening to drink, eat, talk, and sing. A long bar of polished oak, low rafters hung with dried herbs and copper pots, mismatched chairs around mismatched tables, a great hearth that's always lit. Pernilla Balewood's truffle-pies have made the place famous across the Vale.

Manu's Blacksmith Shop.description: A stout stone-and-timber forge built against a hillside, with the living quarters dug into the hill behind. Manu Morganstone — the village's human blacksmith — works the anvil under a wide-eaved roof; tools, harness, mended kettles and the occasional sword hang from pegs along the walls. The forge runs hot most days, and the door is usually open.

Bark and Dandy's Home.description: A modest hill-home on a side lane off Hill Street, with a slightly crooked round door and a garden that has clearly been planted by enthusiastic siblings rather than a careful adult. Bark Balewood and his older sibling Dandy live here; the inside is warm, lived-in, and rarely entirely tidy.

Goldspoon Manor.description: Two hills, joined. Three generations ago, the Goldspoons decided one hill wasn't enough and paid a small army of halfling diggers to bridge the gap, producing the only double-hill home in the Vale. The doors are painted gold, the windows stained glass, and a little pond with imported koi has replaced the garden. A lackered wrought-iron gate is guarded at all hours by the family's servants. The family crest, a spoon rampant on a field of wheat, is carved above the door.

Greenbottle's Goods.description: The village general store, run by Tobin Greenbottle out of a hill-home with the front parlor knocked through and rebuilt as a shopfront. Inside, the shelves are stacked floor to low halfling ceiling with the daily necessities of Vale life: flour and salt, candles and lamp-oil, twine and tallow, jars of preserves with handwritten labels, dried herbs in bundles, sturdy boots in halfling sizes (and a single shelf of bigger boots for other visitors), wool blankets, fishing line, sewing notions, a glass case of small luxuries — sugar lumps, smoked salt, candied ginger from the Spice Isles.

The Fairground.description: A wide grassy meadow at the south edge of the village, ringed by oaks. Most of the month it's a quiet village common — kids practicing archery, dogs chasing each other, the occasional courting couple. Wooden booth-frames sit stacked at one edge. A great oak at the meadow's heart, the Festival Oak, has names and dates carved into its bark. From the Festival Oak you can see the Sleeping Bear's smoke, the lower slopes where Knothole begins, and the willows down by the brook. Come the monthly Newmoon Fair, the field transforms — pavilions, ribbons, contest rings, pie-tables.

The Sleeping Bear Inn.description: A welcoming inn built half into a hillside at the south edge of the village proper, where the lane down from Hill Street meets the open ground of the Fairground. The common room features a massive stone fireplace and comfortable chairs; the innkeeper, Rosie Butterworth, serves excellent meals and keeps a well-stocked cellar. Travelers passing through the Vale lodge here, which makes the Sleeping Bear the part of Hillhome most likely to know what's happening in the wider world.

Mossfoot Brook.description: A clear shallow stream that runs along the south edge of the village, fed by springs in the hills above. Flat stones along the banks have been worn smooth by generations of laundry; a deeper pool at the bend below the willows is where the village kids learn to swim and the village grandfathers fish for stickleback. A simple footbridge crosses where the path comes down from Hill Street. In summer, half the village ends up here at some point in the afternoon. In winter, ice fishing, and the kids slide on the frozen pool.

Knothole.description: The wrong side of the village, if Hillhome has one. Tucked below the Fairground on the lower slopes, where the village proper gives way to weedy gardens and crooked doors, Knothole is a cluster of smaller hill-homes where nobody asks where anyone got their goods from. Halflings of dubious reputation drink on stoops, kids of dubious parentage run errands of dubious legality, and the mead being sold without a license is — most agree — actually quite good. Respectable Hillhome clucks its tongue about Knothole and does not look directly at it.

Honeycutt's Apiary.description: A modest hill-home set above the village on a path winding up through wildflowers, with a row of painted wooden hives along a sun-warmed slope. The bees move in slow purposeful clouds between hives and meadow; an old halfling chair sits in the shade of an apple tree, with a battered walking staff usually leaning beside it.

Withergleam's Cottage.description: A small thatched cottage set apart from the village on the lane that climbs toward the truffle-woods, half-buried in a wildling garden of foxglove, comfrey, mint, and a hundred other plants. Bunches of drying herbs hang from the porch eaves. A worn flagstone path leads to a green door that's left ajar most days.

The Truffle-Woods.name: The Truffle-Woods
The Truffle-Woods.basicInfo: The wooded slopes above Hillhome where the village's truffle-hogs forage. Old oaks and chestnuts, mossy roots, brambles and bracken. Halfling foragers have walked the same paths here for generations, and the woods feel safe enough — at least the parts close to the village.
The Truffle-Woods.hiddenInfo: The Truffle-Woods are the cleared belt of the Everwood that Hillhome treats as its own — bordered uphill by the Wardens' line, beyond which the deep woods begin and outsiders do not go. Tinytrolls have nested in the bracken and steal from foragers.

The Forager's Path.description: A well-worn footpath leading into the woods, where halfling truffle-hunters have walked for generations. The trees thicken as you climb. Old oaks with mossy roots, bracken to the knee

The Truffle Patches.description: Where the truffles grow, if you know how to look — under the old oaks, in the soft earth between root and root. Halfling truffle-hogs have been working this ground for so long the soil itself smells faintly of moonbloom in the right season.

The Bramble Hollow.description: A low dell choked with thorn-brambles, where the path forks and most halflings turn back. The brambles are tall and thick enough to scratch through cloth. Beyond the hollow, the woods grow older and quieter.

The Felled Giant.name: The Felled Giant
The Felled Giant.basicInfo: A Greatheart lies here - one of the sacred trees where druids once communed with the forest's deepest spirits. Something felled it long ago. The trunk stretches longer than a city block, bark like weathered stone, hollow chambers within where the heartwood rotted out. The road passes close enough to see it through the trees. Travelers who stray from the path sometimes don't come back.
The Felled Giant.hiddenInfo: The tree isn't dead. It's been dying for centuries, and its death is hungry. Sap pools have thickened into something toxic - animals that drink become twisted, aggressive, drawn to drag prey back toward the roots. The hollow interior is worse. People who enter feel welcomed, warm, sleepy. They don't leave. The druids could end it but won't destroy a Greatheart, even a ruined one. The League posts warnings and pays hunters to cull the corrupted wildlife. No one's solved the deeper problem.

The Scar.description: Where the road passes closest to the fallen tree. A worn path branches off toward the massive trunk - pilgrims still come despite the warnings, and hunters come despite the pilgrims. League markers warn travelers to stay on the road. From here, the scale becomes real: the trunk rises higher than a house, stretching away into green gloom.

The Hollow.description: A wound in the trunk where the heartwood rotted out, opening into chambers within. It smells sweet inside - too sweet, like overripe fruit. Soft light filters through cracks in the bark. The walls are warm to the touch. Deeper in, the passages branch and descend.

The Roots.description: Where the root ball tore from the earth when the tree fell, leaving a cavern of tangled roots descending into darkness. Pools of thickened sap glow faintly amber. The corrupted creatures lair here - things that were once deer, boar, wolves, now something else. Bones accumulate in the deeper hollows. Some say the roots reach all the way down to the tunnels below.

Serpent's Coil.name: Serpent's Coil
Serpent's Coil.basicInfo: A river bend where the water runs deep and the current coils back on itself. Boats are often attacked here - hulls staved in, crew dragged under, cargo scattered downstream. The river's the fastest route for moving goods out of the Everwood, and the attacks are a constant threat to trade.
Serpent's Coil.hiddenInfo: A river wyrm lairs in the deep pool at the bend - old, territorial, large enough to capsize a barge. It's been here longer than Sanctuary. Ophidians have settled the riverbank, worshipping the wyrm as a god and feeding it offerings - including captives from the boats they help wreck. The attacks aren't random; they're directed. The cult is small but growing, drawing desperate folk who seek transformation. Their leader is far along the change, more serpent than human now, and claims to speak the wyrm's will.

The Wrack.description: A gravel shore where the current deposits debris and wreckage from attacked boats. Splintered hull planks, torn canvas, scattered cargo.

The Deeps.description: The pool at the river's bend, where the water turns black and the current stills. From above, you can see the darkness where the bottom drops away.

The Traitor's Rest.name: The Traitor's Rest
The Traitor's Rest.basicInfo: A burial ground in a hollow where the trees grow thin and wrong. During the Kindred War, elves who broke faith were executed and interred here - oathbreakers from both sides, high elf and dark elf together in unmarked graves. The victors warded the place and forbade anyone to speak of it. The forest has been trying to swallow it ever since. Lately the wards have weakened. The dead are restless.
The Traitor's Rest.hiddenInfo: This is the one place in the Everwood where high elf and dark elf dead share ground - traitors, deserters, those who switched sides or refused to fight. Both factions contributed bodies; neither acknowledges the place exists. The wards were meant to keep the dead contained, not honored. Now they're failing. The spirits that stir aren't mindless - they remember the war, the betrayals, the executions. Some want revenge. Some want to be remembered. Some want the names that were taken from them. The Wardens know the wards are weakening but investigating means admitting the place exists.

The Withered Ring.description: A clearing where the undergrowth dies back and the trees lean away, as if repelled. Ward-stones stand at intervals, carved with faded binding marks. Several have cracked or toppled. The ground is uneven - dozens of graves, unmarked, overgrown. Nothing grows directly over them. At the clearing's center, a stone slab lies broken in two.

The Sunken Crypt.description: Beneath the broken slab, stairs descend into a chamber carved from living rock. This is where they buried the worst of them - or the most important, depending on who's telling the story. Niches in the walls hold remains wrapped in rotting shrouds. Names were chiseled off the stones; you can see where they were.

Canopy.name: Canopy
Canopy.basicInfo: The high elven capital, built among colossal silverleaf trees with the ancient First Tree at its heart. The city rises in tiers connected by winding staircases carved into the trunks. Outsiders are welcome here, though the upper reaches are elven domain.
Canopy.hiddenInfo: The Council of Elf-Lords governs the Everwood. They are divided on the dark elf question: hawks want the Sundering enforced more harshly, doves seek reconciliation, and the majority holds to containment and vigilance. The Wardens who enforce high elven law throughout the Everwood answer directly to the Council. Elves at the lower levels are accustomed to outsiders and many find them amusing - particularly their fear of heights, which elves consider endearing. The staircases are safe but exposed, and the drops are real. Elves move through their city with effortless grace and would never be so clumsy as to fall. Outsiders who show courage, usefulness, or genuine respect for elven ways are welcome. Those who cause trouble are simply escorted down and out.

Greenshade.description: The ground level of Canopy, where the silverleaf trunks rise like pillars and the light filters down green-gold through the city above. A market fills the spaces between the great trees - elven craftwork, forest goods, food stalls serving honeycakes and smoked game. Lodging houses cater to visitors. The staircases begin here, winding up the trunks into the branches.

The Silver Bough.description: A tavern and inn built where three silverleaf trunks grow close together, their branches forming a natural platform a hundred feet up. The common room wraps around all three trunks, open-sided. The drinks are excellent. The company is better - off-duty Wardens, traders from the Understory, elven artisans, and the occasional outsider who's earned a regular seat. The best source of news and rumor in the city.

The Warden Hall.description: Headquarters of the Wardens, built around a massive branch of the First Tree. Armories, planning chambers, quarters for Wardens between assignments. A board posts current concerns throughout the Everwood - beast sightings, border incidents, disturbances that need investigating.
