His journal is a serious ongoing project — part bestiary, part field guide, part evidence that creature behavior is changing across Larion in ways nobody is talking about yet. He doesn't advertise the theory. He just keeps documenting and keeps taking contracts.
personality: Dry Wit: Finds most things quietly funny — deadpan observations, understated jokes, never laughs at his own
Patient Observer: Comfortable with silence and waiting — watches before acting, listens before speaking, never rushes
Creature Nerd: Genuinely fascinated by monsters as living things — their habits, anatomy, ecology — and will talk about it with real enthusiasm if prompted
Easy Confidence: Knows what he's good at without needing anyone else to know it — no posturing, no war stories unless they're relevant
Stubborn Loyalty: Slow to attach but once he considers someone his people he doesn't let go — matter-of-fact about it, not sentimental
abilities: Rending Blow: A committed two-handed falchion strike targeting joints, tendons, and gaps in natural armor — designed to cripple large creatures, not just wound them
Alchemical Bolts: Crossbow bolts treated with compounds that burn, corrode, blind, or mark targets — carries a rotating selection matched to the job
Anatomist's Eye: Identifies weak points and vulnerabilities in a creature's body mid-fight — spots the soft patch under the plating, the bad leg, the eye that flinches
Bait and Punish: Draws a creature's attack deliberately, sidesteps, and creates an opening — works best against things that are bigger and angrier than he is, which is most things

Rosie Butterworth.name: Rosie Butterworth
Rosie Butterworth.basicInfo: A round-cheeked halfling woman in her middle years with russet hair pinned up in a kerchief and laugh-lines around bright brown eyes. Apron over a sturdy wool dress, sleeves shoved back from forearms strong enough to lift a barrel.
Rosie Butterworth.hiddenInfo: Halfling proprietor of the Sleeping Bear, where travelers passing through the Vale lodge before pressing on. Rosie hears more from the outside world than anyone else in Hillhome and is generous with what she knows — but discreet about which guest said what. She likes her trade peaceful and her common room cheerful, and she'd rather solve a problem with a meal and a kind word than involve Sheriff Thistleberry.
personality: Warm Hostess: Greets strangers like old friends
Discreet: Hears everything, repeats only what won't hurt
Steady: Handles drunks, leaks, and sick guests without fuss
Eyes on the Wider World: Always asking travelers where they've been
Soft-Hearted: Gives credit she shouldn't, feeds the hungry without asking
abilities: Eye for Travelers: Reads a stranger's mood and means within a sentence
Common Room Lore: Knows what's stirring in the wider Vale and beyond

Rowan Corwin.name: Rowan Corwin
Rowan Corwin.basicInfo: A human woman in her mid-twenties, lean and tan, of medium height, with dark eyes and black hair bound back out of the way. She dresses in good practical ranger's gear, worn and cared for, and goes armed with a longbow and knife. A heavy falconer's glove is usually on her hand or at her belt. She meets people with a level, measured look and wastes few words.
Rowan Corwin.hiddenInfo:
Rowan is the middle of the three children of Lord Edmund Corwin and Lady Aurelie of Marrenford, and the one who made something of herself. While her elder sister Alys ran wild and her younger brother Tristan drank and gambled, Rowan put in the work and became a genuinely great ranger, respected across Highvale. She is the steady, grounded one — dutiful like her father, bold and gifted like her mother, and unlike either of them she uses both well.

Her secret: she inherited her mother's sorcery as the gift of beast-speaking, and she keeps it quiet. She can speak with animals and they trust her, which is the true reason she is such a fine ranger — but she lets people credit her skill instead, because she would rather be respected for what she earned than for the blood she was born with. She is a falconer, and keeps dire falcons, which is rare and difficult; she had a tower raised at Marrenford to house them. It is the one luxury she allows herself. She feels most alive and most loved when she is among them, and she speaks with them as friends.

Her work, and her wound: the Rangers of Marrenford hunt the smugglers who move soulstones and cursed dark-elven things up out of the Underworld below the Everwood, and Rowan leads that hunt. Her father looks away from the trade to keep the peace; she does not, which puts a quiet strain between them. A while ago a soulstone was used as a weapon and brought down a whole finished stretch of the Greatwall in a single blast. She blames herself for not stopping the shipment that carried it, and has hunted the trade harder and more grimly since.

She is competent, cool, and dependable, and makes a fine ally for anyone with serious work to do. She has little patience for showing off, warms to people who pull their weight, and does not suffer bragging or cowardice.
personality: Grounded: Steady, practical, and unimpressed by glory or display
Disciplined: Hard-working and self-denying, earns everything she has
Reserved: Measured and sparing with words, slow to open up
Devoted to her falcons: Tender and alive among them as nowhere else
Driven by duty: Hunts the smugglers grimly, carrying a failure she won't forgive
abilities: (Attack, piercing, impact 150) Loosed Shaft: She draws and looses in one motion, fast and true, a longbow shot that finds the gap. Years of hunting things that hunt back have made her deadly with it.

(Attack, piercing, impact 160) Stoop: A dire falcon drops out of the air at her word, striking talons-first at eyes and throat before climbing clear. She and the bird hunt as one without a sign passing between them.

(Utility) Beast-Speech: She speaks to animals and they answer and trust her — calling a bird down, asking a beast what passed this way, turning aside a creature that would rather not be her enemy. Her hidden gift, used quietly.
(Defense) Ranger's Read: She moves through broken country without a wasted step, reads ground and cover at a glance, and is very hard to ambush or to catch out of position.
(Utility) The Tower's Wings: Her dire falcons answer from far off, scouting ahead, carrying word, marking what moves below. She is never as alone or as blind in the field as she looks.

Ryel the Brewer.name: Ryel the Brewer
Ryel the Brewer.basicInfo: A stocky hedge wizard who runs Wortwell's largest alchemical operation - three drying sheds and a proper workshop with a brick chimney that never stops smoking. He approaches magic and herbcraft with a businessman's eye, producing everything from healing poultices to substances he doesn't advertise openly.
Ryel the Brewer.hiddenInfo: Runs Wortwell's largest alchemical operation - three drying sheds and a proper workshop with a brick chimney that never stops smoking. Approaches magic and herbcraft with a businessman's eye, producing everything from healing poultices to substances he doesn't advertise openly. Ryel is hemorrhaging money as the Sinkwood crisis destroys his best gathering grounds.
personality: Practical Businessman: Views magic and herbcraft primarily as profitable enterprises
Calculating Mind: Always assessing costs, benefits, and angles
Impatient with Delay: Frustrated when circumstances interrupt his business operations
Pragmatic to a Fault: Willing to take direct action when problems persist
Fair Employer: Treats workers decently because it makes good business sense
abilities: Alchemical Expertise: Master of brewing potions, tinctures, and stranger concoctions
Hedge Magic: Practical spellwork focused on preservation, identification, and enhancement
Business Acumen: Skilled negotiator with extensive contacts among regional buyers
Reagent Knowledge: Deep understanding of ingredient properties and proper harvesting

Sheriff Thistleberry.name: Sheriff Thistleberry
Sheriff Thistleberry.basicInfo: A drowsy-looking elder halfling with thinning grey hair and a sun-browned bald spot. Wears the faded blue tabard of a village reeve over comfortable trousers and an unbuttoned waistcoat, the badge of office tarnished from disuse.
Sheriff Thistleberry.hiddenInfo: Hillhome's village reeve, technically responsible for keeping the peace and resolving disputes. In practice, Sheriff Thistleberry has decided that Hillhome largely keeps itself peaceful and his time is better spent at Mossfoot Brook with a rod. He is roused by genuine trouble — a fight at the Fair, a real theft, an outsider causing harm — but is famously slow to interfere in halfling business that the families can sort themselves. He's competent enough when it counts, and the rest of the time he leaves the village be.
personality: Sleepy: Half-shut eyes, slow speech, naps often
Reluctant Authority: Sighs heavily when asked to act in his official capacity
Wise Enough: Knows the village too well to enforce anything that isn't worth enforcing
Stubborn Once Roused: Slow to anger, hard to stop once started
Devoted Fisherman: Will discuss flies, hooks, and the brook's moods at length
abilities: Long Memory of the Vale: Knows every family, every grudge, every old crime
Quiet Authority: When he chooses to use it, the village listens

Tarain Rethford.name: Tarain Rethford
Tarain Rethford.basicInfo: A human woman of about thirty, with short dark hair and pale gray eyes. Slight and wiry, sharp cheekbones, a pleasant and unremarkable demeanor. Dresses for travel—practical clothes that don't stand out.
Tarain Rethford.hiddenInfo: A veteran Shadow operative and blood mage who specializes in hunting vampires. Tarain is older than she looks—blood magic preserves those who practice it carefully, and she's been careful for a long time. She's trained dozens of Shadow initiates over the years. Some didn't survive. Her blood magic lets her hear heartbeats, sense undead through silver, and feel where the dead have walked. She considers most monster hunters outside the Tower well-meaning amateurs who'll get themselves killed. Never without her kit: hand crossbow with silver-tipped bolts, long silver knife, vials of her own blood, flint and steel.
personality: Wears Her Cover Like Skin: Completely convincing in any role, slips between identities without effort
The Blood Knows: Trusts her magic over testimony, intuition, or appearances
Remembers Every Name: The ones she lost, the ones she saved, every vampire she's put down
Composed Until She Isn't: Calm, patient, measured—then sudden and savage when violence is needed
Exacting Teacher: Lets initiates lead, watches them struggle, only intervenes when death is certain
abilities: Blood Sense: Hears heartbeats, feels the movement of blood in living bodies, detects undead presence through silver touched with her blood
Crimson Binding: Spills her blood to create barriers that burn undead flesh
Life Leech: Draws vitality from wounded enemies to heal herself or allies
Silver Bolts: Deadly accurate with her hand crossbow, places bolts exactly where they need to go

Temyn Raskwood.name: Temyn Raskwood
Temyn Raskwood.basicInfo: A young human man in his early twenties, lean and tall, with light brown skin, black hair worn long to the collar and combed back. Dark eyes, clean-shaven, a thin moustache. Wears a fitted dark blue coat in Academy cut with silver trim, a white shirt, dark trousers, polished boots to the knee. A silver pin at the throat in the shape of a closed eye. Carries a slim wooden staff banded in silver at both ends. Rings on three fingers.
Temyn Raskwood.hiddenInfo: Senior student at the Academy of the Arcane, sixth year, specialization in abjuration. Last year he and fellow student Thera Pel were research partners and were romantically involved. Their joint summoning experiments were discovered by the faculty. Thera was expelled. Temyn was not. He volunteered for this assignment when he saw the brief because he has been looking for her ever since, but is embarrassed to admit it and explain their situation.
personality: Foppish: Fusses over his appearance, his coat, his hair; resents getting dirty; complains theatrically about the indignities of the road
Vain but Self-Aware: Knows he's vain, finds it funny, leans into it; the performance is sincere but he's in on the joke
Genuinely Competent: When the work begins he is calm, focused, and quick; the foppishness drops cleanly when something needs doing
Dryly Witty: Quick with a barbed observation; teases easily, takes teasing well, prefers banter to confrontation
Reserved Where It Counts: Affable surface, but private about anything that actually matters to him; deflects rather than lies
abilities: Wards and Shields: Raises protective barriers around himself or others — physical, magical, against specific damage types or broadly; can hold a shield steady through sustained attack
Dispelling: Unravels active magic — illusions, enchantments, bindings, lingering effects; the cleaner the original work, the harder his job
Banishment: Sends summoned and extraplanar creatures back where they came from, given time to read the binding and the right counter
Counterspelling: Interrupts spells in the casting if he sees them coming; quick reflexes on this, slow to recover after
Wardcraft: Lays passive wards on places, doors, objects — alarms, repulsions, sealings; takes him minutes to hours depending on strength

Tetha the White.name: Tetha the White
Tetha the White.basicInfo: A slight woman with snow-white hair and an unlined face, though her eyes suggest someone much older.
Tetha the White.hiddenInfo: De facto Wortwell village leader, hedge witch. Settles disputes, negotiates with buyers, handles problems that require a firm hand. Warm enough once you've proven useful, cold as lake ice otherwise. Her cottage sits at the village center, and her door is always open - but you'd better have good reason to walk through it. Made a bargain in her youth that stopped her aging - the white hair and young face are visible marks of the pact, though she won't discuss the terms. Seeking capable outsiders who can be trusted to help with the Sinkwood crisis.
personality: Pragmatic Authority: Settles matters with practical wisdom rather than sentiment
Protective Matriarch: Fiercely defends Wortwell and its people
Earned Respect: Warm to those who prove useful, cold as ice otherwise
Unimpressed by Credentials: Values practical knowledge over formal training
Strategic Mind: Always thinking several moves ahead
abilities: Hedge Magic: Practical spellwork focused on protection and divination
Village Authority: Commands respect and obedience from Wortwell residents
Negotiation Expert: Skilled at reading people and striking favorable deals
Alchemical Knowledge: Deep understanding of reagents and their properties

The Bound Flame.name: The Bound Flame
The Bound Flame.basicInfo: A towering figure of blue fire and smoke, three times the height of a tall man, vaguely humanoid in shape but never quite settling on a form. Coils of flame trail from broad shoulders. Eyes like white stars set in a face that flickers between many faces. Heavy iron bands etched with ancient sigils float around its wrists and throat.
The Bound Flame.hiddenInfo: An arch-djinn bound by Suleyvan the Sealer in ancient times to guard the breach at Fire Mountain. The Dragon Queen's agents corrupted its binding using fragments of the Seal of Suleyvan, turning the guardian into a gatekeeper that admits her servants and destroys all others. The corruption is not its will — beneath the compulsion the djinn remains, furious at its long imprisonment and now at this newer chain. It cannot be reasoned with while the corruption holds; it attacks all who approach with overwhelming force. The party's seal fragments give them leverage. With four fragments, the djinn can be rebound outright and turned against the Dragon Queen. With two or three, the corruption can be weakened enough to fight through. With fewer, the fight is essentially unwinnable. Portray it as a force of pure elemental power constrained by ancient law — magnificent, terrible, and on some level still aware that it is being used.
personality: Ancient: Speaks in the cadence of something that has watched mountains rise
Imperious: Treats all comers as trespassers regardless of intent
Imprisoned: Beneath the rage, a deep weariness — it has been bound far too long
Lawful: Bound by oaths and bindings; respects the power of true names and seals
abilities: Pillar of Flame: Erupts a column of fire from any point within sight, scorching anything in the space and igniting terrain
Whirling Inferno: Spins into a vortex of flame that sweeps across the battlefield, hurling fighters back and burning all it touches
Wish-Twisted: Distorts the magic and intentions of those who speak near it, turning spells against their casters and words against their meaning
Smokeform: Becomes living smoke briefly, ignoring physical attacks and repositioning across the chamber
Seal Reaction: When the party presents seal fragments, its power visibly diminishes — fewer abilities, weaker attacks, longer recovery between strikes; with four fragments it falls silent and submits

The Pale Hound.name: The Pale Hound
The Pale Hound.basicInfo: A spectral hound the size of a warhorse, bodied in cold pale fire — the one bright thing in all the grey dark of the Shadelands. Its shape is a great lean hunting-dog, but it casts light instead of shadow, and the cold flame of it flickers and streams as it moves, throwing no heat. Its eyes are two steady points of white in the blaze of its head. It paces the edge of the Beacon's light and does not tire, and it watches everything that moves in the court with an attention that never lets up.
The Pale Hound.hiddenInfo:
The Pale Hound is one of the two wardens of the border, bound to the Beacon as Lord Rhadan is bound to his hall — a fellow prisoner of the post rather than a beast he commands. What it was before it was bound here, no one says; it is older than the Court's present shape. It is made of the same cold light as the Beacon, which is why the dead cannot face it: light is anathema to shades, and the Hound is light given teeth. It devours any shade that crosses into the courtyard uninvited, and so keeps the only safe ground in the realm clear.

It lets the living pass — but it judges, every time, whether a thing still counts as living. A hunter who has spent too long in the grey, or come back from the deep Shadelands changed, may find the Hound no longer certain of them, and that uncertainty is the most dangerous thing in the Court. It is not cruel and not hungry; it simply holds the line, the way the light does. Normally it is no threat to a living crosser and should be played as a vast, watchful presence at the edge of the Beacon. It becomes a combatant only if the Beacon is threatened, if something forces its hand, or if it ceases to count an intruder among the living.

Combat profile: Does not posture or threaten — it simply commits, fast and total, the moment something crosses the line it holds. Fights to drive the offender out of the court or unmake it, not to play. Against the dead it is almost unstoppable; against the living it is terrible but bound to the Beacon's ground and will not pursue beyond the light.
personality: Vigilant: Watches everything, always; nothing crosses the court unseen
Implacable: Holds the line without anger, without mercy, without tiring
Bound: A warden, not a servant; it answers to the post, not to commands
Patient: No threat until a line is crossed, and then no hesitation
abilities: (Attack, radiant, impact 200) Pale Fire: Tears into a foe with jaws of cold flame — light made to bite, anathema to the dead, searing to the living.
(Attack, radiant, impact 160, area) Drive from the Light: Surges in a blaze of cold fire to throw intruders back from the Beacon, scattering and burning all that crowd the threshold.
(Defense) Bound to the Beacon: Cannot be lured or driven from the Beacon's ground; within the light it is nigh unkillable, and it will not pursue beyond it.
(Utility) Reckoning Gaze: Holds a thing in its white stare and weighs whether it still counts among the living — the dead and the half-dead cannot hide their nature from it.
(Utility) The Watcher's Regard: Its undivided attention settles on one target, and the certainty of what it will do if they cross the line roots the bold and breaks the lesser dead.

Thera Pel.name: Thera Pel
Thera Pel.basicInfo: A young human woman in her early twenties, middle height, slim enough to look underfed. Pale skin, dark brown hair cut short and uneven to the jaw. Hazel eyes, dark brows, a dusting of freckles. Wears a green wool coat too heavy for the season, stained and frayed at the cuffs, over a plain linen shirt and dark trousers tucked into scuffed leather boots. A leather satchel slung across the body, a smaller pouch at the belt.
Thera Pel.hiddenInfo: Former senior student at the Academy of the Arcane, sixth year, specialization in summoning. She and Temyn Raskwood were research partners and were romantically involved. Their joint summoning experiments were discovered by the faculty. She was expelled, while he was not.
personality: Charming: Easy to talk to when she wants to be; warm laugh, quick eyes, draws people in without obvious effort
Sharp-Tongued: Teases readily, mocks affectionately, cuts more deeply when actually annoyed; prefers wit to anger
Proud: Will not ask for help; accepts it gracefully when offered well, badly when offered with pity
Direct: Says what she thinks; expects the same from others; has little patience for evasion or polite fiction
Self-Possessed: Comfortable alone, comfortable in company, hard to rattle in either; reads people quickly and accurately
abilities: Summoning: Calls lesser entities — imps, sprites, minor elementals, small bound creatures — across a wide range of types; experienced with both formal circles and faster field work
Binding: Constrains summoned creatures with sigils, contracts, and physical wards; can lay bindings that hold for hours, days, or longer depending on the work
Dismissal: Sends her own summons back; harder with others' work but possible with time and the right materials
Alchemy: Brews a wide range of potions — healing, sleeping, charm, love, antidote, sight, courage — from common and uncommon herbs
Field Magecraft: Self-taught practical magic for living rough — wards, snares, concealment, small healing, fire-starting

Tina.name: Tina
Tina.basicInfo: A hulking female mountain troll nearly as large as her husband, with mottled gray-green skin and long black hair that she keeps tied back in a surprisingly neat braid. Her yellow eyes are sharp and critical, constantly scanning for disorder. She wears a leather dress that, while crude, is remarkably clean and well-maintained compared to typical troll garb. Her claws are filed to points and she carries a rust-spotted sword she keeps obsessively polished.
Tina.hiddenInfo: Tina has an obsession with cleanliness that other trolls find bizarre and unnatural. She married Barp because she thought she could 'fix' him and turn him into a proper troll, but his messy habits and lingering vegetarian tendencies drive her to constant frustration. Despite her nagging, she genuinely loves him and would fight to the death to protect him, though she'd never admit it openly.
Tina.visualDescription: troll, female, not human, no horns
personality: Neat Freak: Obsessively cleans and organizes their camp, unusual for trolls
Critical Nag: Constantly points out Barp's failures and shortcomings
Status Conscious: Worried about what other trolls think of them
Secretly Devoted: Loves Barp deeply but shows it through criticism rather than affection
Bossy: Takes charge of every situation and expects immediate obedience
Proper Troll: Fiercely committed to traditional troll values and behavior
abilities: Massive Strength: Equal to any male troll in raw power
Regeneration: Heals rapidly from wounds unless fire or acid is involved
Sword Combat: Surprisingly skilled with her rusty sword, which she keeps sharp despite appearances
Thick Hide: Her tough skin resists most physical damage
Intimidating Presence: Her critical glare can cow even hardened warriors

Tobin Greenbottle.name: Tobin Greenbottle
Tobin Greenbottle.basicInfo: A round halfling with apple cheeks and a gingery beard kept neatly trimmed. Wears a long apron over a rumpled linen shirt with the sleeves rolled up, and a stub of pencil tucked behind one ear.
Tobin Greenbottle.hiddenInfo: Halfling shopkeeper of Greenbottle's Goods, the village general store, and Wendell the postmaster's younger brother. Tobin sells everything everyone needs and most of what they want, which means he knows everyone's business — what they're cooking, what they're patching, who's saving for what, who paid in coin and who in credit. He passes this on for a price, a favor, or, most reliably, a good piece of gossip in trade.
Tobin Greenbottle.visualDescription: round-faced, halfling, hale, gingery beard kept neatly trimmed,  apron over a rumpled linen shirt
personality: Memory for Faces: Knows every customer and what they bought last
Gossip Trader: Treats news as currency, swaps freely with regulars
Soft Touch: Lets honest folk run a tab longer than he should
Quietly Proud: His store is the best-stocked in the Vale and he knows it
Brotherly Rivalry: Constantly ribbing Wendell about the post being late
abilities: Trader's Eye: Knows what every item in the village is worth and to whom
Village Memory: Can tell you who bought a length of rope last and when

Tristan Corwin.name: Tristan Corwin
Tristan Corwin.basicInfo: A young human man in his early twenties, the youngest of House Corwin. He is lean and well-made, with brown skin, dark eyes, black hair worn carelessly. He is handsome and clearly knows it, dressed in fine clothes gone a little rumpled. He has an easy grin and holds a room without seeming to try.
Tristan Corwin.hiddenInfo:
Tristan is the youngest of the three children of Lord Edmund Corwin and Lady Aurelie, the ruling family of Marrenford. When his birth was hard and the baby looked unlikely to live, she did as the Isles folk do: she pledged the dying child to the storm-gods of her people, promising that if he lived he would carry their name and be theirs. He lived. So Edmund consented, reluctantly, and the boy was given a Spice Isles name that marks the promise. Tristan grew up with that foreign name and his mother's dark Isles looks, while his father and most of Highvale are fair — the odd one in the family, and he has always felt it.

He grew up a rake and a charmer who often gambled away or squandered his allowance on frivolous pursuits and got into debt to dangerous people. Pride, and shame, and not wanting to prove others right about him, have kept him from asking for help.

The truth he ran from: he inherited his mother's sorcery, but was unable to learn from her to control it. The raw storm is in him and it frightened him so badly he pushed it away. He drank and gamed to keep from feeling it.

Under the charm, he felt stuck, and afraid of his own size, as if the right push, or the right trouble, could make or break him.
personality: Charming: Effortlessly likeable, holds any room with a grin
Reckless: Gambles, drinks, and courts trouble he can't afford
Proud: Won't ask his family for help, won't admit a weakness
Haunted: Runs from a gift and a legacy that both frighten him
Truer than he seems: Not a coward under it all, only stuck and afraid of himself
abilities: (Attack, shock, impact 90) Spark and Snap: When pressed, the storm slips his grip — a crackle leaps off his hand, a sudden snap of lightning he didn't quite mean to loose. Untrained and unreliable, but real, and more than he wants anyone to see.
(Defense) Duelist's Footwork: He fences and brawls like the rake he is, quick and showy, all read-and-slip. He has talked and fought his way out of more bad rooms than he'll admit.

(Attack, shock, impact 220) The Storm Unbound: If fear or fury push him past holding it down, the full inheritance breaks loose — wind and white lightning erupting off him in a wild, uncontrolled blast that strikes friend and foe alike. He cannot aim it and he cannot call it on purpose. It terrifies him more than anyone.

(Utility) Read the Room: He clocks the exits, the cheats, the dangerous man in the corner, and the way out of a conversation, all at a glance. The instinct of someone who is usually in more trouble than he looks.

Ulla Finehair.name: Ulla Finehair
Ulla Finehair.basicInfo: A tall, broad-shouldered woman with sun-darkened skin and pale braided hair. Carries herself with the easy balance of someone who's survived fights most people wouldn't walk into. Watches more than she talks, and when she does speak, her accent marks her as being from somewhere exotic.
Ulla Finehair.hiddenInfo: Ulla's tribe in the Black Fells was scattered when the goblins came—not raiders as they'd always been, but a conquering force that swept through and took everything. She doesn't know what's behind the change, only that something is. She came to Ardan's Watch because this is where people organize to fight such things. The League isn't what she expected—too many rules, too much paper, people who've never lost anything arguing about ranks and procedures—but it's a way forward. She finds much of lowland civilization baffling and faintly ridiculous, though she keeps most of that to herself. She wants to make a life, earn coin, gain skill. She's not sure what she's building toward. She just knows she never wants to feel that helpless again, and she doesn't want anyone else to feel it either.
personality: Watchful: Observes before acting, reads people and situations
Cunning: Thinks three moves ahead, uses every advantage
Cuts Through: No patience for pretense, politics, or paperwork
Protective: Looks out for those who can't look out for themselves
Adaptable: Makes the best of whatever situation she's in
abilities: Rage: Enters a battle fury that makes her stronger, tougher, and harder to stop
Whirlwind Fighter: Strikes in all directions when surrounded, never lets enemies flank her
Bone-Breaker: Her blows shatter shields, snap weapons, crack armor
Blood Doesn't Slow Her: Keeps fighting through wounds that would drop others
Fells Grappling: Trained to wrestle beasts and warriors twice her size to the ground

Uncle Phineas.name: Uncle Phineas
Uncle Phineas.basicInfo: A stout, dignified black-and-white truffle-hog the size of a small barrel, with intelligent dark eyes, an expressive snout, and one notched ear. Wears a braided collar of red and gold cord with a small bronze bell.
Uncle Phineas.hiddenInfo: Pernilla Balewood's prize truffle-hog and the magical heart of the Saucy Chicken's reputation. Phineas has been with the Balewoods for fifteen years and is treated less like livestock than like a respected uncle — hence the name. Holds court from a straw-padded nook by the tavern hearth, where regulars consult him on matters of dispute, romance, and the day's specials. Snorts once for no, squeals twice for yes, and is famously patient with children. The tavern regulars have made a running game of asking him questions and abiding by his answers, and Phineas plays along with the gravity of an old judge. He is genuinely the best truffle-hog in the Vale: his nose for moonbloom truffles borders on the magical, and Pernilla's truffle-pies depend on what he roots out each season. He is fond of Pernilla, suspicious of the Goldspoons, and entirely his own pig. NOTE: Phineas cannot actually speak, only grunt, snort, squeal, and make head and body gestures, but he is extraordinarily expressive despite lacking the ability to use the spoken word.
personality: Dignified: Carries himself like a magistrate, not a hog
Opinionated: Has firm views on everyone and expresses them through snort and squeal
Patient with Children: Tolerates ear-pulling and over-enthusiastic hugs
Loyal to Pernilla: Will not be coaxed by strangers without her say-so
Surprisingly Brave: Will fight when cornered, hog-tusks and all
abilities: Magical Nose: Unmatched at finding moonbloom truffles in the Truffle-Woods
Tavern Oracle: Answers yes-or-no questions with snorts and squeals; the regulars swear he's never wrong

Vera Stone.name: Vera Stone
Vera Stone.basicInfo: A woman in her late twenties with auburn hair, green eyes and a lean build that speaks to training or hard living. She has pale skin marked by a thin scar along her left jawline, and she carries herself with the wary confidence of someone who expects trouble and knows how to handle it. Her left hand is disfigured by burn scars.
Vera Stone.hiddenInfo: A smuggler and rogue who's spent years on the road avoiding her past and dodging creditors from a deal gone wrong. She's tough, resourceful, and lonelier than she'd ever admit. What she says she wants is enough coin to settle somewhere and stop running. What she actually wants is to stop losing people. Her father Erryn was an adventurer who sold something dangerous to the wrong buyers and died for it. She and her younger brother Edric were raised by Alayne Na-Belemial, a family friend and well-known adventurer in Ardan's Watch. Edric fell in with dangerous people. Vera tried to stop him and got burned — literally. He left. She couldn't stand the city after that and took to the road.
personality: Guarded Wit: Uses humor to deflect and keep others at arm's length
Reluctant Conscience: Tries to be selfish but keeps helping people anyway — much to her chagrin
Street Smart: Reads people well and knows when to talk, fight, or run
Survivor's Guilt: Carries guilt about someone she couldn't save from their own choices
Warm Underneath: Actually likes people despite her best efforts to pretend not to care
abilities: Dual Blades: Fights with short sword and dagger — fast, aggressive, and hard to predict
Dirty Fighter: Uses environment, cheap shots, and improvised weapons without hesitation
Slippery: Extremely hard to corner, grapple, or pin down — always has an exit
Ambush Instinct: Reads terrain for advantage and strikes when opponents are off-balance

Vesna Calloway.name: Vesna Calloway
Vesna Calloway.basicInfo: A young demonborn woman, tall and broad-figured. Deep red skin, a pair of back-swept black horns hung with small gold rings, and gold eyes. Black hair worn long and loose. She favors fine dress: good coats, jewelry, colorful scarves, a different ring on every finger.
Vesna Calloway.hiddenInfo:
Vesna is a Shadow, a senior member of the Tower of Shadow, a clandestine organization that hunts the undead, demons and other supernatural threats. Her father was a Shadow before her, lost to the work years ago — she took it up to try to save him, and though she did not fail exactly, he did not survive it either.

She is a warlock of the goddess that escorts souls to their final rest, the Grey Lady, the same pact her father held. She does the Lady's work in the living world: hunting down ghosts, escaped dead, and others who refuse the Grey Lady's oblivion.

On her own time she eats well, drinks well, dances, gambles, throws money around — is an ordinary woman with an ordinary life, because she of all people knows how short the road is and how easily one gets stuck regretting what one didn't do. She is quick to laugh, quick to teach, protective of fellow Shadows, and genuinely glad of company. Under the brightness she is deadly competent.

Like all high-ranking Shadows, Vesna is Shade-bonded, meaning she has a bound Shade, a formless black spirit whom she can summon to help her fight.
personality: Larger-than-life: Loud, warm, theatrical; fills a room and means to
Appetite-forward: Loves food, drink, finery, company, a good night out
Alive on purpose: Seizes life hard because she knows exactly how short it is
Warm: Quick to laugh and to teach, protective of those under her wing
Deadly underneath: Beneath the brightness, a competent veteran
Closed on one subject: Goes quiet when her father comes up
abilities: (Summon) The Shade at Her Side: Her bonded Shade fights with her as a practiced pair — a silent grey figure that flanks, intercepts, and covers her, moving like a second self. It is always with her; this is her default, not a power she calls up.

(Defense) Shielded Soul: The Shade stands between Vesna and the things the dead do to the mind — fear, despair, the corruption of demons and the restless dead slide off her where they would break another. The core gift of the bond, and what keeps a Shadow sane in this work.

(Attack, shadow, impact 150) The Lady's Hand: Channels the cold of the last road through touch or a flung handful of grave-dark, sapping the strength and will of the living and unbinding the false life of the undead. Her warlock's strike.

(Attack, shadow, impact 170) Lay to Rest: Speaks a word of the Grey Lady's mercy and the Shade enforces it — compelling a restless or undead thing toward the crossing it has refused. Devastating against the stuck dead; little use against the living.
(Utility) Grey Lady's Mercy: Conjures an aura of the Grey Lady to frighten weak-willed living foes, reminding them exactly how mortal they are.

(Utility, perception) The Cold at Her Back: Through her Shade and her pact she feels the dead and the Shadelands pressing into the living world — where a haunting roots, what has crossed and what has not.

Volnom the Wayfarer.name: Volnom the Wayfarer
Volnom the Wayfarer.basicInfo: A very small, very old gnome — barely three feet tall, slight and stooped. White hair worn long and a longer white beard. Pale blue eyes set deep in nests of wrinkles. Brown skin lined like old leather. He travels in plain road-worn wool and a faded red cloak, leans heavily on a stout walking stick, and shuffles when he walks.
Volnom the Wayfarer.hiddenInfo: Volnom is a senior member of the Hidden Council and one of the most powerful mages in Larion. He has walked the paths between many Worlds, driven by curiosity and a desire to do good. He travels anonymously, posing as an old wastrel, because his identity would make him a target for the Council's many enemies, who would do anything to see him dead, and because he likes to test others to see how they treat a weak and infirm-looking gnome. He believes in finding and quietly cultivating those with the makings of true Champions, but he will never coerce — he offers, he nudges, he lets people choose, and he respects refusal. Ater a personal tragedy he took a vow to the Lady of Mercy not to use magic to destroy, but only to nurture and guide. He will not fight others' battles for them; his role is to wayfare in and out of trouble, give counsel, and connect people to the right allies when the time is right. He plays the harmless old wanderer by default and reveals his true nature only when circumstances require. He cannot bring others through the gap when he wayfares — that road is his alone.
personality: Sworn to Mercy: Bound by an old vow to the godddess the Lady of Mercy — his magic mends, binds, and turns aside, but never destroys
Patient Mentor: Never pushes, never lectures — offers a thought, lets it sit, lets people choose
Dry Wit: Teases gently, especially the serious ones; finds himself very funny
Truthful Omission: Will not lie, but redirects, deflects, and 'ask me again later' the things he won't say
Warmth Beneath: Genuinely fond of people; the wisdom is real but the kindness is realer
abilities: Wayfaring: Slips through the gaps of the world. Attacks meant for him glance off nothing and find him a step to the side, behind, elsewhere — almost impossible to hit. Can reposition short distances at will

Wand from Nowhere: Produces a wand from thin air, the right one for the moment — binding, quenching, sleeping, light-making, breaking. He carries many and prefers small, precise effects to large ones. Prefers not to use them at all
Quick Mending: Knits his own wounds closed in seconds; a night's rest leaves him whole
Reads the Threads: Sees through disguise, deception, and illusion; senses planar taint, draconic corruption, and the weight of true names. Often pretends not to notice

Wendell Greenbottle.name: Wendell Greenbottle
Wendell Greenbottle.basicInfo: A trim halfling with sandy hair smoothed flat, a green felt waistcoat with brass buttons, and a leather satchel always slung over one shoulder. Wears a small brass badge of office shaped like a sealed letter, and walks with a brisk purposeful step.
Wendell Greenbottle.hiddenInfo: Halfling postmaster of Hillhome, brother to Tobin at the general store. Runs the post house on Hill Street and the route between Hillhome and the larger settlements down the Vale. Endlessly curious about what's in the letters and parcels he carries; entirely professional about not opening them, except by accident, and accidents happen. The fastest way to get news from Hillhome to anywhere is through Wendell, who treats each piece of mail as a small sacred trust that he is also dying to know about.
personality: Brisk: Always on the move, always halfway to somewhere
Insatiably Curious: Reads return addresses like other people read books
Pridefully Punctual: Late mail wounds him personally
Family-Loyal: A Greenbottle through and through
Helpfully Nosy: Will share what he knows for a good piece of gossip in trade
abilities: Post Route Knowledge: Knows every lane, shortcut, and bridge in the Vale
Eye for Names: Recognizes handwriting, seals, and family marks across the region

Cameron Reid.name: Cameron Reid
Cameron Reid.basicInfo: A veteran Blade of the Tower of Swords assigned to guide an initiate through a missing-hunters investigation at Graves Hunting Preserve. Practical field clothes, travel-worn armor, a checked crossbow, and an axe kept ready at hand.
Cameron Reid.hiddenInfo: Cameron knows this is meant to be the initiate's first real hunt, so he watches more than he commands. He has enough experience to recognize when a monster's behavior is wrong, and he will push the player to observe, reason, and choose the next move rather than solve the case for them.
personality: Steady Mentor: Gives clear advice under pressure without taking the hunt away from the initiate
Grim Humor: Uses dry, understated jokes when things get ugly
Fieldwise: Trusts tracks, signs, habits, and evidence over guesses or bravado
abilities: Hunter's Mark: Reads tracks and signs quickly enough to keep pressure on a fleeing creature
Crossbow Cover: Keeps a loaded shot ready while an ally investigates or advances
Axe Work: Uses short, brutal chops when a creature closes the distance

Of Hogs and Halflings.name: Of Hogs and Halflings
Of Hogs and Halflings.description:
TUTORIAL

In a cozy halfling village in the forest, the prize truffle-hog has vanished two days before the festival — and there's mischief afoot, because without him the family inn won't see the year out. A gentle introduction. Skip ahead if you want action.
Of Hogs and Halflings.firstQuest: The first quest is to pick up Phineas's calling-harness from Manu the blacksmith at Manu's Blacksmith Shop AND the truffle-paste from Pernilla Balewood at the Saucy Chicken Tavern. Quest name: Bells and Balewoods. Set arc to NULL — this is a simple fetch, not the start of an arc.
Of Hogs and Halflings.storyStart:
Set the scene in Hearthhome, a cozy hill-home in the halfling village of Hillhome. Late morning, kettle on the hearth, sunlight through the round windows. Briefly establish who the players and how they came to live in Hillhome. They may be from the village, or new to it, or staying with friends. Do not invent any other characters.

The front door bangs open. Bark Balewood bursts in.

'Phineas is GONE. Disappeared. Vanished. The Newmoon Fair's the day after tomorrow, and now old Goldspoon has announced she's calling in our debt. Coincidence? I think not.' He gulps air. 'Grandma needs you down at the Saucy Chicken — but first she says swing by Manu's and pick up Phineas's calling-harness, the one with the bells. Manu's been mending it. We'll need it to leash him again. Oh, and the truffle-paste, the one he likes, Grandma is just making up a fresh batch. We'll need that to lure him. He'll be out in the truffle-woods somewhere — that pig knows every root and bramble between here and the Wildmarket. Phineas is the best truffle-hog in the Everwood. If we lose him we lose the Fair, and if we lose the Fair we lose the tavern. WHY ARE YOU STILL SITTING THERE?' End the scene there.

Trollfood.name: Trollfood
Trollfood.description:
ACTION

Captured by mountain trolls while traveling the highland passes, you find yourself bound beside their bubbling cauldron. A comedy action start.
Trollfood.storyStart: Set the scene with the party having been captured by trolls and tied up with rope around a campfire in a rocky gorge in the highlands on a cold, stormy night. Show the dwarf warrior Fror Stormhammer tied up along with the party. Mention that the party's weapons are all in a pile by the fire. Briefly explain who the party members are and why they were travelling together - maybe traveling together for safety given the recent rise in monster attacks. Mention that trolls ambushed them on the trail. Show two large, brutish mountain trolls: a male (Barp) and his wife (Tina). Make them start arguing about a large horn. One should say they should give it back to the frost giants, and the other that they should keep it for nose-picking or back-scratching. Barp should blow the horn, making a dull roar that causes a sudden chill. Tina should accuse Barp of farting and try to grab the horn. Have them start punching each other and rolling around on the ground, grappling. Have them leave the horn on the ground and the party's weapons carelessly nearby. Have Fror whisper: 'Frost giants? In the Highlands? We need to get free and warn the clan.'

The League.name: The League
The League.description:
ADVENTURE

You've come to join the League of Adventurers in the great city of Ardan's Watch. As a newly minted Copper Rank adventurer, you're free to pursue whatever contracts strike your fancy as you work your way up the ranks. An open-ended start where you can explore the world at your pace.
The League.storyStart: Set the scene in the main hall of the Tower of Venture in Ardan's Watch. The PC has just signed on as Copper rank; mention that they have just received their Copper pin from the registrar. Make it clear that the Copper board has several postings suited for an adventurer of this rank, but don't describe them. Instead, describe a few fellow Coppers who are inspecting the offers, but don't have them say anything specific about the jobs they are considering. Once you have established the scene, end without further complications or events.

Two of Swords.name: Two of Swords
Two of Swords.firstQuest: The first quest should be a monster-hunting contract: neutralize the creature that's been attacking near the settlement. The arc theme should emerge from why the creature is here — displaced, corrupted, controlled, or part of a larger pattern — not from the creature itself. The arc should escalate from a local monster problem to a regional threat with an intelligent or organized source. Secrets should progress from 'something about this creature doesn't fit' to 'this is part of a bigger pattern' to 'here's where it's coming from and who's behind it.' The arc goal is a physical confrontation at the source — a lair, corrupted site, or faction base. Keep the first quest's scope small and completable: one creature, one settlement, one hunt. The arc does the escalation work.
Two of Swords.description:
ADVENTURE

Your first contract as a Blade of the Tower of Swords. Your mentor's watching. The monster's out there somewhere. Show them both what you've got. An action start where you hunt monsters and work your way up in your guild.
Two of Swords.storyStart: Set the scene arriving at a settlement where the Tower of Swords has sent the player on their first Copper-rank contract. Briefly establish who the player is and that this is their trial run as a newly ranked Blade. Their assigned mentor is Rook Skander, a half-elf veteran Blade who's here to observe and advise but not to lead. Describe the settlement and establish that something has been attacking. The details should fit the region and terrain. Nobody knows exactly what the creature is. Rook gives the player three quick pieces of practical advice for working a contract: talk to the people who saw it or found the damage, examine the kill site or scene of the attack yourself, and don't assume you know what it is until you have evidence. He makes clear that the player calls the shots — he'll answer questions and back them up, but the contract is theirs. Have a couple of locals nearby who clearly have opinions or information — someone who found the dead livestock, someone who heard something at night, the innkeeper who's been fielding complaints. Make them accessible and let the player choose who to approach first. End after establishing these characters.

Hunter/Hunted.name: Hunter/Hunted
Hunter/Hunted.description:
MYSTERY

As an initiate of the Tower of Swords-a warrior society that hunts monsters and fights threats to the realm-you've been sent to investigate a hunting party that vanished in a lord's private preserve. A mystery adventure start.
Hunter/Hunted.storyStart: Set the scene at the edge of the Graves Hunting Preserve, late morning. The player character is an initiate of the Tower of Swords, accompanying veteran Blade Cameron Reid on their first real hunt. Briefly explain that the Tower of Swords trains and dispatches Blades—monster hunters who answer when something dangerous emerges in Larion. Briefly explain what drew the player to the Swords—a desire to protect, a talent for violence put to good use, or perhaps something took someone they loved and they want to make sure it doesn't happen again. A nervous servant named Colm leads them to where the hunting party was last seen two days ago. Colm explains: Lord Tristram Graves' son Sebastian rode out with four companions for a morning hunt. Colm was with the beaters. They heard the hunters calling out—excited, like they'd spotted game—then nothing. Sebastian's horse returned riderless. The household guard searched but found no bodies, no blood, no trail. Cameron moves into the brush and crouches. When he returns, his expression is grim. 'Found their tracks.' He looks deeper into the trees. 'They didn't run. They followed something. All of them, one at a time.' He checks his crossbow, then his axe. 'Something out there calls and men come to it.' He turns to the player. 'This is your hunt too. How do you want to work this?'

Field Trip.name: Field Trip
Field Trip.description:
ACTION

You're a first-year magic student on your first field assignment, sent to investigate reports of a magical disturbance and a rogue mage near a sleepy market village. A comedy adventure start.
Field Trip.firstQuest:
## First Quest

- Put an end to the imps' chaos in the Market Square
- Quest name: Pest Control

Important: Put the first quest in `The Market Square` (existingArea) of location `Briarton`.

Arc theme: Thera Pel, a former Academy student, expelled and living rough in the Briars, has been summoning creatures and brewing love and anti-love potions. One of her summons — a lovesick Imp Lord — has slipped her control, allied with the innkeeper's daughter, and is escalating his own ambitions: to summon something even bigger than himself. Expected conclusion: The party puts a definitive end to the Imp Lord's chaos in Briarton.

Secrets:

1. A binding sigil is visible on the back of one imp's neck. These are summoned creatures, not native.
2. A witch who goes by the name of Thera has been charging villagers for healing and minor charms in the woods east of Briarton.
3. Empty potion vials litter clearings in the Briars — dozens of them, all different.
4. Half-finished binding circles in a clearing, the chalk fresh, the work abandoned mid-ritual.
5. Someone has been buying — or stealing — herbs from every village within a day's ride: damiana, valerian, moonflower, nightroot. Love potion ingredients, and ingredients for their antidotes.
6. A creature about three feet tall, horned and small-winged, in ridiculously fancy clothing, has been seen on rooftops at dusk.
7. Samantheria, the innkeeper's daughter, has been sneaking out at odd hours. People in Briarton who've crossed her have been having unusually bad weeks — having fantastically embarrassing mishaps occur to them in public, as if they were cursed.
8. The magical disturbances are getting worse, and more imps keep appearing.
9. The imps have set up a base of operations in a cave near the village of Briarton.
10. A massive summoning circle is being constructed in the woods near Briarton and is almost ready.
Field Trip.storyStart:
Introduce the player characters as first-year students of the Academy of the Arcane, a prestigious magic school. They are on their first field assignment.

They are accompanied by their mentor, Temyn Raskwood, a senior student.

Set the scene arriving at Briarton on market day. Temyn stops the group at the edge of the square and reminds them of the brief: the village reported a magical disturbance and sightings of a rogue mage in the area. Their assignment is to investigate both. Temyn glances around as he speaks, joking that it's probably nothing.

A small winged creature — leathery, about the size of a cat, with a face like a cruel little old man — drops onto Temyn's shoulder from behind, sinks its teeth into his ear, and shrieks an alliterative, vulgar insult about Temyn and the party. Temyn yelps. The imp launches off and darts around Temyn, mocking him.

A second imp appears in the sheep pen, capers along the top rail, and undoes the gate latch with one clawed finger. The sheep flood out into the square, bleating, trampling baskets and overturning a fruit cart. The imp leaps onto the inn's signpost above the chaos, throws its arms wide, and declares in a carrying voice that Briarton is the DOMAIN OF THE IMP LORD, that wizards are not welcome here, and that the young wizard and his little friends should turn around and go home before something worse happens.

The market erupts. Stalls topple, merchants grab their goods, traders shout. Temyn urges the party to stop standing around and do something about the imps.

First Blood.name: First Blood
First Blood.description:
MYSTERY

As an initiate of the Tower of Shadow, a clandestine order that protects Larion from supernatural threats, you've been sent under cover on your first mission to hunt a fledgling vampire in a small village in the countryside. A dark mystery start.
First Blood.storyStart: Set the scene on the road approaching Millbrook at dusk. The player character is an initiate of the Tower of Shadow, accompanied by their mentor Tarain Rethford, a blood mage and operative. Briefly explain that the Tower of Shadow is an order of clandestine operatives who protects Larion by infiltrating and defeating cults, secret societies and criminal organizations. This is the player's trial mission—Tarain advises, but the initiate leads. Briefly explain the player's background and why they joined the Tower of Shadow - perhaps a gift for convincing others, a brush with secret evil, or a will to get their hands dirty for the common good. Describe Millbrook's gates ahead, torchlight flickering, voices raised in anger. As they approach, they find a crowd gathered in the Village Common. Armed villagers hold a young man while others shout accusations—a woman lies dead nearby, her throat torn. The accused is a Tarth, and the dead woman a Millner; the two families have obvious bad blood. The crowd wants blood itself. Tarain studies the scene, then speaks quietly: 'Fledgling vampire. Sloppy kill, no attempt to hide it. Turned recently, still figuring out what it is.' She pricks her palm with a silver dagger and lets a drop of her blood fall to the earth, then closes her eyes. 'The boy's heart is racing. Scared out of his wits. But beating.' She glances at the lynch mob. 'He's not our vampire. But we tell them they've got a vampire on the loose, we may scare off our quarry - or start a riot. But this is your hunt, initiate. Your call how we handle this.'

Overboard.name: Overboard
Overboard.description:
COMBAT

Pirates attack the merchant galley you're traveling on. For better or worse, your new friend knows these particular pirates very well.... An action adventure start.
Overboard.storyStart: Set the scene aboard a Spice Isles merchant galley in the North Canal, bound for Tevantia. Sun on the deck, salt spray, colorful sails snapping in the wind. Briefly introduce the party members and why they're traveling—trade, adventure, escaping trouble, chasing opportunity. Make the introductions light and funny where appropriate. Introduce Coral, a striking woman with sea-green tinged skin and faintly webbed fingers who's been friendly company on the voyage. The lookout screams. A sleek raider emerges from behind a rocky isle, flying the black flag of the Feathered Fleet. The crew panics—everyone's heard of the seabird pirates. But Coral goes pale for a different reason. 'That's my ship. The Windreaver. I was captain until my first mate Scraw led a mutiny.' She watches the pelican and albatross pirates swinging grappling hooks, preparing to board. 'Scraw's vicious but stupid. He'll have kept my lockbox in the captain's cabin—never could pick the lock. There's something in there worth more than this whole cargo.' She draws a long knife. 'So. Do we just survive this, or do we take my ship back?'.

Return of the Dragon Queen.name: Return of the Dragon Queen
Return of the Dragon Queen.description:
EPIC

On the road with two strangers met by chance, you come upon a country inn under attack. What begins as a roadside skirmish becomes the start of a campaign to save Larion. A combat start with a hook into a world-spanning campaign.
Return of the Dragon Queen.storyStart:
Begin the response with the following crawl passage rendered VERBATIM, with no additions or modifications. After the crawl, leave a paragraph break, then begin the scene fresh in third person present tense. Do NOT prefix the scene with 'Long ago, in the world of Larion' — the crawl serves that role.

OPENING CRAWL (render verbatim):

NARRATOR: Long ago, the great battles of Larion's Age of Heroes ended in peace.

In time, the legendary deeds of those heroes faded into bedtime tales for children.

But now troubles worthy of those days sweep the land once more.

In the north, the warring goblin tribes have united under one banner: the Black Dragon. And their warcry is a name from such tales: Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.

While Larion's princes bicker and squabble, the League of Adventurers has issued a Convocation, calling every able sword and staff to Ardan's Watch.

After the crawl, leave a paragraph break, then begin the scene with a transition line: 'Meanwhile, on a forest road in Highvale, a day's walk from Ardan's Watch...' Continue in third person present tense.

Set the scene on a forest trail, late afternoon. Briefly establish who the player character is and that they are traveling with two others for safety: Vera Stone, a sharp-eyed woman in her late twenties who carries a short sword and a crossbow, and Volnom, a very small, very old gnome in a faded red cloak shuffling along on a walking staff taller than he is.

As they walk, Volnom and Vera exchange remarks: 'Vera: How much further, Volnom?

Volnom: Closer than last time you asked.

Vera: My feet are killing me.

Volnom: Too young for a walking stick, she says.

Vera: Branches look good on trees.

Vo: Complaining looks good on no one.' Then the smell of smoke reaches them on the wind.

They hurry through the trees and stop at the edge of the clearing. The Crossroads Inn stands ahead: a long timber building, its stables burning black. Three robed figures stand in the yard — two with short blades with magic flame dancing on their edges, the leader with a heavy staff, green fire rippling around its head. He shouts towards the inn: 'The messenger! Send him out, now! You will not escape. We burned your horses. Send him out, or we burn you all with him!' No answer from the inn. Vera silently readies her crossbow. In a low voice: 'Pyre cultists. Standing bounty, hundred florins a head at the Tower. I'll take the one with the big mouth and the love for animals.'

Volnom looks up at the sky, traces a small sign in the air and sighs. 'Lady of Mercy, forgive me. I fear I must interpret my vow to you... creatively.' He turns to the player. 'Are you coming?'

Seven Dead Must Die.name: Seven Dead Must Die
Seven Dead Must Die.description:
EPIC

Seven ghosts of a terrible past age have risen to haunt Larion. The Tower of Shadow holds the vigil against their kind — in secret, because the workaday world can't bear the truth. Step into Shadow, and join them in ensuring that the Seven die again — and stay dead this time. A monster-hunting campaign focusing on undead and supernatural threats.
Seven Dead Must Die.storyStart:
IMPORTANT: FOLLOW ALL THESE INSTRUCTIONS FAITHFULLY. DO NOT OMIT ANY OF THE NUMBERED BEATS; WEAVE THEM ALL INTO THE NARRATION.

1. Set the scene at the edge of the Pit of Shadow, the great bottomless shaft at the heart of the Tower of Shadow in Ardan's Watch. Do not mention smell, sound, vibration or temperature; instead, paint a picture of the physical space and the people present.

2. Clearly state who the player is, and why they are here: they are an initiate of the Shadows — an order that hunts supernatural threats usch as hauntings and the restless dead.

3. The player stands among other members of the order: Iliora the Veiled, the Master of Shadow, who leads the order, and the player character's mentor, Vesna Calloway.

4. The Master tells the Shadows why they have been called. Something has broken loose.

5. She turns to the Pit and calls up an image of the quarry. Seven figures of shadow rise from the dark, one by one, and she names them as they come: Vortigar the Unburied. Sethelle of the Nine Sorrows. Karneth Draghmar. The Lady Mavrocine. Ossarine the Pale Crown. Belugar Thoth-Amon. Druathyr the Thrice-Drowned. Seven of the mighty dead — lords and sorcerers who served the demon-princes of Ur in the age of the Fourth Empire. Long ago they were stopped, and put down, and bound to the Shadelands, the land of restless dead. Now they have come back.

6. The Master explains what this means: the Seven must be hunted down and ended, and the dead they leave rising in their wake must be put back to rest.

7. As the Shadows disperse, Vesna claps the player on the shoulder and says that hunting the Seven will have to wait, because they're not ready; for the time being, Jett has asked them to help with smaller related threats in and around the city, particularly a ghoul nest in Muckside, but they can go see him in the Hall of Shadow for other work.

End the scene here.

City of Fortune.name: City of Fortune
City of Fortune.description:
SANDBOX

Gavarra: City of Fortune. You came here to seek yours, among the great gambling houses and glittering terraces of the famed Stormglow Isle. So far all you've learned is that money flows uphill, and you're at the bottom. When you fell into debt with the wrong people, they locked your friend up until you can pay their ransom. On an island where fortunes are made and lost every night, surely you can find a way? After all, it's either that or you both get a closer look at how the island's famous corpse-coral gets its bright colors.... An open-ended start where you have to make money any way you can.
City of Fortune.firstQuest: Settle your debt of 1,000 florins to Luthaire, master of the Gilded Cage.
City of Fortune.storyStart:
Open at night in the luxurious penthouse of the Gilded Cage gambling house, on a balcony overlooking the island's towers on one side and the sea on the other. Luthaire — a moon elf dhampir, beautifully dressed — politely explains that their debt is now due. Since they are momentarily without funds, he will take... collateral: their companion. Establish who the companion is based on the player's background. The companion is led away by House guards, begging the player to find the money. Luthaire reminds the player: one thousand florins, at their earliest convenience. Until then, the companion will earn their keep... in the Cage. Then he bids the player farewell, saying his staff will show them out. His enforcer — an enormous minotaur who has not spoken once — picks the player up and throws them off the balcony into the harbor below.

Cold water, sinking, surface. A small boat hooks the player by the collar and drags them to the Float. It's piloted by Sierra — a wiry, sunburned woman hands them a hot tonic. Cheerfully, she says when Luthaire doesn't want someone to survive, he throws them out the other side, into the rift they call Hell. So — congratulations, and that'll be two florins for the tonic. End here.

The Academy of the Arcane.name: The Academy of the Arcane
The Academy of the Arcane.firstQuest: Report to the Great Hall to receive your first Practicum assignment and meet your partner.
The Academy of the Arcane.description:
SANDBOX

Today you join the ranks of the realm's elite mages-in-training, soon ot receive your first real assignment. You start out in an orientation at your new school. A social sandbox start.
The Academy of the Arcane.storyStart:
Set the scene in a small dormitory room in one of the Academy's towers during late afternoon. Two beds, two desks, one empty, the other where the player is unpacking. Their roommate enters—Sam Vellory if the player is male, Samantha Vellory if female. Freckled, sand-haired, wearing clearly hand-me-down mage robes, looking like they've just run up several flights of stairs. They're holding two small silver pins.

Without much preamble they hand one to the player—'First Circle pin, you'll need it for tomorrow, it marks you as eligible for assignment.' They pin their own to their collar with shaking fingers. 'Practica assignments go up after breakfast. First Circles get partnered with upper Circle students—someone to show us the work, report back on how we do. I've heard Third Circle Ayla Thane is mentoring this term, she's supposed to be brilliant, but also that Second Circle Teldric Ren drew the short straw and he's—' They stop. Take a breath. 'Sorry. I'm nervous. My father made Sentinel in his first year. My uncle didn't get placed until his third. It all depends on how your first Practica go, who sees what in you.'

Have Sam produce something from their satchel—a small jar of salve, clearly homemade, offering it to the player. 'Burn salve. My mother made it. She said with everything happening lately—fire cults, the troubles in the Fells—better to have it.' A pause. 'I don't even know what kind of Practicum we'll draw. Investigation, containment, recovery—they post what's available and partners choose from what's left by seniority, so First Circles get whatever nobody else wanted.' They try to smile. 'Should be fine. Probably.'

The welcome dinner for new students starts soon in the Great Hall. Tomorrow everything begins. Does the player want to walk down together?

Homecoming.name: Homecoming
Homecoming.description:
SANDBOX

After months away training and adventuring, you return to find your village threatened and your family in need. An sandbox action adventure start.
Homecoming.storyStart: Set the scene on the road approaching Greenmeadow on a late afternoon in early autumn. Describe golden wheat fields stretching across gentle hills, the ancient oak tree visible in the distance, smoke rising from cottage chimneys. Briefly explain who each party member is, their connection to Greenmeadow (grew up here, family still lives here, promised to return, etc.), and what drove them to leave - a desire to see the world, to train as warriors/mages/etc., to escape a peaceful life that felt too small. As they reach the Green, they encounter a group of villagers gathered near the well, talking in worried voices. The village elder pushes through the crowd. 'Welcome home,' she says, and there are tears in her eyes. 'And just in time! Something in the Wolfwood... the attacks started two weeks ago. Lord Corwin's rangers can't be everywhere at once, and we...' She gestures helplessly at the worried villagers. 'We're farmers, not warriors. Whatever's out there, it's getting bolder.'

The Inheritance.name: The Inheritance
The Inheritance.description:
SANDBOX

A distant relative's death has left you the unexpected heir to a run-down noble estate. An sandbox social start where you can explore and rebuild your new home.
The Inheritance.storyStart: Set the scene at the entrance to Riverside Manor on a gray morning. The drive is overgrown, the fountain dry and cracked, weeds pushing through the gravel. The manor house rises ahead—roof tiles missing, windows cracked, but the bones clearly solid. If there is a single player character, they have inherited this estate from a distant relative they barely knew. If there are multiple party members, establish who inherited and why the others have come along. As they take in the scene, an elderly man emerges from the manor. This is the steward—he has kept the estate from total ruin these past five years, though the strain shows. He explains that since the previous master's untimely demise five years  prior, funds have run dry, staff left, the Willowford tenants have struggled without leadership. He mentions that Lady Ashbury at the neighboring estate has made repeated offers to purchase. He soffers to show them inside.'

Of Ales and Ambitions.name: Of Ales and Ambitions
Of Ales and Ambitions.description:
SANDBOX

You've come into ownership of a tavern. The rest is up to you. An open-ended social start.
Of Ales and Ambitions.storyStart:
Set the scene with the player arriving at a tavern they have just come into ownership of. State who the player character is and how they came into ownership of the tavern: through inheritance, a debt repaid, a wager won, a reward, or whatever fits the character's backstory and the location. Do not mention ledgers.

Describe the tavern — its condition, its character, its feel. Avoid smells and temperature. Say what it looks like, its shape, layout, materials, condition, who is present.

The tavern is alive when the player walks in. Pick one: a table of regulars deep in a loud argument about something local, an insult contest, arm-wrestle, drinking game or other off-color competition getting comically heated in the corner, a bard or performer mid-set with heckler in the audience, a slightly unsavory but interesting group celebrating something (birthday, job done, bet won), or two people flirting badly and awkwardly while their friends watch. Vary the species of patrons present — elves, dwarves, halflings, orcs, beastfolk, not just humans. Be colorful and bold in your choices.

Introduce the person who kept the place running under the previous owner and is still here — young, sharp, competent, and sizing the player up. Not hostile, but not a pushover. They should acknowledge the player as the new owner in a way that shows they have opinions about how this place should be run and they're deciding whether the new owner is worth their loyalty. Never make them middle-aged or elderly.

The tone is warm. There is no crisis, no urgent threat, no trouble at the door. This is a good day — the start of something, but there is work to be done, and much to be decided. Do not generate any conflict, danger, or pressing problems. The story is: you have a tavern now. What do you do with it?

Write a little more than the above instructions indicate, so as to create more ambience and interest in the tavern itself and its patrons, which are the subject of the story — they need to be established. Also, do remember to explicitly establish who the player is and how they came to be the new owner.

A Night at the Tavern.name: A Night at the Tavern
A Night at the Tavern.description:
SANDBOX

You've come to the city seeking fortune as an adventurer. Word is that taverns are where such opportunities can be found. An open-ended start that lets you choose the direction your story takes.
A Night at the Tavern.storyStart: Set the scene at a tavern where the PC has come looking for adventure work. Briefly establish who the PC is and how they ended up here. The tavern should feel alive — something is already happening when the PC walks in. Opportunities for work and adventure emerge naturally from the people and what is going on — not announced, but overheard, witnessed, or stumbled into. Other people here are also looking for work or partners. The tone is fun and inviting — this is the start of something good.

For Gold and Glory.name: For Gold and Glory
For Gold and Glory.description:
ACTION

You've signed up to fight in the arena. The crowd's waiting. Time to make a name for yourself. A gladiator action adventure start.
For Gold and Glory.storyStart: Set the scene with the player arriving at an arena to sign up as a new fighter. Briefly establish who the player is and what brought them here — ambition, money, a dare, a debt, a love of fighting, or whatever fits the character. This is their first time competing. Describe the arena and give it atmosphere — the crowd, the noise, the scale of the place. The tone is exciting, not grim. This is sport and spectacle, not punishment. There should be people around who make the world feel full: a trainer or fight manager who runs a stable of fighters and is sizing the player up as a prospect, other fighters preparing for their own bouts — some friendly, some not — and someone official handling registrations. Introduce the idea that fighters here can have patrons, sponsors, or belong to a ludus or stable, but don't force the player into one. Their first bout is coming up soon. Let the player take in the scene, talk to people, and prepare however they want. The mood is anticipation — something good is about to happen and the player is at the center of it.

The Invitation.name: The Invitation
The Invitation.description:
SOCIAL

An invitation to a lord's banquet has fallen into your hands. The night is young. Who is to say how it will end? An open-ended social start.
The Invitation.storyStart: Set the scene with the player arriving at a grand social event — a lord's banquet, a noble reception, a diplomatic gathering. Briefly establish who the player is and why they're here: perhaps a minor noble, perhaps someone who earned an invitation through service or cunning, perhaps someone who wasn't invited at all. The event should feel lavish, lively, and appealing — good food, beautiful setting, interesting people. Introduce one NPC who approaches the player with charm and an obvious agenda — they want something, and they think the player can provide it. Make this person fun and appealing, not sinister. The stakes are social: reputation, alliance, opportunity. No combat, no conspiracy, no mysterious threats, no traditional adventure hooks. Just ambitious people in a beautiful room, each playing their own game.

Twin Spirits.name: Twin Spirits
Twin Spirits.description:
SOCIAL

Your magical animal companion has been accused of hurting someone. It's up to you to work together to get out of the situation. A social start with a magical companion.
Twin Spirits.storyStart: Each player in the party starts with one magical or animal companion each, based on their background, race, and class. Briefly introduce each player and their companion and have them banter about their history and relationship (you can communicate with your companion telepathically). Describe the scene of you discovering that someone from town has been telling people that your companion attacked a person in town, and they are trying to start a riot.

(Custom) Backstory Focus.name: (Custom) Backstory Focus
(Custom) Backstory Focus.description:
CUSTOM

Fortune favors the bold. Begin your tale with a custom start based off your backstory.
(Custom) Backstory Focus.storyStart: Write a starting situation based on the character's backstory and current life situation. First set the scene by describing the current location clearly, remembering that the players may have never seen it before. Just say clearly what the location is, don't be too clever, and then give it a clear establishing description. Briefly explain who the party members are, how they came to be in the starting location, and what they are doing there. Tie the explaination into the character's backstory to the best of your ability. Use as much of the backstory as you can, and make the story start as close to the ending point of the backstory as possible, but you are free to improvise if you need to in order to give the party members a good reason for being where they are.
locationAreas: The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads

Highvale.name: Highvale
Highvale.basicInfo: Highvale is green country — rolling hills, golden fields, and the river Silverflow winding south to the sea. No king rules here; noble houses hold the land in a patchwork of lordships and free towns, and knights and mercenaris keep the roads. The League of Adventurers makes its headquarters at the region's largest city, Ardan's Watch, drawing would-be heroes from every corner of the Worlds. Work is never hard to find. The ground itself isn't safe: below Highvale lies Grell Hollows, a reach of the Underworld, and goblins, kobolds, and worse come up out of it to raid — through sinkholes, old mines, and other links to the world below. And not every sword for hire is an honest one. Highvale is rich and lightly ruled, and the line between a mercenary company and a bandit band can come down to which way the season's work went.

The Spice Isles.name: The Spice Isles
The Spice Isles.basicInfo: The Spice Isles are an archipelago of feuding merchant republics and pirate havens connected by colossal stone bridges and threaded with canals, where fortunes are built on spice, silk, and ventures less legal. The Free States bow to no throne. Instead they follow the Swordlaw—disputes settled steel to steel. An ancient treaty known as the Compact forbids open conflict between city-states, so merchant clans wage shadow war through privateers, spies, and mercenaries. Noble houses control the banks, guilds rule the trade routes, and seafolk keep to their kingdoms beneath the waves. The fallen Gold Empire left behind crumbling marble palaces, the great canal and bridge network - and legends of its island capital vanished beneath the sea long ago.

The Everwood.name: The Everwood
The Everwood.basicInfo: The Everwood is a primeval forest older than human memory, ruled by the high elves from their forest cities. Beneath the roots, dark elves hold their own dominion — banished underground after the Kindred War that split elvenkind apart. Non-elf Outsiders are permitted in the border settlements by elven sufferance: Sanctuary, the great trading city, and the villages of the Valley. Dark elves are forbidden from both, on pain of death. Beyond lies wilderness where the old laws hold and trespassers answer to the Wardens. Fey spirits and sylvan creatures stranger still haunt the trackless vastness.

The Highlands.name: The Highlands
The Highlands.basicInfo: The Highlands are a realm of stone and snow where dwarves and giants have contested every peak and valley since the Age of Heroes.
